Intermediate Code Generation

Three-Address Code (TAC)
generate x = y op z instructions
Quadruples
(op, arg1, arg2, result)
Triples & Indirect Triples
index-based representation
Static Single Assignment (SSA)
each variable assigned once
Control Flow Translation
if-else, while, for → TAC
Array Access Translation
a[i] → base + i * size
Boolean Expressions

variable/function declarations → TAC

Address Calculation
array offsets, struct member addresses

calling sequences, stack frames, parameter passing

attribute grammars, S & L-attributed

compare allocation strategies
Medium-Level IL (MIL)
processor-independent intermediate representation

Backpatching

Code Optimization

Control Flow Graph
visualize basic blocks & control flow
Constant Folding
compute constant expressions at compile time
Constant Propagation
replace variables with constant values
Copy Propagation
replace one variable with another (x = y → use y instead of x)
Dead Code Elimination (DCE)
remove computations whose results are never used
Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)
detect & eliminate redundant computations
Loop Invariant Code Motion
move invariant computations outside loops
Strength Reduction
replace * with + and << (x*2 → x+x)
Induction Variable Elimination
replace expensive induction variables (i*k + c)
Loop Unrolling
reduce loop overhead by replicating body
Function Inlining
replace function calls with function body
Global Data Flow Analysis
reaching definitions, live variables, available expressions
Peephole Optimization
local instruction pattern matching & replacement
Redundant Load/Store Elimination
remove unnecessary memory accesses
Algebraic Simplification
x+0 → x, x*1 → x, x-x → 0, etc.
Interprocedural Optimization
inline expansion, constant propagation across calls
Tail Call Optimization
replace recursive tail calls with jumps
Software Pipelining
overlap instructions from different loop iterations
Instruction Scheduling
reorder instructions to avoid pipeline stalls
Loop Fusion
combine adjacent loops with same iteration space
Loop Interchange
swap nested loops to improve cache locality
Loop Unswitching
move invariant conditionals outside loops
Sparse Conditional Constant Prop
aggressive propagation with condition evaluation
Global Value Numbering
hash-based detection of redundant expressions
Partial Redundancy Elimination (PRE)
eliminate expressions that are partially redundant
Bitwise Operations
simplify shifts, masks, and combine operations

Operator Overloading

Control Flow Type Checking

Tokenization · scanning made interactive

tokens found: 0

Token stream

# Lexeme Token type Attribute
enter source and click Tokenize

GATE insight – tokenization

Lexical analysis breaks source into tokens: keywords, identifiers, operators, literals, separators. Scanner discards whitespace/comments, produces (token-name, attribute-value) pairs. Common tokens: <id, "x"> , <num, 10> , <op, '+'> , <keyword, 'if'> .

Lexeme Extraction · isolating raw substrings

Extracted Lexemes

float result = (a + b) * 3.14;
# Lexeme Start Position End Position
enter source and click Extract Lexemes

What is Lexeme Extraction?

Lexeme extraction is the process of isolating raw substrings from source code that form meaningful tokens. The scanner reads characters sequentially and groups them into lexemes based on pattern matching rules. Each lexeme becomes a token after classification.

"int count = 42;" → Lexemes: ["int", "count", "=", "42", ";"]

Pattern Matching · regular expressions for tokens

Regular Expression Patterns

Keywords

\b(int|float|if|else|return|while|for)\b

Identifiers

[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*

Numbers

\b\d+(\.\d+)?\b

Operators

[+\-*/%=<>!&|^~]+

Separators

[(){}[\];,.]

Strings

"[^"]*"

Pattern Matching Analysis

int x = 42 + 15;
# Lexeme Pattern Type Regex Used Match Position
enter code and click Analyze

Pattern Matching in Lexical Analysis

Pattern matching uses regular expressions to define valid token patterns. The scanner reads input and matches sequences against these patterns to identify tokens. This is the foundation of lexer generators like Lex/Flex.

Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA) Regular Expressions Token Recognition

Symbol Table Management · identifier tracking

Symbol Table Entries

Total Identifiers: 0 Global: 0 Local: 0 Re-declarations: 0
int main() { int age = 25; float salary = 45000.50; }
# Identifier Data Type Scope Line No. Value Memory Location Attributes
enter code and click Build Symbol Table

Scope Hierarchy

🌐 Global scope

Symbol Table Operations

Insert

Add new identifier with type, scope, and attributes

Lookup

Find identifier in current scope

Update

Modify attributes (value, type, etc.)

Delete

Remove identifier from scope

Symbol Table in Compiler Design

The symbol table is a critical data structure used by compilers to store information about identifiers (variables, functions, classes) appearing in the source code. It enables:

  • Scope Management: Tracks global and local variables with proper nesting
  • Type Checking: Verifies type compatibility in expressions
  • Memory Allocation: Assigns memory locations to variables
  • Semantic Analysis: Detects redeclarations and undeclared identifiers
Symbol Table Entry → (name, type, scope, line, value, memory_location, attributes)

Whitespace & Comments Removal · filtering non-significant characters

Filtering Results

Original Length: 0 Filtered Length: 0 Reduction: 0% Comments Removed: 0 Whitespace Removed: 0

Original Code:

// This is a comment\nint main() {\n /* Block comment\n spanning multiple lines */\n int x = 10; // Initialize x\n int y = 20; // Initialize y\n \n // Calculate sum\n int sum = x + y;\n \n return sum;\n}

After Removal (Clean Code):

intmain(){intx=10;inty=20;intsum=x+y;returnsum;}

All comments and whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) have been removed.

Removal Analysis:

# Removed Item Type Position Length
enter code and click Remove

Visual Representation:

🔴 Red = Removed | 🟢 Green = Kept

Why Remove Whitespace and Comments?

In lexical analysis, whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) and comments are filtered out because they don't contribute to the program's semantics. This process:

  • Reduces input size for subsequent compiler phases
  • Simplifies token recognition by eliminating noise
  • Improves performance of parsing and analysis
  • Maintains code meaning while removing non-essential characters
Single-line comments (//) Multi-line comments (/* */) Spaces & tabs Newlines

Lookahead Analysis · peeking ahead to resolve token ambiguity

Lookahead Visualization

int x = 10;

Current Character

' '

Next Character (1 char)

' '

Lookahead (2 chars)

' '

Lookahead (3 chars)

' '

Lexical Decision

Ready to analyze...

Lookahead Scenarios Detected

# Pattern Context Lookahead Used Resolution
enter code and click Analyze Lookahead

Common Lookahead Patterns

1. Maximal Munch Rule

>=, <=, ==, !=, &&, ||, ++, --, +=, -=, *=, /=

Always take the longest possible token. Lookahead determines if next char forms a compound operator.

2. Number vs. Dot Operator

3.14 vs obj.method()

When seeing a digit followed by '.' - lookahead to next char: if digit, it's a float; if letter, it might be a member access.

3. C++ Style Resolution

>> vs >> in templates

Lookahead needed to distinguish shift operator from closing nested templates.

What is Lookahead in Lexical Analysis?

Lookahead refers to reading one or more characters ahead of the current position to determine the correct token. This is essential for:

  • Maximal Munch: Always take the longest possible token (e.g., >= vs > and =)
  • Ambiguity Resolution: Distinguish between similar patterns (e.g., x+++y could be x++ + y or x + ++y)
  • Context-Sensitive Tokens: Determine if '.' is part of a float or member access
  • Multi-character Operators: Recognize compound operators like <<, >>, &&, ||
while (peek() == expected) { advance(); } /* lookahead loop */

Error Reporting · detecting invalid tokens & malformed syntax

Total Errors: 0 Invalid Characters: 0 Unterminated Strings: 0 Unterminated Comments: 0 Malformed Identifiers: 0 Malformed Numbers: 0

Source Code with Error Highlighting

🔴 Invalid/Malformed 🟠 Warning/Suspicious 🟢 Valid

Detailed Error Report

# Error Type Error Message Location (Line, Col) Offending Token Suggestion
enter code and click Analyze Errors

Lexical Error Categories

1. Invalid Characters

Characters not in the language's alphabet: @, $, #, ^, & (when not operator), etc.

Example: int @count = 10;

2. Unterminated Strings

String literals missing closing quotes.

Example: char* name = "John;

3. Unterminated Comments

Multi-line comments without closing */.

Example: /* comment never closed

4. Malformed Identifiers

Identifiers starting with digits or containing invalid characters.

Example: int 123variable = 5;

5. Malformed Numbers

Numbers with letters, multiple decimal points, or invalid format.

Example: int x = 123abc;

6. Invalid Operators

Operator sequences not recognized by the language.

Example: int x = 5 ** 2;

GATE Insight: Error Recovery Strategies

Panic Mode

Skip characters until a synchronizing token (like ';') is found.

Insertion

Insert missing character (e.g., closing quote) and continue.

Deletion

Delete offending character and resume scanning.

Replacement

Replace invalid character with valid one.

Thompson's Construction · Regular Expression → NFA

Thompson's Construction ε-NFA Start/End States GATE 2024

NFA Diagram

Thompson's Construction Steps

Step Operation RE Fragment NFA Constructed
enter RE and click Convert

NFA Transition Table

State a b ε
Generate to see transition table

Start state: q0 | Final state: —

GATE Level: Thompson's Construction Rules

📌 Basic Symbol: 'a'
q0 a q1

Single character transition

📌 Concatenation: AB
NFA(A) — NFA(B)

Connect final state of A to start of B with ε

📌 Union: A|B
⬤ → ε → NFA(A) → ε → ⬤

New start and final with ε transitions

📌 Kleene Star: A*
⬤ ⟲ ε

ε transitions for zero or more repetitions

Input Buffering · Sentinels & Buffer Pairs for Efficient Scanning

Sentinel Method Buffer Pairs Double Buffering GATE 2024 Focus

Interactive Buffer Visualization

16 bytes 64 bytes 128 bytes
Slow 5x Fast

Buffer 1

Forward Ptr: 0 Lexeme Begin: 0

Buffer 2

Forward Ptr: 0 Lexeme Begin: 0
File Position: 0 / 0
0%
0
Buffer Hits
0
Buffer Misses
0
Buffer Reloads
0%
Efficiency

Sentinel Method (EOF Marker)

❌ Without Sentinel

while (1) {
    ch = getchar();
    if (ch == EOF) break;  // Check each time
    process(ch);
}

⚠️ Condition checked on every character

Buffer reads: 100
Comparisons: 100
Overhead: 100%

✅ With Sentinel (EOF)

buffer[limit] = EOF;  // Place sentinel
while (1) {
    ch = *forward++;
    if (ch == EOF) break;  // Only at buffer end
    process(ch);
}

✓ Check only at buffer boundaries

Buffer reads: 100
Comparisons: 10
Improvement: 90%

Buffer Pairs (Double Buffering)

Memory Layout

Buffer 1 [0..N-1]
Buffer 2 [0..N-1]
forward pointer
lexemeBegin pointer

How Buffer Pairs Work

  1. Load Buffer 1 with first chunk of input
  2. Forward pointer scans characters
  3. LexemeBegin marks start of current lexeme
  4. When forward reaches end, start loading Buffer 2
  5. Scanner continues from Buffer 2 while Buffer 1 reloads
  6. Double buffering enables continuous scanning
if (forward == &buffer[N-1]) {
    reload_current_buffer();
    forward = &buffer[0];
}

Buffer Management Performance

No Buffering
100 ops
Single Buffer
50 ops
Buffer Pairs
25 ops
+ Sentinel
15 ops

GATE Insight: Input Buffering Techniques

📌 Why Buffering?

I/O operations are expensive (1000x slower than memory). Buffering reduces system calls by reading large chunks at once.

read() vs memory access
📌 Sentinel Optimization

Place EOF marker at buffer end to eliminate end-of-buffer check for each character. Reduces comparisons from O(n) to O(1).

if (*forward++ == EOF) break;
📌 Buffer Pairs Advantage

Double buffering allows overlap of I/O and processing. While scanning one buffer, the other is being refilled.

Pipeline: Process Buffer 1 while loading Buffer 2
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions

GATE 2022: In a lexical analyzer, a buffer of size 1024 characters is used with the sentinel technique. If the input file has 10,000 characters, how many end-of-buffer checks are performed?

Show Answer

Answer: 10 checks (10000/1024 ≈ 10 buffer loads). Without sentinel: 10,000 checks. With sentinel: only at buffer boundaries.

GATE 2020: Which of the following is NOT an advantage of using buffer pairs in lexical analysis?

A) Overlap I/O with processing
B) Reduce number of system calls
C) Eliminate need for sentinel
D) Enable lookahead beyond buffer boundary

Show Answer

Answer: C - Buffer pairs do not eliminate the need for sentinel; sentinel is still used within each buffer for efficiency.

Implementation in C

Basic Buffer with Sentinel

#define BUFSIZE 1024
char buffer[BUFSIZE + 1];  // +1 for sentinel

int lex() {
    char *forward = buffer;
    char *lexemeBegin = buffer;
    
    while (1) {
        char c = *forward++;
        if (c == EOF) {  // sentinel
            if (forward == &buffer[BUFSIZE + 1])
                reload_buffer();
            else
                break;
        }
        // process character
        if (need_to_return_token()) {
            lexemeBegin = forward;
        }
    }
}

Buffer Pairs Implementation

#define BUFSIZE 1024
char buffer1[BUFSIZE + 1];
char buffer2[BUFSIZE + 1];
char *current = buffer1;

int next_char() {
    char c = *forward++;
    if (c == EOF) {
        if (current == buffer1) {
            current = buffer2;
            load_buffer(buffer2);
        } else {
            current = buffer1;
            load_buffer(buffer1);
        }
        forward = current;
        return *forward++;
    }
    return c;
}

NFA → DFA Conversion · Subset Construction Algorithm

ε-NFA Subset Construction DFA States GATE 2024

NFA Diagram

DFA Diagram (after conversion)

Subset Construction Steps

Step Operation NFA States DFA State Created
generate NFA and click Convert

DFA Transition Table

DFA State NFA States a b
Convert to see DFA transition table

Start state: — | Final states: —

GATE Level: Subset Construction Algorithm

📌 Step 1: ε-closure

Find all states reachable from a given state using ε transitions

ε-closure({q0}) = {q0, q1, q2}
📌 Step 2: Move

For each symbol, find states reachable on that symbol

move({q0,q1}, a) = {q3,q4}
📌 Step 3: Subset Construction

Each DFA state = set of NFA states

DFA state A = {q0,q1,q2}
📌 Step 4: Mark Final States

Any DFA state containing an NFA final state is final

If q3 ∈ DFA state → DFA state is final

# Lexeme Token type Line Column Language
enter source and click Tokenize

Language-Specific Tokenization

C/C++: Keywords: int, float, return, if, else, while, for, include, define | Preprocessor directives, pointers (*, &), struct/class

Java: Keywords: public, class, static, void, new, import, package | Access modifiers, generics <T>, annotations @Override

Python: Keywords: def, class, import, from, as, if, elif, else, while, for, in | Indentation-based blocks, self parameter, list comprehensions

Parse Tree

enter expression and click Parse

parsing steps will appear here

First & Follow Sets

LL(1) Parsing Table

Non-terminal Terminals (click Parse to generate)
parsing table will be generated automatically

Top-Down Parsing Concepts

LL(1) Parsing

Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, 1 token lookahead

Recursive Descent

Top-down parser with recursive functions for each non-terminal

Left Recursion

Must be eliminated for top-down parsing: A → Aα | β becomes A → βA', A' → αA' | ε

Bottom-Up Parsing · Shift-Reduce & LR Parsing

Bottom-Up Parse Tree

enter expression and click Parse

Shift-Reduce Parsing Steps

Shift: move next input to stack Reduce: replace RHS with LHS Accept: parsing successful
Step Stack Input Buffer Action Production/Handle
parsing steps will appear here

LR(0) Items & States

LR(0) Items

Click Parse to generate

Canonical Collection

Click Parse to generate

LR Parsing Table

State ACTION (Terminals) GOTO (Non-terminals)
parsing table will be generated automatically

Handle Pruning & Rightmost Derivation

Rightmost derivation will appear here

Bottom-Up Parsing Concepts

Shift-Reduce Parsing

Uses a stack to hold grammar symbols. Shift: push next input. Reduce: pop RHS, push LHS.

Handle

A substring that matches the RHS of a production; reducing it is one step in reverse rightmost derivation.

LR Parsing

Left-to-right scan, Rightmost derivation in reverse. LR(0), SLR(1), LR(1), LALR(1) variants.

Conflicts

Shift-Reduce: can't decide to shift or reduce. Reduce-Reduce: multiple possible reductions.

Left Recursion Elimination · Transforming Grammars for LL(1) Parsing

Original Grammar (with Left Recursion)

E → E + T | T
T → T * F | F
F → ( E ) | id

Transformed Grammar (Left Recursion Eliminated)

E → T E'
E' → + T E' | ε
T → F T'
T' → * F T' | ε
F → ( E ) | id

ε represents epsilon (empty production)

Step-by-Step Transformation Process

Step Non-terminal Original Productions Transformation Applied
click Eliminate Left Recursion to see steps

GATE Formula: Left Recursion Elimination

For immediate left recursion of the form:

A → Aα₁ | Aα₂ | ... | Aαₙ | β₁ | β₂ | ... | βₘ

Transform to:

A → β₁ A' | β₂ A' | ... | βₘ A'
A' → α₁ A' | α₂ A' | ... | αₙ A' | ε
Why Eliminate?

Top-down parsers (LL, recursive descent) cannot handle left recursion as it causes infinite loops.

Types

Immediate (A→Aα) and Indirect (A→Bα, B→Aβ) left recursion.

Algorithm

For each non-terminal, separate left-recursive and non-left-recursive parts, then apply transformation.

Practice Examples

Example 1

A → A B | C
B → B d | e
C → f

Example 2 (Expressions)

E → E + T | E - T | T
T → T * F | T / F | F
F → ( E ) | id

Example 3

S → S a | S b | c | d

GATE Previous Year Questions

GATE 2018: Eliminate left recursion from the grammar: A → Aa | b

Show Answer

A → bA' , A' → aA' | ε

GATE 2015: Remove left recursion from: E → E + T | T, T → T * F | F, F → id

Show Answer

E → T E', E' → + T E' | ε, T → F T', T' → * F T' | ε, F → id

Left Factoring · Deferring Decisions for Predictive Parsing

Original Grammar (with Common Prefixes)

A → a b | a c | a d

Factored Grammar (Common Prefix Removed)

A → a A'
A' → b | c | d

ε represents epsilon (empty production) where applicable

Step-by-Step Left Factoring Process

The Left Factoring Algorithm

  1. Step 1: Identify productions with a common prefix: A → αβ₁ | αβ₂ | ... | αβₙ | γ
  2. Step 2: Create a new non-terminal A' to handle the different suffixes
  3. Step 3: Transform to: A → αA' | γ and A' → β₁ | β₂ | ... | βₙ
  4. Step 4: Repeat for any remaining common prefixes in A'
Step Non-terminal Original Productions Common Prefix & Transformation
click Apply Left Factoring to see steps

Parse Tree Comparison: Before vs After Factoring

Without Left Factoring ❌

A
α
β₁
β₂
β₃

Common prefix α repeated in each branch

With Left Factoring ✅

A
α
A'
β₁
β₂
β₃

Common prefix factored out, decision deferred to A'

LL(1) Parsing Table: Before vs After Factoring

Non-terminal Input: a Input: b Input: c Input: d
A (before) CONFLICT A→ab, A→ac, A→ad — — —
A (after) CLEAR A→aA' — — —
A' (after) — A'→b A'→c A'→d

Left factoring resolves multiple entries in the parsing table, making the grammar LL(1).

Practice Examples for GATE

Example 1: Simple Prefix

A → a b | a c | a d

Common prefix: "a" → factor it out

Example 2: Dangling Else

stmt → if E then stmt | if E then stmt else stmt

Classic dangling-else ambiguity

Example 3: Expression Grammar

E → T+E | T
T → int | int*T | (E)

Multiple common prefixes

GATE Formula: Left Factoring

For productions with common prefix α:

A → αβ₁ | αβ₂ | ... | αβₙ | γ

Transform to:

A → α A' | γ
A' → β₁ | β₂ | ... | βₙ
Why Factor?

Eliminates multiple entries in LL(1) parsing table, enabling predictive parsing without backtracking.

vs Left Recursion

Left factoring handles common prefixes; left recursion elimination handles A → Aα patterns. Both needed for LL(1).

Decision Deferral

Parser reads α first, then decides which β to take based on next input symbol.

GATE Previous Year Questions

GATE 2017: Consider the grammar: S → iEtS | iEtSeS | a (where 'i' stands for 'if', 'e' for 'else'). Apply left factoring.

Show Answer

S → iEtS S' | a
S' → eS | ε

GATE 2015: After left factoring, the grammar A → abd | acd | ae becomes:

Show Answer

A → a A'
A' → bd | cd | e

GATE 2013: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of left factoring?

A) Eliminates backtracking
B) Makes grammar suitable for LL(1)
C) Removes ambiguity
D) Decreases number of productions

Show Answer

Answer: D - Left factoring often INCREASES number of productions (adds new non-terminals).

FIRST & FOLLOW Sets · Essential for LL(1) Parsing

FIRST Sets

Click Compute to see FIRST sets

FOLLOW Sets

Click Compute to see FOLLOW sets

Step-by-Step Computation Process

Step Non-terminal FIRST Computation FOLLOW Computation
click Compute to see step-by-step process

GATE Formula: FIRST & FOLLOW Computation Rules

FIRST(X) Rules
  1. If X is terminal → FIRST(X) = {X}
  2. If X → ε → add ε to FIRST(X)
  3. If X → Y₁Y₂...Yₖ:
    • Add FIRST(Y₁) - {ε} to FIRST(X)
    • If Y₁ → ε, add FIRST(Y₂) - {ε}
    • Continue until a Yⱼ doesn't derive ε
    • If all Yᵢ → ε, add ε to FIRST(X)
Example: FIRST(E) for E → TE'
FIRST(E) = FIRST(T) = { (, id }
FOLLOW(A) Rules
  1. Place $ in FOLLOW(start symbol)
  2. If A → αBβ:
    • Add FIRST(β) - {ε} to FOLLOW(B)
    • If β → ε, add FOLLOW(A) to FOLLOW(B)
  3. If A → αB, add FOLLOW(A) to FOLLOW(B)
Example: FOLLOW(E) for E → TE'
FOLLOW(E) = { $, ) }

Key Properties for GATE

LL(1) Condition

For A → α | β, we must have:
FIRST(α) ∩ FIRST(β) = ∅
If ε ∈ FIRST(α), then FIRST(β) ∩ FOLLOW(A) = ∅

Nullable Non-terminals

A non-terminal A is nullable if:
A → ε or A → X₁X₂...Xₙ where all Xᵢ are nullable

FIRST of String

FIRST(αβ) = FIRST(α) if ε ∉ FIRST(α)
else (FIRST(α) - {ε}) ∪ FIRST(β)

Follow Set Rules

FOLLOW never contains ε
FOLLOW(start) always contains $

GATE Previous Year Questions

GATE 2021: Consider the grammar: S → aS | bS | ε. Compute FIRST(S) and FOLLOW(S).

Show Answer

FIRST(S) = {a, b, ε}
FOLLOW(S) = {$}

GATE 2019: For grammar E → TE', E' → +TE' | ε, T → FT', T' → *FT' | ε, F → (E) | id. Find FIRST and FOLLOW for all non-terminals.

Show Answer

FIRST(E) = FIRST(T) = FIRST(F) = {(, id}
FIRST(E') = {+, ε}
FIRST(T') = {*, ε}
FOLLOW(E) = {$, )}
FOLLOW(E') = FOLLOW(E) = {$, )}
FOLLOW(T) = {+, $, )}
FOLLOW(T') = FOLLOW(T) = {+, $, )}
FOLLOW(F) = {*, +, $, )}

Practice Examples

Example 1

S → Aa | b
A → cS | ε

Example 2 (Expression)

E → E+T | T
T → T*F | F
F → (E) | id

Example 3 (Nullable)

S → aAB
A → bC | ε
B → c | ε
C → d

LR Parsing Algorithms · LR(0), SLR, CLR, LALR

LR(0): No lookahead SLR(1): Simple LR with FOLLOW CLR(1): Canonical LR with lookahead LALR(1): Lookahead LR (merged states)

Augmented Grammar

E' → E
E → E + T | T
T → T * F | F
F → ( E ) | id

LR(0) Canonical Collection

Click Compute LR Items to generate

LR(0) Parsing Table

State ACTION GOTO
Compute LR Items to generate

SLR(1) Parsing Table (Simple LR with FOLLOW)

State ACTION GOTO
Click Show SLR(1) to generate

CLR(1) Parsing Table (Canonical LR with Lookahead)

State ACTION GOTO
Click Show CLR(1) to generate

LALR(1) Parsing Table (Lookahead LR - Merged States)

State ACTION GOTO
Click Show LALR(1) to generate

Parsing Steps (LR(0))

Step Stack Input Buffer Action Details
Select algorithm and compute to see parsing steps

GATE Comparison: LR Parsing Algorithms

Algorithm Lookahead States Power Table Size Conflicts
LR(0) No lookahead Fewest Weakest Small Many shift-reduce conflicts
SLR(1) Uses FOLLOW sets Same as LR(0) Medium Small Resolves many using FOLLOW
CLR(1) Explicit lookahead Most (canonical) Most powerful Very large Fewest conflicts
LALR(1) Merged lookahead Same as LR(0) Almost CLR(1) Small May introduce reduce-reduce

When to use which?

✅ LR(0) Grammar

No shift-reduce conflicts. Every state has either shift or reduce, not both. Rare in practice.

✅ SLR(1) Grammar

LR(0) items + FOLLOW resolves conflicts. Works for most programming languages.

✅ CLR(1) Grammar

Most general. Can parse all deterministic context-free languages. Large tables.

✅ LALR(1) Grammar

CLR(1) states merged. Used in YACC, Bison. Good balance of power and size.

GATE Previous Year Questions

GATE 2021: Which of the following is TRUE about LALR(1) parsers?

A) They have more states than CLR(1)
B) They may introduce reduce-reduce conflicts not in CLR(1)
C) They cannot parse all LR(1) grammars
D) They use lookahead in items

Show Answer

Answer: B - Merging states with same core but different lookaheads may introduce reduce-reduce conflicts.

GATE 2019: Consider the grammar: E → E + T | T, T → id. The number of states in the LR(0) item set is _____

Show Answer

Answer: 8 (Calculate using closure and goto)

GATE 2017: Which parser has the highest parsing power?

A) LR(0) B) SLR(1) C) LALR(1) D) CLR(1)

Show Answer

Answer: D - CLR(1) (Canonical LR) has the highest parsing power.

Shift Operation · Pushing Next Input Token onto Stack

SHIFT: Move token from input → stack Stack grows from left to right Current token highlighted

Stack & Input Visualization

Parser Stack

0
top

Stack contains states and grammar symbols

Input Buffer

id
+
id
*
id
$

Current token: id

Current Operation: SHIFT id to stack

Moving token 'id' from input buffer to stack

Shift Operations Trace

Step Stack (before shift) Input Token Action Stack (after shift)
Click Simulate Shift Operations to start

Shift Operation Animation

id
Stack

Understanding the Shift Operation

📌 What is Shift?

In LR parsing, SHIFT means:

  • Read the next input token
  • Push it onto the parser stack
  • Push the corresponding state
  • Move input pointer forward
Stack: [0] → SHIFT id → [0 id 5]
⚡ When to Shift?

The parser shifts when:

  • ACTION table entry says "sX" (shift to state X)
  • No handle is found on top of stack
  • Need more tokens before reducing
🔄 Shift vs Reduce
SHIFT → Push token + state
REDUCE → Pop RHS, push LHS
Formal Definition
SHIFT( statei, tokenj ) → statek

If ACTION[i, tokenj] = sX, then:
Push tokenj onto stack, then push state X

GATE Previous Year Questions on Shift Operation

GATE 2020: In LR parsing, what happens during a shift action?

A) Pop symbols from stack
B) Push next input symbol onto stack
C) Replace handle with LHS
D) Accept the input

Show Answer

Answer: B - Shift pushes the next input symbol onto the stack along with a state.

GATE 2018: Given ACTION[2, '+'] = s6, what does this mean?

Show Answer

Answer: In state 2, if the next input is '+', shift it onto the stack and go to state 6.

GATE 2015: How many stack pushes occur during a single shift operation in LR parsing?

Show Answer

Answer: 2 - The grammar symbol and the new state are both pushed onto the stack.

Reduce Operation · Replacing Handle with Non-terminal

REDUCE: Replace handle with LHS Handle: RHS of production found on stack Stack size decreases after reduce

Stack & Handle Visualization

Parser Stack

top
0

Current Handle

—
Reduction Rule: —
Non-terminal: —
Pop count: 0 symbols

Remaining Input

Current Operation: REDUCE

Waiting for simulation...

Reduce Operations Trace

Step Stack (before reduce) Handle Found Production Used Stack (after reduce)
Click Simulate Reduce Operations to start

Handle Pruning (Reverse Rightmost Derivation)

Understanding the Reduce Operation

📌 What is Reduce?

In LR parsing, REDUCE means:

  • Find a handle (RHS of a production) on top of stack
  • Pop the handle symbols (and their states) from stack
  • Push the LHS non-terminal onto stack
  • Consult GOTO table for new state
Stack: [0 id 5] → REDUCE by F→id → [0 F 3]
⚡ What is a Handle?

A handle is:

  • A substring that matches the RHS of a production
  • Appears on top of the stack (for bottom-up parsing)
  • Reducing it is one step in reverse rightmost derivation

Example: In grammar E → E + T | T, when stack has E + T, that's a handle for E

🔄 Reduce vs Shift
SHIFT → Push token + state (stack grows)
REDUCE → Pop RHS, push LHS (stack shrinks)
Formal Definition of Reduce
If ACTION[statei, token] = rX (reduce using production X: A → β)
then: pop 2×|β| symbols from stack, then push A, then push GOTO[new_top, A]

Example: Reduce by F → id → pop 'id' and its state, push 'F', push new state from GOTO

GATE Previous Year Questions on Reduce Operation

GATE 2021: In LR parsing, what happens during a reduce action?

A) Push next input symbol onto stack
B) Pop handle from stack and push LHS
C) Accept the input
D) Shift to next state

Show Answer

Answer: B - Reduce pops the handle (RHS) from stack and pushes the LHS non-terminal.

GATE 2019: Given grammar E → E + T | T, T → id. If stack contains "E + T" and next input is $, what action is performed?

Show Answer

Answer: REDUCE by E → E + T, replacing "E + T" with "E"

GATE 2017: How many stack pops occur during a reduce action for production A → XYZ (3 symbols)?

Show Answer

Answer: 6 - Each symbol has a corresponding state, so pop 3 symbols + 3 states = 6 pops.

Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) · Omitting Syntactic Details

Syntactic sugar removed Only essential operators/nodes Used in semantic analysis

Parse Tree vs Abstract Syntax Tree

Parse Tree (Concrete)

Generate to see comparison

Includes all grammar symbols: terminals, parentheses, etc.

Abstract Syntax Tree

Generate to see comparison

Omits parentheses, semicolons, and other syntactic sugar

Abstract Syntax Tree Visualization

enter expression and click Build AST

AST Construction Steps

Step Operation Node Created Syntactic Details Omitted
Build AST to see construction steps

AST Node Details

Node ID Node Type Value/Operator Children Original Token
Build AST to see node details

GATE Insight: AST vs Parse Tree

📌 Parse Tree (Concrete Syntax Tree)
  • Contains all grammar symbols (terminals & non-terminals)
  • Includes parentheses, semicolons, commas
  • One-to-one mapping with grammar rules
  • Used during parsing phase
E → E + T → (E + T) node
📌 Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
  • Omits syntactic sugar (parentheses, semicolons)
  • Only contains operators and operands
  • More compact representation
  • Used in semantic analysis & code generation
E + T → (+) node with children E and T
📊 Key Differences
Feature Parse Tree Abstract Syntax Tree
Size Larger (includes all tokens) Smaller (omits syntactic details)
Parentheses Present as nodes Absent (implicit in tree structure)
Example: a + b E → E + T → T + T → id + id (+ a b) or operator node
Used in Parsing (syntax analysis) Semantic analysis, optimization

AST Examples for Practice

Expression with precedence

a + b * c

AST: (+) with left 'a', right (*) with 'b','c'

If-Else statement

if (x > 0) y = 10 else y = 20

AST: If node with condition, then, else branches

While loop

while (i < 10) { sum += i; i++; }

AST: While node with condition and body block

GATE Previous Year Questions on AST

GATE 2020: What is the difference between a parse tree and an abstract syntax tree?

Show Answer

Parse tree contains all syntactic information including terminals (like parentheses, semicolons), while AST abstracts away syntactic details, keeping only the essential semantic structure. AST is more compact and used in later compiler phases.

GATE 2018: For the expression a = b * -c + d, how many nodes will be there in the AST?

Show Answer

Answer: 7 nodes (Assignment node, '+' node, '*' node, unary '-' node, and leaf nodes for a, b, c, d)
Parse tree would have more nodes due to parentheses and non-terminals.

GATE 2015: Which of the following is NOT present in an AST but present in a parse tree?

A) Operators B) Operands C) Parentheses D) Keywords

Show Answer

Answer: C) Parentheses - Parentheses are syntactic sugar; the tree structure itself encodes precedence.

Type Errors Detected

Type System in Action

✅ Strong Typing

int a = 5; char b = a;

❌ Error: Cannot assign int to char without explicit cast

🔄 Type Coercion

float x = 5; // 5 → 5.0

✓ Implicit conversion from int to float

📊 Type Promotion

char c = 'A'; int i = c; // 'A' → 65

✓ char promoted to int (ASCII value)

⚡ Type Inference

auto x = 3.14; // x is double

✓ Type inferred from initializer

GATE Previous Year Questions on Type Checking

GATE 2021: Consider the following C code: int x = 5; float y = 3.14; int z = x + y; What is the result of type checking?

Show Answer

Answer: Type mismatch warning/error. In strict typing, int + float requires explicit cast. In C, y would be implicitly converted to int (truncated), losing precision.

GATE 2019: In static type checking, when does type checking occur?

A) Runtime B) Compile time C) Link time D) Load time

Show Answer

Answer: B - Static type checking occurs at compile time.

Type Rules Configuration

Type Inference · Automatic Type Deduction

Type Inference Type Deduction Type Promotion auto keyword

Type Promotions

enter expression and click Infer Types

Type Inference Tree (for last expression)

Infer types to see deduction tree

GATE Level: Type Inference Rules

📌 Rule 1: Literals
  • 42 → int
  • 3.14 → double
  • true/false → bool
  • 'A' → char
  • "hello" → const char[6]
📌 Rule 2: Binary Operations
  • T1 + T2 → common type after promotion
  • int + double → double
  • float + double → double
  • char + int → int
📌 Rule 3: auto Deduction
  • auto x = expr → type of expr
  • auto& y = expr → reference to expr's type
  • const auto& z = expr → const reference
  • decltype(expr) → exact type of expr
📊 Type Promotion Hierarchy
char (1) short (2) int (4) long (8)
float (4) double (8) long double (12+)

GATE Practice Examples

Example 1: Basic Types

int → double → mixed → boolean

Example 2: References & Pointers

references, pointers, const qualifiers

Example 3: Type Promotions

int→double, float→double promotions

Example 4: Advanced Types

initializer_list, arrays, rvalue refs

Type Inference in Compiler Design

Type inference is the ability to automatically deduce the type of an expression at compile time. Key concepts for GATE:

Static Typing

Types are checked at compile time. Type inference reduces verbosity while maintaining safety.

Hindley-Milner

Most powerful type inference algorithm, used in ML, Haskell. Can infer most general type.

Local vs Global

Local inference (C++ auto) works within expressions. Global inference (ML) works across functions.

Type Constraints

Type inference solves equations: from expr1: T1, expr2: T2, op: T1×T2→T3, deduce T3.

Type Inference Algorithm Steps:
  1. Assign type variables to subexpressions
  2. Generate constraints from language rules
  3. Solve constraints using unification
  4. Apply substitution to get concrete types

GATE Previous Year Questions on Type Inference

GATE 2021: Consider the following C++ code: auto x = 5; auto y = 3.14; auto z = x + y; What is the type of z?

Show Answer

Answer: double - Due to type promotion, int + double → double

GATE 2019: In Hindley-Milner type inference, what is unification?

Show Answer

Answer: Unification is the process of finding a substitution that makes two type expressions equal. It's the core algorithm in type inference.

GATE 2017: For the expression λx.λy.x y, infer the most general type.

Show Answer

Answer: (a → b) → a → b - Function takes a function and an argument, applies function to argument.

Identifier Lookup Scope Resolution Attribute Retrieval Undeclared Detection

Identifiers Found
0
Successfully Resolved
0
Undeclared Identifiers
0
Shadowed Variables

Source Code with Identifier Highlighting

🟢 Global scope
🟠 Function scope
🔵 Block scope
💜 Undeclared error

Symbol Lookup Results

# Identifier Line Lookup Result Type Scope Value Memory Declaration Line
enter code and click Perform Lookup

Scope Hierarchy with Identifiers

🌐 Global scope

Symbol Table Contents

ID Identifier Type Scope Value Line Memory
No symbols in table

GATE Insight: Symbol Table Lookup Algorithm

📌 Lookup Process
  1. Start from current scope
  2. Search for identifier in current scope's symbol table
  3. If found → return attributes (type, value, line, memory)
  4. If not found and parent exists → repeat with parent scope
  5. If no parent → undeclared identifier error
lookup("x", current_scope) → {type: int, value: 42, scope: "main"}
📌 Attributes Retrieved
  • Type: int, float, char, array, pointer
  • Scope: global, function, block
  • Value: current value (if initialized)
  • Memory: allocated address
  • Line: declaration line number
  • Size: bytes occupied
📌 Shadowing Detection

When an identifier in inner scope has same name as outer scope:

  • Inner declaration hides outer declaration
  • Lookup returns innermost declaration
  • Compiler may warn about shadowing
int x; { int x; // shadows outer x }
📝 Lookup Algorithm Pseudocode
function lookup(name, currentScope):
    // Search in current scope
    if symbol exists in currentScope.symbolTable[name]:
        return {
            type: symbol.type,
            value: symbol.value,
            scope: currentScope.name,
            line: symbol.line,
            memory: symbol.memory,
            size: symbol.size
        }
    
    // If not found and has parent, search parent
    if currentScope.parent != null:
        return lookup(name, currentScope.parent)
    
    // Not found in any scope
    return error("Undeclared identifier: " + name)

GATE Previous Year Questions on Symbol Table Lookup

GATE 2022: In a compiler, during semantic analysis, when an identifier 'x' is encountered, which of the following is retrieved from the symbol table?

A) Token type only
B) Type, scope, and memory location
C) Only the value
D) Parse tree node

Show Answer

Answer: B - Symbol table stores type, scope, memory location, and other attributes.

GATE 2020: Consider the following C code:

int a = 5;
void f() {
    int a = 10;
    a = a + 1;
}

When looking up 'a' inside f(), which declaration is found?

Show Answer

Answer: The local 'a' (line 3) is found due to lexical scoping. The global 'a' is shadowed.

GATE 2018: In which phase of compiler does symbol table lookup for identifiers occur?

A) Lexical Analysis B) Syntax Analysis C) Semantic Analysis D) Code Generation

Show Answer

Answer: C) Semantic Analysis - Type checking and scope resolution occur during semantic analysis.

Scope Resolution · Determining Variable Declarations

Lexical Scoping Nested Scopes Name Hiding Static/Block Scope

Scope Analysis Dashboard

0
Variable References
0
Successfully Resolved
0

0
Undeclared References

Source Code with Scope Highlighting

🟢 Global scope
🟠 Function scope
🔵 Block scope
🔴 Shadowed declaration
💜 Undeclared error

Scope Hierarchy & Variable Binding

Scope Tree

🌐 Global scope

Symbol Table (by scope)

Variable Reference Resolution

# Reference Line Resolved to Scope Declaration Line Resolution Path
enter code and click Resolve Scopes

GATE Level: Scope Resolution Rules

📌 Rule 1: Lexical Scoping

Variables are resolved based on static program structure, not runtime call stack.

Refer to the innermost enclosing scope
📌 Rule 2: Name Hiding

A declaration in an inner scope hides declarations in outer scopes with the same name.

int x; { int x; // hides outer x }
📌 Rule 3: Search Order

Compiler searches:

  1. Current block scope
  2. Enclosing blocks (innermost to outermost)
  3. Function scope
  4. Global scope
📊 Scope Resolution Algorithm
function resolve(name, currentScope):
    // Search current scope
    if declaration exists in currentScope:
        return declaration
        
    // If not found and current scope has parent
    if parentScope exists:
        return resolve(name, parentScope)
    
    // Not found in any scope
    return error("Undeclared identifier: " + name)

GATE Practice Examples

Example 1: Simple Hiding

int a = 5;
void f() { int a = 10; b = a; }

Inner 'a' hides global 'a'

Example 2: Nested Blocks

Multiple levels of nesting and shadowing

Example 3: Parameters & Blocks

Function parameters create new scope

GATE Previous Year Questions on Scope

GATE 2020: Consider the following C program:

int x = 5;
void f() {
    int x = 10;
    {
        int x = 15;
        x++;
    }
    x--;
}
printf("%d", x);

What is printed?

Show Answer

Answer: 5 - The global x is never modified. Inner x declarations are separate.

GATE 2018: What is the output of following C code?

int a = 1;
int main() {
    int a = 2;
    {
        int a = 3;
        printf("%d ", a);
    }
    printf("%d", a);
    return 0;
}
Show Answer

Answer: 3 2 - Inner block prints 3, function scope prints 2.

GATE 2015: Which of the following is true about lexical scoping?

A) Variables are resolved at runtime based on call stack
B) Variables are resolved based on program structure
C) Variables are resolved by dynamic scope
D) All variables must be global

Show Answer

Answer: B - Lexical scoping uses static program structure.

Label & Goto Validation · Ensuring Jump Targets Exist

Label Definition Goto Statement Undefined Label Duplicate Label

Label Validation Dashboard

0
Label Definitions
0
Goto Statements
0
Valid Jumps
0
Validation Errors

Source Code with Label/Goto Highlighting

🟢 Label Definition
🔵 Goto Statement
🔴 Undefined Label Error
🟠 Duplicate Label Error
💜 Valid Jump

Label Definitions

# Label Name Line Column Status Used By
click Validate to see label definitions

Goto Statements Analysis

# Goto Target Line Column Target Exists? Target Line Validation
click Validate to see goto statements

Label Scope Rules

Rule 1: Function Scope

Labels have function scope - they are visible throughout the entire function where they are defined.

void func() { goto label; label: return; } ✓

Rule 2: No Cross-Function Gotos

Cannot jump from one function to a label in another function.

void f1() { goto f2_label; } void f2() { f2_label: } ✗

Rule 3: No Duplicate Labels

Labels must be unique within the same function.

label: x=5; label: y=10; ✗

Rule 4: Label Definition Required

Every goto must have a corresponding label in the same function.

goto missing; // error if 'missing' not defined

GATE Insight: Label & Goto Validation

📌 Semantic Analysis Phase

Label validation occurs during semantic analysis after parsing. The compiler builds a symbol table of labels and verifies each goto has a target.

⚡ Symbol Table for Labels

Labels are stored in a separate symbol table with: name, defining function, line number, and address for code generation.

🔄 Two-Pass Approach

Some compilers use two passes: first collect all labels, then validate gotos. This handles forward references naturally.

Common Label/Goto Errors

❌ Undefined Label

goto end; // but 'end:' not defined

Compiler error: "label 'end' used but not defined"

❌ Duplicate Label

start: x=5; start: y=10; // two 'start' labels

Compiler error: "duplicate label 'start'"

❌ Cross-Function Goto

void f1() { goto f2_label; } void f2() { f2_label: }

Compiler error: "cannot jump from this function to another function's label"

❌ Goto into Block (C/C++)

goto inside; { int x = 5; inside: x = 10; }

Compiler error: "jump to label crosses initialization of 'x'"

GATE Previous Year Questions on Label & Goto

GATE 2019: Consider the following C program:

int main() {
    int a = 5;
    goto label;
    {
        int b = 10;
        label:
        a = b;  // Is this valid?
    }
    return 0;
}

What is the result of semantic analysis?

Show Answer

Answer: Error - "jump to label crosses initialization of 'b'". In C/C++, cannot jump into a block that bypasses variable initialization.

GATE 2017: In which phase of compiler does label validation occur?

A) Lexical Analysis B) Syntax Analysis C) Semantic Analysis D) Code Generation

Show Answer

Answer: C) Semantic Analysis - Checking that goto targets exist is a semantic rule, not syntactic.

GATE 2015: What is the scope of a label in C?

Show Answer

Answer: Function scope - labels are visible throughout the entire function where they are defined, regardless of block structure.

Conditional Type Casting · Implicit Int to Float Conversions

Implicit Cast Explicit Cast Truncation Type Promotion

Type Casting Analysis

0
Expressions Analyzed
0
Implicit Casts
0
Explicit Casts
0
Truncations

Type Promotion Hierarchy

char (1 byte)
short (2 bytes)
int (4 bytes)
long (8 bytes)
float (4 bytes)
double (8 bytes)

Lower types are promoted to higher types in expressions

Type Casting Analysis Results

# Expression Left Type Right Type Operation Result Type Cast Type
enter expressions and click Analyze Type Casting

GATE Level: Type Casting Rules

📌 Rule 1: Implicit Casting

In expressions with mixed types, lower types are automatically promoted to higher types:

  • int + float → float
  • float + double → double
  • char + int → int
  • int * double → double
float x = 5; // 5 (int) → 5.0 (float)
📌 Rule 2: Explicit Casting

Programmer forces type conversion using cast operator:

  • (float)5 / 2 → 2.5
  • (int)3.14 → 3 (truncation)
  • (char)65 → 'A'
int x = (int)3.14; // 3.14 truncated to 3
📌 Rule 3: Truncation

When converting from floating point to integer, fractional part is discarded:

  • int x = 3.99; // x = 3
  • int y = -2.7; // y = -2

⚠️ Warning: Truncation loses precision!

📊 Type Casting Hierarchy (from lowest to highest)
char short int long float double

GATE Previous Year Questions on Type Casting

GATE 2020: Consider the expression: int a = 5; float b = 3.14; float c = a + b; What is the type of a+b and what conversion occurs?

Show Answer

Answer: a+b is of type float. Implicit type casting occurs where int 'a' is promoted to float before addition (int → float).

GATE 2018: What is the output of: int x = 5/2; float y = 5/2; float z = 5.0/2;

Show Answer

Answer: x = 2 (integer division truncates), y = 2.0 (5/2 is int division, then cast to float), z = 2.5 (5.0 is float, so float division)

GATE 2015: Which of the following involves implicit type conversion?

A) (float)5/2 B) 5/2.0 C) 5/2 D) 5.0/2.0

Show Answer

Answer: B - 5/2.0 has implicit conversion of int 5 to float 5.0. Option A is explicit cast.

Array Bound Checking · Detecting Static Index Errors at Compile-Time

Compile-Time Check Out of Bounds Constant Expression Runtime Check Needed

Array Bound Analysis Dashboard

0
Array Declarations
0
Array Accesses
0
Compile-Time Errors
0
Need Runtime Check

Source Code with Bound Checking

🟢 Within bounds
🔴 Out of bounds error
🟠 Needs runtime check
🔵 Constant expression

Array Declarations

# Array Name Element Type Size Valid Index Range Line Initializer
click Check Array Bounds to analyze

Array Access Analysis

# Array Index Expression Line Index Value Valid Range Status
click Check Array Bounds to analyze

GATE Insight: Compile-Time Array Bound Checking

📌 When can bounds be checked at compile-time?
  • Index is a constant expression (literal, sizeof, const variable)
  • Array size is known at compile-time (static arrays)
  • Index is computed from constants: arr[2+3], arr[sizeof(arr)/4 - 1]
int arr[5]; arr[3] = 10; ✓ (3 within 0-4)
📌 When is runtime check needed?
  • Index is a variable: arr[i]
  • Index computed from variables: arr[x+y]
  • Array size is dynamic (C99 VLA)
int i = getUserInput(); arr[i]; ⚠️ needs runtime check
📌 Common Errors Detected
  • Index too high: arr[10] where size is 5
  • Negative index: arr[-1]
  • Off-by-one: for i ≤ size instead of i < size
  • Zero-sized array access
📊 Array Bound Checking in Compiler Phases
Phase What is checked
Lexical Analysis Array syntax: arr[expr]
Syntax Analysis Parse tree for array access
Semantic Analysis ✅ Type checking, ✅ Bound checking (constant expressions)
Code Generation Insert runtime checks for variable indices

GATE Previous Year Questions on Array Bound Checking

GATE 2020: Consider the following C code:

int arr[5] = {1,2,3,4,5};
int i = 2;
int x = arr[i+3];  // Line P
int y = arr[sizeof(arr)/sizeof(int)];  // Line Q

Which of these lines cause compile-time array bound error?

Show Answer

Answer: Line Q: sizeof(arr)/sizeof(int) = 5, so index 5 is out of bounds (max 4).
Line P: i+3 is not constant expression, so runtime check needed, not compile-time error.

GATE 2018: Which of the following is TRUE about array bound checking in C?

A) Always done at compile-time
B) Always done at runtime
C) Done at compile-time for constant indices, runtime for variable indices
D) Never done in C

Show Answer

Answer: C - C compilers can detect constant index out of bounds at compile-time, but variable indices require runtime checks.

GATE 2015: In which phase of compiler is array bound checking typically performed?

A) Lexical Analysis B) Syntax Analysis C) Semantic Analysis D) Code Optimization

Show Answer

Answer: C) Semantic Analysis - Bound checking is a semantic rule that verifies index expressions against array declarations.

Practice Examples for GATE

Example 1: Basic Bounds

int arr[10];
arr[5] = 0; // valid
arr[10] = 1; // error (max 9)
arr[-1] = 2; // error

Example 2: Constant Expressions

arr[2+2] ✓, arr[2*3] ✗, arr[sizeof/4-1] ✓

Example 3: Loop Bound Errors

for(i=0; i<=5; i++) arr[i] → i=5 out of bounds

Function Call Validation · Matching Arguments with Parameters

Parameter Count Type Compatibility Mismatch Errors Type Promotion

Validation Dashboard

0
Functions Declared
0
Function Calls
0
Valid Calls
0
Validation Errors

Function Declarations

# Function Name Return Type Parameters Param Count Line Signature
click Validate to see function declarations

Function Call Analysis

# Function Call Line Expected Params Actual Args Count Match? Type Match? Status
click Validate to see function calls

Validation Errors

Parameter Matching Visualization

Select a function call to see parameter matching

GATE Insight: Function Call Validation Rules

📌 Rule 1: Parameter Count

The number of arguments must match the number of parameters exactly.

int add(int a, int b) → add(5, 10) ✓
add(5) ✗, add(5,10,15) ✗
📌 Rule 2: Type Compatibility

Argument types must match parameter types (with implicit promotions allowed).

float mult(float x) → mult(5) ✓ (int→float)
mult(3.14) ✓, mult("hi") ✗
📌 Rule 3: Type Promotion Rules

Implicit promotions allowed:

  • char → int → long → float → double
  • int → float allowed
  • float → int NOT allowed (requires explicit cast)

GATE Previous Year Questions

GATE 2021: Consider the function declaration: void func(int a, float b, char c). Which of the following calls are valid?

A) func(5, 3.14, 'A')
B) func(5, 3, 'B')
C) func(5.5, 3.14, 'C')
D) func(5, 3.14)

Show Answer

Answer: A and B are valid. A matches exactly, B promotes int to float. C requires double→float demotion (error). D has wrong count.

GATE 2019: In which phase of compiler is function call validation performed?

A) Lexical Analysis B) Syntax Analysis C) Semantic Analysis D) Code Generation

Show Answer

Answer: C) Semantic Analysis - Checking function calls requires type information and symbol table lookup.

Three-Address Code (TAC) Generation · Intermediate Representation

Temporary Variables t1, t2, t3... Control Flow Basic Blocks

TAC Generation Summary

0
Total TAC Statements
0
Temporary Variables
0
Labels Created
0
Basic Blocks

Three-Address Code Output


        

    


    
    

        
 Quadruple Representation

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Operation
                        Arg1
                        Arg2
                        Result
                        Description
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Generate TAC to see quadruples
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Triple Representation

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Operation
                        Arg1
                        Arg2
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Generate TAC to see triples
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 TAC Generation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Expression
                        Generated TAC
                        Temp Used
                        Rule Applied
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Generate TAC to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Basic Blocks & Flow Graph

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Three-Address Code

        
        

            

                
📌 What is TAC?

                
Three-Address Code is an intermediate representation where each instruction has at most three operands: x = y op z.

                

                    t1 = b * c
t2 = a + t1
a = t2
                

            


            

                
📌 Quadruples

                
Representation as (op, arg1, arg2, result):

                

                    
(*, b, c, t1)

                    
(+, a, t1, t2)

                    
(=, t2, -, a)

                

            


            

                
📌 Triples

                
Uses indices to refer to results:

                

                    
(*, b, c)

                    
(+, a, (0))

                    
(=, a, (1))

                

            


            

                
📌 Indirect Triples

                
Separate instruction list and execution order list.

                Instructions: (1) (*,b,c) (2) (+,a,(1)) (3) (=,a,(2))
Order: 1, 2, 3
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on TAC

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Convert the expression a = - (b + c) * d into three-address code.

            

                Show Answer
                

                    t1 = b + c

                    t2 = - t1

                    t3 = t2 * d

                    a = t3
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: How many temporary variables are needed for x = a + b * c - d / e in TAC?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 3 - t1 = b * c, t2 = d / e, t3 = a + t1, x = t3 - t2
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2017: Which of the following is NOT a form of intermediate code?

            
A) Quadruples B) Triples C) Abstract Syntax Tree D) DAG

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: D) DAG - DAG is a data structure, not a form of intermediate code.
                

            

        

    






    
 Quadruples · (op, arg1, arg2, result) Representation


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Quadruple Format
             (op, arg1, arg2, result)
             Temporary Variables
             Control Flow
        

    


    
    

        
 Quadruple Generation Summary

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Quadruples

            

            

                
0

                
Temporary Variables

            

            

                
0

                
Arithmetic Ops

            

            

                
0

                
Control Flow

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Quadruple Representation

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Operator
                        Argument 1
                        Argument 2
                        Result
                        Description
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Generate quadruples to see representation
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Triple Representation (for comparison)

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Operator
                        Argument 1
                        Argument 2
                    

                
                
                    

                        No triples generated
                    

                
            

        

        

             Triples use indices instead of result fields. Compare with quadruples above.
        

    


    
    

        
 Quadruple Generation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Expression
                        Quadruple Generated
                        Temp Variable
                        Rule Applied
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Generate to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Quadruple Optimization Techniques

        
        

            

                
1. Constant Folding

                
t1 = 5 + 3 → t1 = 8

                
Evaluate constant expressions at compile-time

            

            

                
2. Copy Propagation

                
t2 = t1 → replace t2 with t1

                
Eliminate unnecessary temporary variables

            

            

                
3. Dead Code Elimination

                
Remove quadruples whose results are never used

            

            

                
4. Algebraic Simplification

                
t1 = t2 * 1 → t1 = t2

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Quadruples: Advantages & Disadvantages

        
        

            

                
 Advantages

                
    
                    
  • Easy to rearrange: Moving quadruples doesn't affect others
    • 
                    
  • Explicit results: Each instruction has a result field
    • 
                    
  • Optimization friendly: Well-suited for code optimization
    • 
                    
  • Symbol table independence: No pointer dereferencing needed
    • 
                    
  • Three-address code: Direct mapping to TAC
    • 
                

            

            
            

                
 Disadvantages

                
    
                    
  • More memory: Stores result field (4 fields per instruction)
    • 
                    
  • Temporary variables: Creates many temporaries
    • 
                    
  • Less compact: More space than triples
    • 
                    
  • Indirect triples: For optimization, indirect triples may be better
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Quadruples vs Triples vs Indirect Triples

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Feature
                        Quadruples
                        Triples
                        Indirect Triples
                    

                
                
                    

                        Format
                        (op, arg1, arg2, result)
                        (op, arg1, arg2)
                        Instruction list + pointer list
                    

                    

                        Result Storage
                        Explicit in result field
                        Implicit (position index)
                        Separate pointer list
                    

                    

                        Space per instruction
                        4 fields
                        3 fields
                        3 fields + pointer
                    

                    

                        Optimization ease
                        Easy (can reorder)
                        Hard (indices change)
                        Easy (pointers can be updated)
                    

                    

                        Memory
                        More
                        Less
                        Medium
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Common Quadruple Patterns

        
        

            

                
Assignment

                (=, x, -, y)
                
y = x

            

            

                
Arithmetic

                (+, a, b, t1)
                
t1 = a + b

            

            

                
Unary Minus

                (uminus, a, -, t1)
                
t1 = -a

            

            

                
Conditional Jump

                (if, a, b, L1)
                
if a goto L1

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on Quadruples

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Convert the expression a = b * c + d / e into quadruples. How many temporary variables are needed?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Quadruples:

                    (1) (*, b, c, t1)

                    (2) (/, d, e, t2)

                    (3) (+, t1, t2, t3)

                    (4) (=, t3, -, a)

                    Temporary variables needed: 3
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: Which of the following is NOT an advantage of quadruples over triples?

            
A) Easier to optimize
B) Takes less memory
C) Instructions can be rearranged easily
D) Explicit result field

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: B) Takes less memory - Quadruples actually take more memory (4 fields vs 3 fields in triples).
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2017: Consider the code: x = y + z * w. How many quadruples are generated?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 3 quadruples

                    (1) (*, z, w, t1)

                    (2) (+, y, t1, t2)

                    (3) (=, t2, -, x)
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2015: In quadruple representation, what does the entry (j<, a, b, (4)) represent?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: Conditional jump - if a < b then jump to quadruple number 4.
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Simple Arithmetic

                a = b * c + d
                
2 temporaries, 3 quadruples

            

            

                
Example 2: Unary & Parentheses

                x = -y * (z + 5)
                
3 temporaries, 4 quadruples

            

            

                
Example 3: Control Flow

                if (a > b) goto L1 else L2
                
Conditional and unconditional jumps

            

            

                
Example 4: Array Access

                a[i] = b[j] * 10
                
Array indexing quadruples

            

        

    






    
 Triples & Indirect Triples · Index-Based Intermediate Representation


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Triples: (op, arg1, arg2)
             Indirect Triples: Pointer List
             No Temporary Variables
             Memory Efficient
        

    


    
    

        
 Triple Generation Summary

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Triples

            

            

                
0

                
Arithmetic Ops

            

            

                
0

                
Control Flow

            

            

                
0

                
Indirect Entries

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        

            
            
        


        
        

            
 Triple Representation (op, arg1, arg2)

            
            

                

                    
                        

                            #
                            Operator
                            Argument 1
                            Argument 2
                        

                    
                    
                        

                            
                                 Generate triples to see representation
                            
                        

                    
                

                

                     Triples use position indices to refer to results. For example, (0) refers to the result of triple #0.
                

            

        


        
        

            
 Indirect Triple Representation (Pointer List)

            
            

                
                

                    
Instruction List (Triples)

                    

                        

                            
                                

                                    #
                                    Op
                                    Arg1
                                    Arg2
                                

                            
                            
                                
Generate triples first

                            
                        

                    

                

                
                
                

                    
Execution Order (Pointer List)

                    

                        

                            
                                

                                    Order
                                    Pointer
                                    Points to Triple
                                

                            
                            
                                
No execution order

                            
                        

                    

                

            

            
            

                

                    
                    Key Insight: Indirect triples separate the instruction list from execution order. 
                    This makes code optimization easier - you can reorder execution without changing the actual instructions!
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Triple Generation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Expression
                        Triple Generated
                        References
                        Rule Applied
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Generate to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Triples vs Indirect Triples vs Quadruples

        
        

            

                
📌 Standard Triples

                
Format: (op, arg1, arg2)

                
Memory: 3 fields per instruction (compact)

                
Result: Implicit (position index)

                
Example: For a = b * c + d:

                

                    (0) (*, b, c)

                    (1) (+, (0), d)

                    (2) (=, a, (1))
                

                
 Problem: Moving instructions changes indices (hard to optimize)

            


            

                
📌 Indirect Triples

                
Format: Instruction list + pointer list

                
Memory: 3 fields + pointers (more)

                
Optimization: Easy - reorder pointers, not instructions

                
Example: Same expression:

                

                    Instructions: same as triples

                    Execution: [0, 1, 2] or [0, 2, 1] etc.
                

                
 Benefit: Reorder execution without changing instructions

            


            

                
📌 Quadruples (for comparison)

                
Format: (op, arg1, arg2, result)

                
Memory: 4 fields per instruction (more)

                
Result: Explicit temporary variables

                
Example: For a = b * c + d:

                

                    (0) (*, b, c, t1)

                    (1) (+, t1, d, t2)

                    (2) (=, t2, -, a)
                

                
Temporary variables explicit

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Why Indirect Triples? Optimization Example

        
        

            

                

                    
Before Optimization

                    
Triples:
(0) (*, b, c)
(1) (+, (0), d)
(2) (=, a, (1))

Indirect:
Instructions: same as above
Order: [0, 1, 2]

                

                
                

                    
After Optimization (Reordering)

                    
Triples: ❌ Can't reorder without renumbering
(0) (*, b, c)
(2) (=, a, (1))  // (1) no longer exists!

Indirect: ✅ Just reorder pointers!
Instructions: unchanged
Order: [0, 2, 1] // just changed pointers!

                

            

            

                 
                Indirect triples keep instructions fixed - only the pointer list changes during optimization!
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions

        
        

            
GATE 2020: Which of the following is TRUE about indirect triples?

            
A) They use more memory than quadruples

            B) They make code optimization easier by separating execution order

            C) They cannot represent control flow statements

            D) They require temporary variables

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: B - Indirect triples separate instruction list from execution order, making optimization easier.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2018: Convert x = -a * b + c into triple representation.

            

                Show Answer
                

                    (0) (uminus, a, -)

                    (1) (*, (0), b)

                    (2) (+, (1), c)

                    (3) (=, x, (2))
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2015: What is the main advantage of indirect triples over standard triples?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: Code optimization is easier because the execution order can be changed without modifying the actual instructions - just update the pointer list.
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Simple Expression

                a = b * c + d
                
3 triples, uses index references

            

            

                
Example 2: Unary & Parentheses

                x = -y * (z + 5)
                
Unary minus, nested operations

            

            

                
Example 3: Control Flow

                if (a > b) goto L1 else L2
                
Conditional and unconditional jumps

            

            

                
Example 4: Complex Expression

                result = (a+b)*(c-d)/e
                
Multiple operations, nested

            

        

    






    
 Static Single Assignment (SSA) · Each Variable Assigned Once


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             φ-functions
             Basic Blocks
             Versioned Variables
             Optimization
        

    


    
    

        
 SSA Transformation Summary

        
        

            

                
0

                
Original Variables

            

            

                
0

                
Variable Versions

            

            

                
0

                
φ-functions

            

            

                
0

                
Basic Blocks

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code (Non-SSA)

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Static Single Assignment (SSA) Form

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 φ-functions (Phi Functions)

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Block
                        Phi Function
                        Arguments
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Convert to SSA to see phi functions
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Variable Versioning

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Original
                        SSA Versions
                        Definition Points
                        Use Points
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Convert to SSA to see variable versions
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 SSA Transformation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Operation
                        Before SSA
                        After SSA
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Convert to SSA to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Control Flow Graph with φ-functions

        
        

            

                 CFG will appear here (for control flow examples)
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Static Single Assignment (SSA) Properties

        
        

            

                
📌 What is SSA?

                
Static Single Assignment is an intermediate representation where each variable is assigned exactly once, and every variable is defined before it is used.

                

                    Before: x = a + b; x = x * c;

                    After:  x₁ = a + b; x₂ = x₁ * c;
                

            


            

                
📌 φ-functions (Phi Functions)

                
At join points in control flow, φ-functions select the correct version of a variable based on which incoming path was taken.

                

                    x₃ = φ(x₁, x₂)  // x₁ from block1, x₂ from block2
                

            


            

                
📌 Key Benefits

                
    
                    
  • Simplifies data flow analysis
    • 
                    
  • Enables powerful optimizations
    • 
                    
  • Makes def-use chains explicit
    • 
                    
  • Constant propagation is trivial
    • 
                    
  • Dead code elimination is easy
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 SSA Optimizations

                
    
                    
  • Copy propagation
    • 
                    
  • Constant folding
    • 
                    
  • Global value numbering
    • 
                    
  • Dead code elimination
    • 
                    
  • Partial redundancy elimination
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 SSA Construction Algorithm

        
        

            

                
Step 1: Insert φ-functions

                
At each join point (where multiple paths converge), insert φ-functions for each variable that may have different values along different paths.

                
if (cond)
    x = 1;      // x₁
else
    x = 2;      // x₂
// Join point
x₃ = φ(x₁, x₂); // φ-function

            

            
            

                
Step 2: Rename Variables

                
Replace each assignment with a new version of the variable. Update all uses to refer to the appropriate version.

                
x₁ = a + b
x₂ = x₁ * c
y₁ = x₂ + d
x₃ = x₂ - e
z₁ = y₁ + x₃
y₂ = z₁ * 2

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on SSA

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Consider the following code fragment:

            
a = b + c;
c = a + d;
a = e + f;
c = c + a;

            
After converting to Static Single Assignment (SSA) form, how many versions of variable 'a' will exist?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 2 versions (a₁ and a₂)

                    a₁ = b + c

                    c₁ = a₁ + d

                    a₂ = e + f

                    c₂ = c₁ + a₂
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: What is the purpose of φ-functions in SSA form?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: φ-functions are used at join points in the control flow graph to merge different definitions of the same variable coming from different paths. They ensure that each variable has a single static assignment while maintaining the correct dynamic value based on which path was taken.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2017: Which of the following optimizations are facilitated by SSA form?

            
A) Constant propagation
B) Dead code elimination
C) Copy propagation
D) All of the above

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: D) All of the above - SSA simplifies all these optimizations by making def-use chains explicit.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2015: In SSA form, how many times can a variable appear on the left-hand side of an assignment?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: Exactly once - Each variable version is assigned exactly once, which is the fundamental property of SSA.
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Simple Reassignment

                x = a + b
x = x * c
y = x + d
                
2 versions of x (x₁, x₂)

            

            

                
Example 2: With Control Flow

                if-else with phi functions
                
Includes φ-function at join point

            

            

                
Example 3: Loop

                while loop with induction variable
                
Multiple versions with loop φ

            

            

                
Example 4: Multiple φ-functions

                Multiple variables in branches
