Both x and y need φ-functions

            

        

    






    
 Control Flow Translation · if-else, while, for → TAC


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Labels: L1, L2, ...
             Conditional Jumps
             Basic Blocks
             Control Flow Graph
        

    


    
    

        
 Translation Summary

        
        

            

                
0

                
TAC Statements

            

            

                
0

                
Labels Created

            

            

                
0

                
Basic Blocks

            

            

                
0

                
Jump Instructions

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Three-Address Code Output

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Translation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Control Structure
                        Generated TAC
                        Labels Used
                        Translation Rule
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Translate to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Control Flow Graph

        
        

            

                 CFG will appear here for control structures
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Control Flow Translation Rules

        
        

            
            

                
📌 if-else Statement

                
if (x > y) {
    z = x;
} else {
    z = y;
}

                
Translation:

                
    if x > y goto L1
    goto L2
L1: z = x
    goto L3
L2: z = y
L3: ...

                
Rule: condition → jump to then/else

            


            
            

                
📌 while Loop

                
while (i < 10) {
    sum = sum + i;
    i = i + 1;
}

                
Translation:

                
L1: if i < 10 goto L2
    goto L3
L2: sum = sum + i
    i = i + 1
    goto L1
L3: ...

                
Rule: condition at entry, jump back

            


            
            

                
📌 for Loop

                
for (i=0; i<10; i++) {
    sum = sum + i;
}

                
Translation:

                
    i = 0
L1: if i < 10 goto L2
    goto L4
L2: sum = sum + i
L3: i = i + 1
    goto L1
L4: ...

                
Rule: init → condition → body → increment

            

        


        
        

            
📊 Translation Patterns Comparison

            

                
                    

                        Structure
                        Labels
                        Jumps
                        Key Pattern
                    

                
                
                    

                        if-then
                        1 (else label)
                        1 conditional, 1 unconditional
                        if cond goto L1; goto L2; L1: then; L2:
                    

                    

                        if-then-else
                        2 (else, end)
                        2 conditional, 1 unconditional
                        if cond goto L1; goto L2; L1: then; goto L3; L2: else; L3:
                    

                    

                        while loop
                        2 (start, exit)
                        1 conditional, 1 unconditional
                        L1: if cond goto L2; goto L3; L2: body; goto L1; L3:
                    

                    

                        for loop
                        3 (start, body, exit)
                        2 conditional, 2 unconditional
                        init; L1: if cond goto L2; goto L4; L2: body; L3: inc; goto L1; L4:
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Translate the following code to three-address code:

            
if (x > 0) {
    y = x + 1;
} else {
    y = 0;
}

            

                Show Answer
                
    if x > 0 goto L1
    goto L2
L1: y = x + 1
    goto L3
L2: y = 0
L3:

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: How many TAC statements are generated for a while loop with 3 statements in its body?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 6 + body statements

                    1: condition check

                    1: conditional jump

                    3: body statements

                    1: unconditional jump back

                    = 6 TAC statements total
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2017: In three-address code, how are labels represented for a for loop?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: For a for loop, three labels are typically used: L1 (start of condition), L2 (body), L3 (increment), and L4 (exit).

                    Pattern: init → L1 → condition → L2 → body → L3 → increment → goto L1 → L4 (exit)
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Simple if-else

                if (a > b) max = a; else max = b;
                
2 labels, 2 jumps

            

            

                
Example 2: While Loop

                while (x < 100) { x = x + y; y = y + 1; }
                
2 labels, backward jump

            

            

                
Example 3: For Loop

                for (i=0; i<10; i=i+1) { sum = sum + i; }
                
3 labels, 3 jumps

            

            

                
Example 4: Nested Control Flow

                if-else with nested while
                
Multiple labels, complex jumps

            

        

    






    
 Array Access Translation · a[i] → base + i × size


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Base Address
             Element Size
             Index Calculation
             Multi-dimensional
        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Array Address Calculation Formulas

        
        

            

                
📌 1D Array (Row-Major)

                

                    Addr(A[i]) = Base + i × w
                

                
where w = size of each element (4 for int, 8 for double, 1 for char)

            

            
            

                
📌 2D Array (Row-Major)

                

                    Addr(A[i][j]) = Base + (i × N + j) × w
                

                
where N = number of columns

            

            
            

                
📌 2D Array (Column-Major)

                

                    Addr(A[i][j]) = Base + (j × M + i) × w
                

                
where M = number of rows

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Three-Address Code Translation

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Detailed Translation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Array Access
                        Three-Address Code
                        Address Calculation
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Translate array accesses to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Memory Layout Visualization

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Array Access Translation

        
        

            

                
📌 Address Calculation Components

                
    
                    
  • Base Address: Starting memory location of array
    • 
                    
  • Element Size (w): Bytes per element (int=4, char=1, double=8)
    • 
                    
  • Index Calculation: i × w gives offset from base
    • 
                    
  • Final Address: Base + offset
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 TAC Translation Rules

                

                    x = arr[i] → 
                    t1 = i * 4; t2 = arr_base + t1; x = *t2
                

                

                    arr[i] = x → 
                    t1 = i * 4; t2 = arr_base + t1; *t2 = x
                

            


            

                
📌 Multi-dimensional Arrays

                
Row-Major (C/C++): arr[i][j] → base + (i × N + j) × w

                
Column-Major (Fortran): arr[i][j] → base + (j × M + i) × w

            

        


        

            
📊 Array Access Translation Algorithm

            
function translateArrayAccess(arrayName, indexExpr, elementSize):
    // Generate temporary for index calculation
    t1 = new Temp()
    code = t1 + " = " + indexExpr + " * " + elementSize
    
    // Calculate address
    t2 = new Temp()
    code += t2 + " = " + arrayName + "_base + " + t1
    
    // For reading: t3 = *t2
    // For writing: *t2 = value
    return { address: t2, code: code }

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on Array Access

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Translate the array access a[i] = b[j] * 5 into three-address code.

            

                Show Answer
                
t1 = j * 4        // offset for b[j]
t2 = b_base + t1  // address of b[j]
t3 = *t2          // value of b[j]
t4 = t3 * 5       // multiply by 5
t5 = i * 4        // offset for a[i]
t6 = a_base + t5  // address of a[i]
*t6 = t4          // store result

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: For a 2D array int A[10][20] stored in row-major order with base address 1000, find the address of A[5][15] (int size = 4).

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 1000 + (5 × 20 + 15) × 4 = 1000 + (100 + 15) × 4 = 1000 + 115 × 4 = 1000 + 460 = 1460
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2017: How many temporary variables are needed to translate x = a[i] + b[j] * c[k] into three-address code?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 6 temporaries (3 for address calculations, 2 for values, 1 for multiplication result)
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Read

                int a[10]; int x = a[5];
                
Simple array read: need offset calculation

            

            
            

                
Example 2: Write

                float b[20]; b[i+1] = 3.14;
                
Array write with complex index

            

            
            

                
Example 3: 2D Array

                int matrix[5][10]; val = matrix[i][j];
                
2D array in row-major order

            

            
            

                
Example 4: Nested Access

                double d[30]; x = d[d[i]];
                
Array index is another array element

            

        

    






    
 Boolean Expressions · Short-Circuit Evaluation & Translation


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Short-Circuit
             Numerical (0/1)
             Labels: Ltrue, Lfalse
             Jump Code
        

    


    
    

        
 Original Boolean Expression

        

            
        

    


    
    

        

            
            
            
        


        
        

            
🔀 Short-Circuit Evaluation (Jump Code)

            

                

            

            

                 Short-circuit evaluation stops as soon as result is known. 
                For &&, if left is false, right is not evaluated. For ||, if left is true, right is not evaluated.
            

        


        
        

            
🔢 Numerical Translation (0 = false, 1 = true)

            

                

            

            

                 Boolean values are computed as 0 (false) or 1 (true). All subexpressions are evaluated.
            

        


        
        

            
📊 Control Flow Graph (for if-else/while)

            

                

                    
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Translation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Operator/Expression
                        Short-Circuit Code
                        Numerical Code
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Translate to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Boolean Expression Translation

        
        

            

                
📌 Short-Circuit (Jump) Code

                
Each boolean operator translates to conditional jumps:

                
    
                    
  • A && B → if !A goto Lfalse; if !B goto Lfalse; Ltrue: ...
    • 
                    
  • A || B → if A goto Ltrue; if B goto Ltrue; goto Lfalse;
    • 
                    
  • !A → if A goto Lfalse; goto Ltrue;
    • 
                

                

                    if (a > b && c < d) →
    if a > b goto L1
    goto Lfalse
L1: if c < d goto Ltrue
    goto Lfalse
                

            


            

                
📌 Numerical Translation

                
Boolean values as 0/1 with arithmetic:

                
    
                    
  • A && B → t1 = A == 0 ? 0 : (B != 0 ? 1 : 0)
    • 
                    
  • A || B → t1 = A != 0 ? 1 : (B != 0 ? 1 : 0)
    • 
                    
  • !A → t1 = A == 0 ? 1 : 0
    • 
                

                

                    t1 = a > b
t2 = c < d
t3 = t1 && t2
                

            


            

                
📌 Relational Operators

                
Comparing values generates boolean results:

                
    
                    
  • if a > b goto Ltrue
    • 
                    
  • t1 = a > b  // 0 or 1
    • 
                    
  • t1 = 1 if a > b else 0
    • 
                

                

                    a > b  →  if a > b goto L1
         t1 = 0
         goto L2
L1:      t1 = 1
L2:
                

            

        


        
        

            
📊 Translation Strategies Comparison

            

                
                    

                        Aspect
                        Short-Circuit (Jump)
                        Numerical (0/1)
                    

                
                
                    

                        Evaluation
                        Stops early when result known
                        All subexpressions evaluated
                    

                    

                        Code Size
                        More compact for conditions
                        More instructions
                    

                    

                        Side Effects
                        May not evaluate all expressions
                        Always evaluates everything
                    

                    

                        Use Case
                        if-else, while conditions
                        Boolean variables, assignments
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions

        
        

            
GATE 2020: Translate the following code using short-circuit evaluation:

            
if (x > 0 && y < 10 || z == 0) {
    a = 1;
} else {
    a = 0;
}

            

                Show Answer
                
    if x > 0 goto L1
    goto L2
L1: if y < 10 goto L3
L2: if z == 0 goto L3
    goto L4
L3: a = 1
    goto L5
L4: a = 0
L5:

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: Which translation strategy always evaluates all operands?

            
A) Short-circuit B) Numerical C) Both D) Neither

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: B) Numerical - Numerical translation computes all subexpressions as 0/1 values.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2018: Write three-address code for: a = (b > c) && (d < e) || !(f == g) using short-circuit evaluation.

            

                Show Answer
                
    if b > c goto L1
    goto L2
L1: if d < e goto L3
L2: if f == g goto L4
    goto L3
L4: goto L2
L3: a = 1
    goto L5
L2: a = 0
L5:

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples

        
        

            

                
Example 1: AND

                (a > b) && (c < d)
                
Simple AND with short-circuit

            

            

                
Example 2: Multiple OR

                (x == 0) || (y != 5) || (z > 10)
                
Multiple OR with short-circuit

            

            

                
Example 3: Mixed with NOT

                !(a && b) || (c && !d)
                
AND, OR, NOT combinations

            

            

                
Example 4: In Control Flow

                if (i < n && arr[i] != key)
                
Boolean in if-statement

            

        

    






    
 Declaration Translation · Variables & Functions → TAC


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Variable Declarations
             Array Declarations
             Function Declarations
             Memory Allocation
        

    


    
    

        
 Declaration Translation Summary

        
        

            

                
0

                
Variables Declared

            

            

                
0

                
Arrays Declared

            

            

                
0

                
Functions Declared

            

            

                
0

                
Memory (bytes)

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        

            
            
            
        


        
        

            
📝 Three-Address Code for Declarations

            

                

            

            

                 Variable declarations allocate memory and optionally store initial values. 
                Functions generate prologue/epilogue code and parameter handling.
            

        


        
        

            
📊 Symbol Table with Memory Offsets

            

                

                    
                        

                            Name
                            Type
                            Scope
                            Offset
                            Size
                            Initial Value
                            TAC Reference
                        

                    
                    
                        
No symbols yet

                    
                

            

        


        
        

            
🧠 Memory Layout Visualization

            

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Translation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Declaration
                        Generated TAC
                        Explanation
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Translate declarations to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Declaration Translation Rules

        
        

            

                
📌 Variable Declarations

                
    
                    
  • int x; → // allocate 4 bytes for x
    • 
                    
  • float y = 3.14; → y = 3.14  (store initial value)
    • 
                    
  • int a = 10, b = 20; → a = 10; b = 20;
    • 
                

                

                    Offset calculation: SP = SP - size
                

            


            

                
📌 Array Declarations

                
    
                    
  • int arr[10]; → // allocate 40 bytes
    • 
                    
  • float matrix[5][5]; → // allocate 100 bytes (5×5×4)
    • 
                

                

                    arr_base = SP; SP = SP - 40
                

            


            

                
📌 Function Declarations

                
    
                    
  • Prologue: Save frame pointer, allocate local space
    • 
                    
  • Parameters: Accessed via FP + offset
    • 
                    
  • Local vars: Accessed via FP - offset
    • 
                    
  • Epilogue: Restore FP, return
    • 
                

                

                    func: push FP; FP = SP; SP = SP - locals
                

            

        


        
        

            
📊 Type Sizes (Typical)

            

                
                    

                        Type
                        Size (bytes)
                        Alignment
                        TAC Example
                    

                
                
                    

                        char
                        1
                        1
                        c = 'A'
                    

                    

                        short
                        2
                        2
                        s = 100
                    

                    

                        int
                        4
                        4
                        i = 42
                    

                    

                        float
                        4
                        4
                        f = 3.14
                    

                    

                        double
                        8
                        8
                        d = 2.718
                    

                    

                        pointer
                        8
                        8
                        p = &x
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on Declaration Translation

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Consider the following declarations in C:

            
int x = 10;
float y;
char str[20];

            
How many bytes of memory are allocated for these declarations (assuming typical sizes)?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 4 (int) + 4 (float) + 20×1 (char array) = 28 bytes
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: What three-address code is generated for function prologue?

            

                Show Answer
                
func:   // function label
    t1 = FP          // save frame pointer
    FP = SP          // set new frame pointer
    SP = SP - locals // allocate local space
    [function body]
    SP = SP + locals // deallocate locals
    FP = t1          // restore frame pointer
    return

            

        


        

            
GATE 2017: For the declaration int arr[10][20]; and float x = 5.5;, what is the total stack space allocated?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: Array: 10×20×4 = 800 bytes, float: 4 bytes → Total 804 bytes
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Simple Variables

                int a = 5; float b = 3.14; char c;
                
Basic variable declarations with initial values

            

            
        

                
Example 2: Arrays

                int arr[10]; float matrix[5][5]; char str[100] = "hello";
                
1D and 2D array declarations

            

            
            

                
Example 3: Function Prototypes

                int add(int a, int b); float max(float x, float y);
                
Function declarations

            

            
            

                
Example 4: Mixed Declarations

                Global, local, static, and pointer declarations
                
Different storage classes

            

        

    






    
 String Table Management · Storing Literal Strings in Constant Table


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             String Table
             String Interning
             Memory Layout
             Duplicate Elimination
        

    


    
    

        
 String Table Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total String Literals

            

            

                
0

                
Unique Strings

            

            

                
0

                
Total Bytes

            

            

                
0

                
Duplicates Saved

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 String Table (Constant Pool)

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        ID
                        String Literal
                        Length
                        Memory Address
                        Size (bytes)
                        References
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             enter code and click Build String Table
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 String References in Code

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Variable/Usage
                        String Literal
                        Line
                        Table Reference
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Build string table to see references
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 String Memory Layout (Constant Pool)

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: String Table Management

        
        

            

                
📌 What is String Table?

                
A constant pool (also called string table or literal pool) stores all string literals used in the program. Benefits:

                
    
                    
  • Memory efficiency: Duplicate strings stored once
    • 
                    
  • Faster comparisons: Compare by address instead of content
    • 
                    
  • Compile-time allocation: Strings placed in read-only data section
    • 
                    
  • String interning: All identical strings share same storage
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 String Table Operations

                
    
                    
  1. Insert: Add new string to table
    2. 
                    
  2. Lookup: Check if string already exists
    3. 
                    
  3. Get reference: Return pointer to string in table
    4. 
                    
  4. Optimization: Merge identical strings
    5. 
                

                

                    StringTable.lookup("Hello") → &str_table[42]
                

            


            

                
📌 Compiler Phases

                
    
                    
  • Lexical: Identify string literals
    • 
                    
  • Semantic: Build string table, eliminate duplicates
    • 
                    
  • Code Gen: Emit strings in .rodata section
    • 
                    
  • Linking: Merge string tables from different modules
    • 
                

            

        


        
        

            
📊 String Storage in Different Sections

            

                
                    

                        Section
                        Content
                        Access
                        Example
                    

                
                
                    

                        .rodata (Read-only data)
                        String literals, const variables
                        Read-only
                        "Hello World"
                    

                    

                        .data (Initialized data)
                        Initialized global strings
                        Read-write
                        char str[] = "data";
                    

                    

                        .bss (Uninitialized data)
                        Uninitialized char arrays
                        Read-write
                        char buffer[100];
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on String Table

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Consider the following C code:

            
char *s1 = "Hello";
char *s2 = "Hello";
char s3[] = "Hello";

            
How many string literals are stored in the string table?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 1 - Only one copy of "Hello" is stored in the string table (constant pool). 
                    Both s1 and s2 point to the same string literal. s3[] creates a separate array initialized with a copy.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: What is string interning in compiler design?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: String interning is the process of storing only one copy of each distinct string value. 
                    All identical string literals are mapped to the same entry in the string table, saving memory and 
                    enabling fast pointer-based comparisons.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2017: In which memory section are string literals typically stored?

            
A) Stack B) Heap C) .rodata D) .data

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: C) .rodata - String literals are stored in the read-only data section (.rodata) as they are constant and should not be modified.
                

            

        

    






    
 Syntax Tree Construction · From Parse Tree to Abstract Syntax Tree


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Parse Tree (Concrete)
             Syntax Tree (Abstract)
             Syntactic Sugar Removed
             Semantic Analysis Ready
        

    


    
    

        
 Parse Tree vs Syntax Tree

        
        

            
            

                

                     Parse Tree (Concrete)
                

                

                    Generate to see parse tree
                

                

                    Contains all grammar symbols: terminals, non-terminals, parentheses
                

            

            
            
            

                

                     Syntax Tree (Abstract)
                

                

                    Generate to see syntax tree
                

                

                    Omits non-terminals, parentheses, captures operator precedence
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Syntax Tree Visualization

        
        

            

                 enter expression and click Build Syntax Tree
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Syntax Tree Construction Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Operation
                        Node Created
                        Syntactic Details Omitted
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Build syntax tree to see construction steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Syntax Tree Node Details

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Node ID
                        Node Type
                        Value/Operator
                        Children
                        Original Token
                        Semantic Attribute
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Build syntax tree to see node details
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Syntax Tree Construction Algorithms

        
        

            

                
📌 S-attributed Definitions

                
Syntax-directed translation using synthesized attributes only.

                
    
                    
  • Build tree bottom-up
    • 
                    
  • Each node's attributes computed from children
    • 
                    
  • Ideal for expression trees
    • 
                

                

                    E → E₁ + T  { E.node = new Node('+', E₁.node, T.node) }
                

            


            

                
📌 L-attributed Definitions

                
Allow inherited attributes with left-to-right flow.

                
    
                    
  • Build tree top-down with inherited info
    • 
                    
  • Used for declarations, type checking
    • 
                    
  • More flexible than S-attributed
    • 
                

                

                    D → T L { L.in = T.type }
                

            


            

                
📌 Parse Tree → AST Conversion

                
Steps to convert concrete syntax to abstract:

                
    
                    
  1. Remove ε-productions
    2. 
                    
  2. Chain reduction (A→B→C becomes C)
    3. 
                    
  3. Eliminate parentheses
    4. 
                    
  4. Merge operator nodes
    5. 
                

            

        


        
        

            
📊 Parse Tree vs Syntax Tree vs AST

            

                
                    

                        Feature
                        Parse Tree
                        Syntax Tree
                        Abstract Syntax Tree
                    

                
                
                    

                        Contains
                        All grammar symbols
                        Only operators and operands
                        Optimized for analysis
                    

                    

                        Parentheses
                        Present
                        Absent (implied by structure)
                        Absent
                    

                    

                        Non-terminals
                        All present (E, T, F)
                        Removed
                        Removed
                    

                    

                        Size
                        Largest
                        Medium
                        Most compact
                    

                    

                        Use in Compiler
                        Parsing phase
                        Intermediate representation
                        Semantic analysis, optimization
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Practice Examples

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Arithmetic

                a = b * c + d
                
Operator precedence captured in tree structure

            

            

                
Example 2: If-Else

                if (x > 0) y = 10 else y = 20
                
Control flow syntax tree

            

            

                
Example 3: While Loop

                while (i < n) { sum += i; i++; }
                
Loop construct in syntax tree

            

            

                
Example 4: Declarations

                int x = 5; float y = 3.14;
                
Declaration syntax tree with types

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on Syntax Trees

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Consider the expression a = b * c - d / e. Draw the syntax tree after removing all non-terminals and parentheses.

            

                Show Answer
                
      (=)
     /   \
    a     (-)
         /   \
        (*)   (/)
       /  \   /  \
      b    c d    e

                
Explanation: The syntax tree captures operator precedence: * and / have higher precedence than -, so they appear lower in the tree.

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: How many nodes are there in the syntax tree for the expression a = (b + c) * d?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 5 nodes (assignment node '=', operator '*', operator '+', and identifiers a, b, c, d) = 7 nodes total.
                    
Parse tree would have additional nodes for parentheses and non-terminals.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2017: Which of the following is NOT present in a syntax tree but present in a parse tree?

            
A) Operators B) Identifiers C) Parentheses D) Constants

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: C) Parentheses - Parentheses are syntactic sugar; their effect is captured by the tree structure (operator precedence and associativity).
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2015: In compiler design, the syntax tree is used in which phase?

            
A) Lexical Analysis B) Syntax Analysis C) Semantic Analysis D) Code Optimization

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: C) Semantic Analysis - The syntax tree is the primary data structure used in semantic analysis for type checking and other semantic rules.
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Syntax Tree vs Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)

        
        

            

                
🌲 Syntax Tree

                
Each subexpression has its own node (no sharing)

                
For a + a * b:

      (+)
     /   \
    a     (*)
         /   \
        a     b

                
Node count: 5 nodes (a appears twice)

            

            
            

                
🔷 DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)

                
Common subexpressions are shared (optimized)

                
For a + a * b:

      (+)
     /   \
    a     (*)
         /   \
        a     b
        ↑ shared!

                
Node count: 4 nodes (a shared)

            

        

    






    
 Address Calculation · Computing Offsets for Arrays & Structures


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             1D/2D/3D Arrays
             Structures
             Row-Major/Column-Major
             Memory Layout
             Padding & Alignment
        

    


    
    

        
 Address Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Declarations

            

            

                
0

                
Address Calculations

            

            

                
0

                
Total Memory (bytes)

            

            

                
0

                
Padding Bytes

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Address Calculation Formulas

        
        

            
            

                

                     1D Array
                

                

                    Addr(A[i]) = Base + (i - LB) × w
                

                
where:

                • Base = starting memory address

                • i = index

                • LB = lower bound (usually 0)

                • w = element size (bytes)

                

                    Example: int arr[10]; arr[5] → Base + 5 × 4
                

            


            
            

                

                     2D Array (Row-Major)
                

                

                    Addr(A[i][j]) = Base + [(i - LBR) × N + (j - LBC)] × w
                

                
where:

                • N = number of columns

                • LBR = row lower bound (0)

                • LBC = column lower bound (0)

                • C/C++ uses row-major order

                

                    Example: int matrix[5][10]; matrix[2][3] → Base + (2×10 + 3) × 4
                

            


            
            

                

                     2D Array (Column-Major)
                

                

                    Addr(A[i][j]) = Base + [(j - LBC) × M + (i - LBR)] × w
                

                
where:

                • M = number of rows

                • Fortran uses column-major order

                • MATLAB also uses column-major

                

                    Example: int A[5][10]; A[2][3] (col-major) → Base + (3×5 + 2) × 4
                

            


            
            

                

                     3D Array (Row-Major)
                

                

                    Addr(A[i][j][k]) = Base + [(i × N × P) + (j × P) + k] × w
                

                
where:

                • N = number of rows

                • P = number of columns (depth)

                • Dimensions: [i][j][k]

                

                    Example: int cube[4][5][6]; cube[2][3][1] → Base + (2×5×6 + 3×6 + 1) × 4
                

            


            
            

                

                     Structure Member
                

                

                    Addr(s.member) = Base + offsetmember
                

                
where:

                • offsetmember = cumulative size of previous members

                • Includes padding for alignment

                

                    Example: struct { char c; int i; } → offset(i) = 4 (after padding)
                

            


            
            

                

                     Array of Structures
                

                

                    Addr(s[i].member) = Base + i × sizeof(struct) + offsetmember
                

                
where:

                • sizeof(struct) includes all members and padding

                • offsetmember = member offset within struct

                

                    Example: struct Point points[10]; points[5].x → Base + 5×12 + 0 (if Point has int x, int y)
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Address Calculation Results

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Detailed Address Calculations

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Access
                        Base
                        Formula
                        Calculation
                        Result Address
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Calculate addresses to see results
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Memory Layout Visualization

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Address Calculation Concepts

        
        

            

                
📌 Row-Major vs Column-Major

                
Row-Major (C/C++): Elements stored row by row

                

                    A[2][3] = Base + (2×N + 3)×w
                

                
Column-Major (Fortran): Elements stored column by column

                

                    A[2][3] = Base + (3×M + 2)×w
                

            


            

                
📌 Padding & Alignment

                
Structure padding ensures proper alignment for faster memory access:

                

                    struct S { char c; int i; }; // sizeof = 8, not 5
                

                
• char at offset 0

                • 3 bytes padding

                • int at offset 4

            


            

                
📌 Array of Structures

                
For struct S arr[10], address of arr[i].member:

                

                    Base + i × sizeof(S) + offset(member)
                

                
• Contiguous in memory

                • Each element size = sizeof(S)

            


            

                
📌 Dynamic Memory Allocation

                
For dynamically allocated arrays:

                

                    int *p = malloc(n × sizeof(int));

                    p[i] address = p + i × sizeof(int)
                

            

        


        

            
📊 Type Sizes & Alignment Requirements

            

                
                    

                        Type
                        Size (bytes)
                        Alignment
                        Typical Offset
                    

                
                
                    
char110 mod 1

                    
short220 mod 2

                    
int440 mod 4

                    
float440 mod 4

                    
double880 mod 8

                    
long880 mod 8

                    
pointer8 (64-bit)80 mod 8

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Consider a 2D array A[10][20] stored in row-major order. Each element requires 4 bytes. Base address is 1000. Find address of A[5][15].

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Formula: Base + (i × N + j) × w

                    = 1000 + (5 × 20 + 15) × 4

                    = 1000 + (100 + 15) × 4

                    = 1000 + 115 × 4

                    = 1000 + 460

                    = 1460
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: For a 3D array A[4][5][6] stored in row-major order, calculate address of A[2][3][1] if base = 2000 and element size = 2 bytes.

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Formula: Base + [(i × N × P) + (j × P) + k] × w

                    where N=5 (columns), P=6 (depth)

                    = 2000 + [(2 × 5 × 6) + (3 × 6) + 1] × 2

                    = 2000 + [60 + 18 + 1] × 2

                    = 2000 + 79 × 2

                    = 2000 + 158

                    = 2158
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2018: Consider the structure:

            
struct student {
    char name[20];
    int roll;
    float marks;
} s[50];

            
Base address of s is 1000. Find address of s[30].marks

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Step 1: Calculate struct size

                    • name[20]: 20 bytes

                    • int roll: 4 bytes (padding after name: none if char array size is multiple of 4)

                    • float marks: 4 bytes

                    Total struct size = 20 + 4 + 4 = 28 bytes


                    Step 2: Calculate offset of marks within struct

                    • offset = 20 + 4 = 24 bytes


                    Step 3: Final address

                    = Base + 30 × 28 + 24

                    = 1000 + 840 + 24

                    = 1864
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2017: A 2D array X[7][9] is stored in column-major order. If each element requires 2 bytes and base address is 1000, find address of X[3][4].

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Column-major formula: Base + (j × M + i) × w

                    where M = number of rows = 7

                    = 1000 + (4 × 7 + 3) × 2

                    = 1000 + (28 + 3) × 2

                    = 1000 + 31 × 2

                    = 1000 + 62

                    = 1062
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: 1D Array

                int arr[10]; x = arr[5];
                
Base + index × 4

            

            
            

                
Example 2: 2D Row-Major

                matrix[i][j] = base + (i×10 + j)×4
                
C/C++ style

            

            
            

                
Example 3: Structure

                p.x offset 0, p.y offset 4
                
No padding needed

            

            
            

                
Example 4: Array of Structs

                s[i].marks = base + i×size + offset
                
With padding calculation

            

        

    






    
 Procedure Call Translation · Calling Sequences & Stack Frames


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Stack Frame
             Parameter Passing
             Caller-Saved Registers
             Callee-Saved Registers
             Return Address
        

    


    
    

        
 Call Translation Summary

        
        

            

                
0

                
Function Calls

            

            

                
0

                
Parameters Passed

            

            

                
0

                
Nested Calls

            

            

                
0

                
Stack Frame (bytes)

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        

            
            
            
            
        


        
        

            
📝 Three-Address Code for Procedure Calls

            

                

            

        


        
        

            
📊 Stack Frame Layout

            

                

            

        


        
        

            
📋 Complete Calling Sequence

            

                

                    
                        

                            Step
                            Phase
                            Action
                            Generated Code
                        

                    
                    
                        
Translate to see calling sequence

                    
                

            

        


        
        

            
🔢 Register Allocation & Conventions

            

                

                    
Caller-Saved Registers

                    

                        
EAX - Scratch register, return value

                        
ECX - Scratch register

                        
EDX - Scratch register

                        
R8-R11 - Volatile registers (x86-64)

                    

                    
Caller must save before call if needed after

                

                
                

                    
Callee-Saved Registers

                    

                        
EBX - Preserved across calls

                        
ESI - Preserved across calls

                        
EDI - Preserved across calls

                        
EBP - Frame pointer

                        
ESP - Stack pointer

                    

                    
Callee must save before use and restore

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Parameter Passing Methods

        
        

            

                
Call by Value

                add(5, x);
                
Arguments are evaluated and copied to stack/registers

                

                    
param 5
param x
call add

                

            

            
            

                
Call by Reference

                swap(&x, &y);
                
Address of argument is passed

                

                    
t1 = &x
t2 = &y
param t1
param t2
call swap

                

            

            
            

                
Call by Value-Result

                proc(x);
                
Copy in on call, copy out on return

                

                    
param x
call proc
x = result

                

            

            
            

                
Call by Name

                proc(a[i]);
                
Parameter is re-evaluated each time (thunk)

                

                    
param address_of_thunk
call proc

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Calling Conventions Comparison

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Convention
                        Parameter Order
                        Stack Cleanup
                        Register Usage
                        Return Value
                    

                
                
                    

                        cdecl (C)
                        Right to left
                        Caller
                        EAX, ECX, EDX scratch
                        EAX
                    

                    

                        stdcall (Win32)
                        Right to left
                        Callee
                        EAX, ECX, EDX scratch
                        EAX
                    

                    

                        fastcall
                        First 2 in ECX, EDX
                        Callee
                        ECX, EDX for params
                        EAX
                    

                    

                        x86-64 SysV
                        RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9
                        Caller
                        6 register parameters
                        RAX
                    

                    

                        ARM AAPCS
                        R0-R3
                        Caller
                        4 register parameters
                        R0
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Procedure Call Translation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Phase
                        TAC Generated
                        Explanation
                    

                
                
                    
Translate to see steps

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Procedure Call Translation Concepts

        
        

            

                
📌 Activation Record (Stack Frame)

                

                    [High Addresses]

                    ← Arguments

                    ← Return Address

                    ← Old Frame Pointer

                    ← Local Variables

                    ← Temporaries

                    [Low Addresses]
                

                
Each function call gets its own activation record on stack

            


            

                
📌 Calling Sequence

                
    
                    
  1. Evaluate arguments (right to left)
    2. 
                    
  2. Save caller-saved registers
    3. 
                    
  3. Push arguments on stack (or use registers)
    4. 
                    
  4. Push return address
    5. 
                    
  5. Jump to function (CALL)
    6. 
                    
  6. Callee sets up frame (prologue)
    7. 
                    
  7. Execute function body
    8. 
                    
  8. Callee restores frame (epilogue)
    9. 
                    
  9. Return to caller (RET)
    10. 
                    
  10. Caller cleans up arguments (if needed)
    11. 
                

            


            

                
📌 Prologue & Epilogue

                
Prologue:

                
push ebp        ; save old frame pointer

mov ebp, esp    ; set new frame pointer

sub esp, N      ; allocate locals
                
                
Epilogue:

                
mov esp, ebp    ; deallocate locals

pop ebp         ; restore old frame pointer

ret             ; return
                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on Procedure Calls

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Consider the following C function call: f(a, b, c); Assuming cdecl calling convention, generate the three-address code for this call.

            

                Show Answer
                
param c
param b
param a
call f
t1 = return_val

                
Parameters are pushed right-to-left, caller cleans stack

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: In which memory section are activation records stored?

            
A) Heap B) Stack C) Data D) Code

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: B) Stack - Activation records are stored on the stack in LIFO order.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2017: What is the purpose of the frame pointer (EBP) in procedure calls?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: The frame pointer provides a stable reference point to access parameters (positive offsets) and local variables (negative offsets) within the stack frame, even as the stack pointer changes during function execution.
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Simple Call

                add(5, 10) → return 15
                
Basic procedure call with two parameters

            

            
            

                
Example 2: Recursive Call

                fact(5) → recursive calls
                
Shows multiple activation records on stack

            

            
            

                
Example 3: Call by Reference

                swap(&x, &y) → pointers passed
                
Address passing and dereferencing

            

            
            

                
Example 4: Nested Calls

                max(10, max(5,7)) → nested
                
Return value used as argument

            

        

    







    
 Syntax-Directed Translation · Attribute Grammars & Translation Schemes


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             S-attributed
             L-attributed
             Synthesized
             Inherited
             Translation Scheme
        

    


    
    

        

            
            
            
            
        


        
        

            
🌳 Parse Tree with Attribute Values

            

                

                     Enter grammar and expression to see parse tree
                

            

        


        
        

            
📊 Synthesized Attributes (Bottom-up Evaluation)

            

                

                    
                        

                            #
                            Node
                            Production
                            Semantic Rule
                            Attribute Value
                        

                    
                    
                        
Evaluate to see synthesized attributes

                    
                

            

            

                
Bottom-up Evaluation: Children evaluated first, then parent. Attributes flow upward in the parse tree.

            

        


        
        

            
📊 Inherited Attributes (Top-down with Context)

            

                

                    
                        

                            #
                            Node
                            Production
                            Inherited Rule
                            Attribute Value
                        

                    
                    
                        
Select declaration grammar to see inherited attributes

                    
                

            

            

                
Top-down with Inheritance: Parent passes information down to children. Used for type information, symbol tables.

            

        


        
        

            
📝 Translation Scheme (Actions embedded in grammar)

            

                

            

            

                 Translation schemes embed semantic actions within the grammar. Actions are executed during parsing.
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Syntax-Directed Translation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Node/Rule
                        Semantic Action
                        Attribute Computed
                        Value
                    

                
                
                    
Evaluate to see translation steps

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Attribute Grammar Types

        
        

            

                
📌 S-attributed SDT

                
Only synthesized attributes (val, type, code)

                
    
                    
  • Evaluated bottom-up (LR parsing)
    • 
                    
  • Attributes flow from children to parent
    • 
                    
  • Used in expression evaluation
    • 
                    
  • Example: E → E + T { E.val = E1.val + T.val }
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 L-attributed SDT

                
Inherited + synthesized with left-to-right flow

                
    
                    
  • Can be evaluated during LL parsing
    • 
                    
  • Attributes flow left-to-right, top-down
    • 
                    
  • Used for declarations, type checking
    • 
                    
  • Example: D → T L { L.in = T.type }
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Translation Schemes

                
Actions embedded in grammar

                
    
                    
  • Actions at end → for synthesized
    • 
                    
  • Actions in middle → for inherited
    • 
                    
  • Generate code during parsing
    • 
                    
  • Example: E → E + T { print('+') }
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Dependency Graphs

                
Attribute dependencies determine evaluation order

                
    
                    
  • Nodes: attribute instances
    • 
                    
  • Edges: dependencies
    • 
                    
  • Acyclic graph = evaluatable
    • 
                    
  • Cycle = circular dependency
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Attribute Dependency Graph

        
        

            

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Practice Examples

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Arithmetic (S-attributed)

                E → E + T | T
T → T * F | F
F → ( E ) | num
                
Synthesized attributes: val

            

            
            

                
Example 2: Declaration (L-attributed)

                D → T L
T → int | float
L → L , id | id
                
Inherited attribute: L.in

            

            
            

                
Example 3: If-Else Translation

                if C then S1 else S2
                
Code generation using SDT

            

            
            

                
Example 4: TAC Generation

                id := E
                
Generate three-address code

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on SDT

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Consider the grammar: E → E + T | T, T → T * F | F, F → (E) | id. If synthesized attribute val is used for expression value, what is the value of val at the root for input "3 + 5 * 2"?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 13 (5 * 2 = 10, 3 + 10 = 13). Due to precedence, multiplication evaluated first.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: Which of the following is TRUE about L-attributed definitions?

            
A) Can be evaluated during any traversal
B) Dependencies are restricted to left-to-right flow
C) Cannot have synthesized attributes
D) Must be evaluated bottom-up

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: B - L-attributed definitions allow inherited attributes with left-to-right flow, evaluable during LL parsing.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2018: For the SDT: D → T L { L.in = T.type }, T → int { T.type = int }, L → L1, id { addType(id.entry, L.in) }, L → id { addType(id.entry, L.in) }. What type of attributes are used?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: T.type is synthesized, L.in is inherited. This is an L-attributed definition.
                

            

        

    






    
 Operator Overloading Resolution · Compile-Time Polymorphism


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Member Function
             Non-Member Function
             Built-in Operators
             Candidate Functions
             Best Viable Function
        

    


    
    

        
 Operator Resolution Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Operators Found

            

            

                
0

                
Member Functions

            

            

                
0

                
Non-Member Functions

            

            

                
0

                
Built-in Operators

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        

            
            
            
            
        


        
        

            
📋 Operator Resolution Process

            

                

                    
                        

                            #
                            Expression
                            Operator
                            Left Operand Type
                            Right Operand Type
                            Resolution
                        

                    
                    
                        
Click Resolve Overloaded Operators to see results

                    
                

            

        


        
        

            
🎯 Candidate Functions for Selected Expression

            

                

                    
                        

                            #
                            Function Signature
                            Type
                            Parameters
                            Return Type
                        

                    
                    
                        
Select an expression to see candidate functions

                    
                

            

        


        
        

            
✅ Viable Functions (After Type Checking)

            

                

                    
                        

                            #
                            Function Signature
                            Conversion Sequence
                            Match Quality
                            Viable?
                        

                    
                    
                        
No viable functions yet

                    
                

            

        


        
        

            
🏆 Best Match Selection

            

                

                    Select an expression to see the best match
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Type Conversion Ranking (for overload resolution)

        
        

            

                
🥇 Exact Match

                
    
                    
  • No conversion needed
    • 
                    
  • Identity conversion
    • 
                    
  • Lvalue-to-rvalue
    • 
                

                int → int
            

            
            

                
🥈 Promotion

                
    
                    
  • char → int
    • 
                    
  • float → double
    • 
                    
  • short → int
    • 
                

                char → int
            

            
            

                
🥉 Standard Conversion

                
    
                    
  • int → float
    • 
                    
  • double → int
    • 
                    
  • derived* → base*
    • 
                

                int → float
            

            
            

                
🎯 User-defined Conversion

                
    
                    
  • Constructor conversion
    • 
                    
  • Conversion operator
    • 
                    
  • Lowest rank
    • 
                

                String → const char*
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Operator Overloading Resolution Rules

        
        

            

                
📌 Step 1: Candidate Functions

                
Collect all functions with the same name as the operator:

                
    
                    
  • Member functions of left operand's class
    • 
                    
  • Non-member functions in scope
    • 
                    
  • Built-in operators (if no overload)
    • 
                

                

                    operator+(const Complex&, const Complex&)
                

            


            

                
📌 Step 2: Viable Functions

                
Functions that can be called with given arguments:

                
    
                    
  • Same number of parameters
    • 
                    
  • Implicit conversions possible
    • 
                    
  • Accessible (public)
    • 
                

                

                    Complex + Complex ✓

                    Complex + int ✓ (conversion)
                

            


            

                
📌 Step 3: Best Viable Function

                
Select based on conversion ranking:

                
    
                    
  • Better conversion sequence wins
    • 
                    
  • Non-template vs template
    • 
                    
  • Non-member vs member (tie-breaker)
    • 
                

                

                    exact match > promotion > conversion
                

            


            

                
📌 Special Rules

                
    
                    
  • =, [], (), -> must be members
    • 
                    
  • Cannot overload ., ::, ?:, sizeof
    • 
                    
  • Preserve arity (number of operands)
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Practice Examples

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Binary Operators

                +, -, == overloads
                
Member and non-member candidates

            

            
                    

            
Example 2: Mixed Types

            String + const char*
            
Conversions and candidate ranking

        


            
            

                
Example 3: Unary Operators

                ++, --, *, -> overloads
                
Prefix vs postfix distinction

            

            
            

                
Example 4: Function Call

                operator() overload
                
Functor and matrix indexing

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions

        
        

            
GATE 2020: Consider the following C++ code:

            
class Complex {
    int real, imag;
public:
    Complex(int r=0, int i=0) : real(r), imag(i) {}
    Complex operator+(const Complex& c) { return Complex(real + c.real, imag + c.imag); }
};

int main() {
    Complex c1(1,2), c2;
    c2 = c1 + 5;  // Which operator is called?
}

            
Which operator is called for c1 + 5?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: The member operator+ is called after converting 5 to Complex(5,0) using the converting constructor.

                    This is a user-defined conversion sequence (rank 4).
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: Which of the following operators cannot be overloaded in C++?

            
A) [] B) -> C) . D) ()

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: C) . - The dot operator cannot be overloaded. Also cannot overload ::, ?:, sizeof.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2018: During overload resolution, which conversion sequence is better?

            
A) int → long → float
B) int → double

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: B) int → double is better because it's a single standard conversion (rank 3), while A involves multiple steps (rank 3 as well but longer sequence).
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2016: Consider:

            
void f(int);
void f(double);
f('a');  // Which f is called?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: f(int) - char to int is a promotion (rank 2), while char to double is a standard conversion (rank 3). Promotion is better.
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Operator Categories & Overloadability

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Category
                        Operators
                        Can Overload?
                        Must be Member?
                    

                
                
                    

                        Arithmetic
                        + - * / %
                        ✅ Yes
                        ❌ No (can be non-member)
                    

                    

                        Relational
                        == != < > <= >=
                        ✅ Yes
                        ❌ No
                    

                    

                        Assignment
                        = += -= *= /=
                        ✅ Yes
                        ✅ Yes (must be member)
                    

                    

                        Subscript
                        []
                        ✅ Yes
                        ✅ Yes (must be member)
                    

                    

                        Function Call
                        ()
                        ✅ Yes
                        ✅ Yes (must be member)
                    

                    

                        Member Access
                        ->
                        ✅ Yes
                        ✅ Yes (must be member)
                    

                    

                        Increment/Decrement
                        ++ --
                        ✅ Yes
                        ❌ No (but int parameter for postfix)
                    

                    

                        Special
                        new delete
                        ✅ Yes
                        ❌ No
                    

                    

                        Non-overloadable
                        . .* :: ?: sizeof
                        ❌ No
                        —
                    

                
            

        

    






    
 Control Flow Type Checking · Boolean Condition Validation


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Boolean Expression
             Non-Boolean Condition
             Syntax Error
             Control Flow
        

    


    
    

        
 Control Flow Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Control Flow Statements

            

            

                
0

                
Valid Boolean Conditions

            

            

                
0

                
Non-Boolean Conditions

            

            

                
0

                
Syntax Errors

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Source Code with Condition Highlighting

        
        

            
        

        
        

            
🟢 Valid boolean condition

            
🔴 Non-boolean condition

            
🟠 Syntax error

            
💜 Strict mode warning

        

    


    
    

        
 Control Flow Statement Analysis

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Statement Type
                        Condition
                        Condition Type
                        Line
                        Status / Message
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             enter code and click Check Condition Types
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Control Flow Type Checking Rules

        
        

            

                
📌 Rule 1: Boolean Conditions

                
In strictly typed languages, if, while, for conditions must be boolean expressions:

                
    
                    
  • if (x > y) ✓ (relational → boolean)
    • 
                    
  • if (x && y) ✓ (logical → boolean)
    • 
                    
  • if (x) ✗ (int, float, pointer not allowed in strict languages)
