    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Rule 2: C Language Rules

                
In C, any non-zero value is considered true:

                
    
                    
  • if (x) allowed (int → boolean conversion)
    • 
                    
  • if (ptr) allowed (pointer → boolean)
    • 
                    
  • if (x = 5) allowed but often bug (assignment in condition)
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Rule 3: Java/C# Rules

                
Strict boolean required:

                
    
                    
  • if (x) ✗ (compile error)
    • 
                    
  • if (x != 0) ✓ (explicit comparison)
    • 
                    
  • if (x == true) ✓ but redundant
    • 
                

            

        


        

            
📊 Type Compatibility for Conditions

            

                
                    

                        Expression Type
                        C/C++
                        Java/C#
                        Python
                    

                
                
                    
boolean✓✓✓

                    
int✓ (0=false, non-zero=true)✗✓ (0=False, non-zero=True)

                    
float✓ (0.0=false)✗✓ (0.0=False)

                    
pointer✓ (NULL=false)✗✗ (None in Python)

                    
assignment⚠️ (allowed but warning)✗✗

                
            

            

                 Click "Toggle Strict Mode" to switch between C-style and Java-style checking
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions

        
        

            
GATE 2020: Consider the following C code:

            
int x = 5;
if (x) {
    printf("True");
}
if (x = 0) {
    printf("False");
}

            
What is the output?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: "True" only. First if: x=5 (non-zero) → true. Second if: x=0 is assignment, value 0 → false.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: In which phase of compiler is it determined that the condition in an if statement has boolean type?

            
A) Lexical Analysis B) Syntax Analysis C) Semantic Analysis D) Code Generation

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: C) Semantic Analysis - Type checking of conditions occurs during semantic analysis.
                

            

        

    






    
 Semantic Analyzer · Multi-Language Semantic Rules Enforcement


    

        

            
            

                
                
                
                
            

        


        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Type Checking
             Scope Resolution
             Undeclared Variables
             Array Bound Checking
             Control Flow Types

 GATE Level: Language-Specific Semantic Rules

        
        

            

                
C/C++ Rules

                
    
                    
  • Implicit int → float allowed
    • 
                    
  • Non-zero values = true in conditions
    • 
                    
  • No array bounds checking
    • 
                    
  • Function prototypes required
    • 
                

            

            
            

                
Java Rules

                
    
                    
  • Strict type checking
    • 
                    
  • Boolean required in if/while
    • 
                    
  • Array bounds checked at runtime
    • 
                    
  • No implicit narrowing
    • 
                

            

            
            

                
Python Rules

                
    
                    
  • Dynamic typing
    • 
                    
  • Names must be defined before use
    • 
                    
  • Indentation defines scope
    • 
                    
  • Runtime type checking
    • 
                

            

        

    






    
 Polymorphic Functions · Compile-Time & Runtime Polymorphism


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Overloading
             Templates
             Virtual
             Static Binding
             Dynamic Binding
             VTable
        

    


    
    

        
 Polymorphism Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Functions

            

            

                
0

                
Overloaded Sets

            

            

                
0

                
Templates

            

            

                
0

                
Virtual Functions

            

            

                
0

                
Polymorphic Calls

            

        

    


    
    

        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        
        

            
📊 Polymorphism Types Comparison

            
            

                

                    

                         Compile-Time Polymorphism
                    

                    
    
                        
  • Function Overloading - Same name, different parameters
    • 
                        
  • Operator Overloading - Custom behavior for operators
    • 
                        
  • Templates - Generic functions/classes
    • 
                        
  • Resolved at compile-time (static binding)
    • 
                        
  • Faster execution (no runtime overhead)
    • 
                        
  • Also called "ad-hoc polymorphism"
    • 
                    

                

                
                

                    

                         Runtime Polymorphism
                    

                    
    
                        
  • Virtual Functions - Overridden in derived classes
    • 
                        
  • Inheritance - Base class pointers/references
    • 
                        
  • Resolved at runtime (dynamic binding)
    • 
                        
  • Uses Virtual Table (VTable)
    • 
                        
  • Slower (indirect call overhead)
    • 
                        
  • Also called "inclusion polymorphism"
    • 
                    

                

            


            
            

                
 Virtual Table (VTable) Mechanism

                
When a class has virtual functions, the compiler creates:

                
    
                    
  • A virtual table (vtable) - array of function pointers for the class
    • 
                    
  • A vptr (virtual pointer) in each object pointing to its class's vtable
    • 
                    
  • At runtime, calls through base pointer use vptr + vtable to find correct function
    • 
                

                

                    Shape* s = new Circle();  

                    s->draw();  // Circle::draw() called via vptr->vtable[draw_index]
                

            

        


        
        

            
🔍 Function Overloading Resolution

            
            

                

                    
Overloaded Function Sets

                    

                        
                    

                

                
                

                    
Resolution Rules (GATE)

                    
    
                        
  1. Exact match - Best
    2. 
                        
  2. Promotion (char→int, float→double)
    3. 
                        
  3. Standard conversion (int→float)
    4. 
                        
  4. User-defined conversion
    5. 
                        
  5. Ellipsis (...) - Worst
    6. 
                    

                

            

            
            

                

                    
                        

                            #
                            Function Name
                            Signature
                            Return Type
                            Line
                        

                    
                    
                        
Analyze to see overloaded functions

                    
                

            

        


        
        

            
🧩 Template Instantiation

            
            

                

                    
                        

                            #
                            Template
                            Instantiated For
                            Generated Function
                            Line
                        

                    
                    
                        
Analyze to see template instantiations

                    
                

            

            
            

                
 GATE Insight: Templates are compiled lazily - only instantiated when used. Each instantiation creates a separate function in the object code.

            

        


        
        

            
🎭 Virtual Function Hierarchy

            
            

                

                    
                        

                            #
                            Class
                            Function
                            Type
                            Override
                            VTable Entry
                        

                    
                    
                        
Analyze to see virtual functions

                    
                

            

        


        
        

            
📋 Virtual Table Memory Layout

            
            

                
            

            
            

                

                    
Memory Layout

                    
class Shape {
    [vptr] -> vtable_Shape
    // no data members
}

class Circle {
    [vptr] -> vtable_Circle
    double radius
}

vtable_Shape:
    0: Shape::area() (pure)
    1: Shape::draw()

vtable_Circle:
    0: Circle::area()
    1: Circle::draw()

                

                
                

                    
Object Size

                    
    
                        
  • ✓ Shape: 8 bytes (vptr only)
    • 
                        
  • ✓ Circle: 16 bytes (vptr + radius)
    • 
                        
  • ✓ Rectangle: 24 bytes (vptr + width + height)
    • 
                        
  • ✓ Virtual call overhead: 1 extra indirection
    • 
                    

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Polymorphic Function Calls

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Function Call
                        Resolved To
                        Binding Type
                        Line
                        Reason
                    

                
                
                    
Analyze to see function call resolutions

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on Polymorphism

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Consider the following C++ code:

            
class Base {
public:
    virtual void show() { cout << "Base"; }
};
class Derived : public Base {
public:
    void show() { cout << "Derived"; }
};
int main() {
    Base* b = new Derived();
    b->show();  // Output?
}

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: "Derived" - Virtual function call uses dynamic binding. Despite Base pointer, the actual object is Derived, so Derived::show() is called via vtable.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: Which of the following is TRUE about function overloading?

            
A) Functions must differ only in return type
B) Functions must have different parameter lists
C) Functions can have same parameters if return type differs
D) Functions must be in different scopes

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: B) Functions must have different parameter lists - Return type alone cannot distinguish overloaded functions.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2018: What is the size of an object of a class that has one virtual function and no data members?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 8 bytes (on 64-bit) / 4 bytes (on 32-bit) - The object contains only a vptr (virtual pointer) pointing to the vtable. No other data.
                

            

        

    






    
 Annotated Parse Tree · Attribute Propagation in SDT


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Synthesized
             Inherited
             val, type, code
             S-attributed
             L-attributed
        

    


    
    

        
 Active Attribute Grammar Rules

        

    


    
    

        
 Annotated Parse Tree

        
        

            

                 enter expression and click Build Annotated Tree
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Attribute Evaluation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Node
                        Attribute Rule
                        Attribute
                        Value
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Build tree to see attribute evaluation
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Symbol Table (with values & types)

        
        

            

                
                    

                        ID
                        Identifier
                        Type
                        Value
                        Line
                        Attributes
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             No identifiers declared
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Attribute Dependency Graph

        
        

            

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Annotated Parse Trees

        
        

            

                
📌 Synthesized Attributes (S-attributed)

                
Attributes computed from children to parent. Evaluated bottom-up.

                

                    E.val = E₁.val + T.val
                

                
Example: val, type, code

            


            

                
📌 Inherited Attributes (L-attributed)

                
Attributes passed from parent/siblings to children. Left-to-right flow.

                

                    L.in = T.type
                

                
Example: env, offset, in

            


            

                
📌 Type Promotion Rules

                
In mixed expressions, lower types promoted to higher:

                
    
                    
  • char → int → float → double
    • 
                    
  • int + float = float
    • 
                    
  • float * double = double
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Dependency Graph

                
Nodes = attribute instances. Edges = dependencies.

                
    
                    
  • Acyclic → evaluatable
    • 
                    
  • Cycle → circular dependency
    • 
                    
  • Topological order = evaluation order
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Practice Examples

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Arithmetic

                3 + 5 * 2
                
S-attributed: val = 13, type = int

            

            
            

                
Example 2: Mixed Types

                2.5 * 3 + 1.5
                
Type promotions: int→float→double

            

            
            

                
Example 3: Assignment

                x = y + 10
                
Lookup types from symbol table

            

            
            

                
Example 4: Parentheses

                a * (b + c)
                
Inherited attributes for scope

            

        

    






    
 Precedence & Associativity · Operator Hierarchy & Evaluation Order


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            

                
                
                
                
            

        


        
        

            
 Custom Precedence Levels

            
Higher number = higher precedence (evaluated first)

            
            

                

                

                

                

                

                

                

                

                

                

            

            
            

                
                
            

        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Higher Precedence
             Lower Precedence
             Left Associative
             Right Associative
             GATE Focus
        

    


    
    

        
 Operator Precedence & Associativity

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Precedence
                        Operator(s)
                        Description
                        Associativity
                        Example
                    

                
                
                    
                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Parse Tree (Precedence-Based)

        
        

            

                 Enter expression and click Analyze
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Evaluation Order (Step by Step)

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Operation
                        Reason
                        Result
                        Expression
                    

                
                
                    
Analyze to see evaluation steps

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: * / before + -

                a + b * c - d / e
                
Multiplication and division have higher precedence

            

            
            

                
Example 2: Assignment

                a = b = c + d * e
                
Right-associative assignment

            

            
            

                
Example 3: Logical & Relational

                a + b > c && d == e || f
                
Relational > logical AND > logical OR

            

            
            

                
Example 4: Mixed Operators

                !a && b || c == d + e * f
                
Unary > arithmetic > relational > logical

            

        

    






    
 Grammar Ambiguity Resolution · Dangling-Else & Operator Precedence


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Dangling-Else
             Operator Precedence
             Multiple Parse Trees
             Associativity
        

    


    
    

        
 Ambiguity Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
1

                
Grammar Rules

            

            

                
0

                
Possible Parse Trees

            

            

                
0

                
Ambiguity Conflicts

            

            

                
—

                
Resolution Method

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Grammar

        

            
E → E + E | E * E | ( E ) | id

        

    


    
    

        
 Ambiguity Type

        
        

            
            
            
        

    


    
    

        
 Multiple Parse Trees (Ambiguity Demonstration)

        
        

            
            

                

                     Parse Tree 1 (Left Associative)
                

                

                     Analyze to see first parse tree
                

            

            
            
            

                

                     Parse Tree 2 (Right Associative)
                

                

                     Analyze to see second parse tree
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Resolved Grammar (Unambiguous)

        
        

            
            

                
With Precedence

                
E → E + T | T
T → T * F | F
F → ( E ) | id

            

            
            
            

                
With Associativity

                
E → E + T | T
E → T + E | T

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Dangling-Else Ambiguity Resolution

        
        

            

                
Ambiguous Grammar

                
ifStmt → if E then S
        | if E then S else S

                
Problem: Which 'if' does the 'else' belong to?

            

            
            

                
Resolved Grammar

                
ifStmt → matched
        | unmatched
matched → if E then matched else matched
        | other
unmatched → if E then S
        | if E then matched else unmatched

                
Solution: Separate matched and unmatched statements

            

        

        
        

            
Rule: "Match else with nearest unmatched if"

            

                

                    if E1 then if E2 then S1 else S2
                    
❌ Ambiguous: Which if?

                

                
                

                    if E1 then [if E2 then S1 else S2]
                    
✅ Resolved: else binds to inner if

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Ambiguity Resolution Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Issue
                        Resolution Technique
                        Resulting Grammar
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see resolution steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Interactive Precedence & Associativity

        
        

            

                Operator: *
                

                    
                    
                    
                    
                

            

            
            

                
                
                

                    Lowest
                    5
                    Highest
                

            

            
            

                
                

                    
                    
                    
                

            

            
            

                
Effect on Expression: a + b * c

                

                    Adjust sliders to see effect
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions

        
        

            

                
GATE 2022: Consider the grammar: E → E + E | E * E | id. Which of the following is TRUE?

                
A) The grammar is unambiguous
B) The grammar is ambiguous due to operator precedence
C) The grammar is ambiguous due to associativity
D) The grammar is LL(1)

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: B & C - The grammar is ambiguous for both precedence (* vs +) and associativity (multiple +). Requires separate non-terminals for precedence and associativity rules.
                    

                

            

            
            

                
GATE 2021: For the dangling-else grammar, how is ambiguity resolved?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: By separating statements into "matched" and "unmatched" categories. The rule "match each else with the nearest unmatched if" resolves the ambiguity.
                    

                

            

            
            

                
GATE 2019: Which of the following grammars is unambiguous?

                
A) S → iSeS | iS | a
B) S → aS | Sa | a
C) E → E+E | E*E | id
D) E → E+T | T; T → id

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: D - This grammar has separate non-terminals for different precedence levels, making it unambiguous.
                    

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Expression Ambiguity

                E → E+E | E*E | id
                
Test: id + id * id → two parse trees

            

            
            

                
Example 2: Dangling-Else

                ifStmt → if E then S | if E then S else S
                
Test: if E1 then if E2 then S1 else S2

            

            
            

                
Example 3: Associativity

                E → E - E | id
                
Test: id - id - id → left vs right associative

            

        

    






    
 Type Conversions · Implicit & Explicit Casting Rules


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Implicit (Coercion)
             Explicit (Casting)
             Truncation
             Type Promotion
             GATE Focus
        

    


    
    

        
 Conversion Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Expressions Analyzed

            

            

                
0

                
Implicit Conversions

            

            

                
0

                
Explicit Casts

            

            

                
0

                
Truncations Detected

            

            

                
0

                
Type Promotions

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Type Promotion Hierarchy

        
        

            

                

                    
char (1 byte)

                    
                

                

                    
short (2 bytes)

                    
                

                

                    
int (4 bytes)

                    
                

                

                    
long (8 bytes)

                    
                

                

                    
float (4 bytes)

                    
                

                

                    
double (8 bytes)

                

            

            

                 Higher rank = wider range, promotion is automatic | Lower to higher = promotion, higher to lower = demotion (may lose precision)
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Type Conversion Analysis Results

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Expression
                        Source Type
                        Target Type
                        Conversion Type
                        Result / Status
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             enter expressions and click Analyze Conversions
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Step-by-Step Type Conversion Analysis

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Operation
                        Left/Arg Type
                        Right/Result Type
                        Conversion Rule
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             analyze to see step-by-step process
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Type Conversion Rules by Language

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Language
                        Implicit Conversions
                        Explicit Casts
                        Promotion Rules
                        GATE Key Points
                    

                
                
                    

                        C/C++
                        ✅ int → float, char → int, float → double
                        ✅ (int)3.14, (float)5
                        Lower rank → higher rank automatically
                        Truncation on float→int, UB on overflow
                    

                    

                        Java
                        ⚠️ Widening only (no implicit narrowing)
                        ✅ (int)3.14 (explicit required)
                        byte→short→int→long→float→double
                        No implicit narrowing, strict type safety
                    

                    

                        C#
                        ⚠️ Widening only, checked context
                        ✅ (int)3.14, Convert.ToInt32()
                        Similar to Java + checked/unchecked
                        checked keyword for overflow checking
                    

                    

                        Python
                        ✅ Dynamic typing, int→float automatic
                        ✅ int(3.14), float(5)
                        Arbitrary precision integers
                        No truncation, float→int truncates
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Type Conversion Rules & Important Concepts

        
        

            

                
📌 Rule 1: Promotion (Widening)

                
Automatic conversion from smaller to larger type:

                
    
                    
  • char → int (ASCII value)
    • 
                    
  • int → float (may lose precision)
    • 
                    
  • float → double (preserves value)
    • 
                

                

                    int a = 5; float b = a; // b = 5.0
                

            


            

                
📌 Rule 2: Demotion (Narrowing)

                
Explicit cast required in strict languages:

                
    
                    
  • float → int (truncation)
    • 
                    
  • double → float (rounding)
    • 
                    
  • long → int (overflow possible)
    • 
                

                

                    float f = 3.99; int i = (int)f; // i = 3
                

            


            

                
📌 Rule 3: Usual Arithmetic Conversions

                
In binary operations, types are promoted to common type:

                
    
                    
  • int + float → float
    • 
                    
  • float + double → double
    • 
                    
  • char + int → int
    • 
                

                

                    5 + 3.14 → 8.14 (double)
                

            


            

                
📌 Rule 4: Integer Promotions

                
Small integer types promoted to int before operations:

                
    
                    
  • char + char → int
    • 
                    
  • short + short → int
    • 
                

                

                    char c1 = 'A', c2 = 'B'; int sum = c1 + c2;
                

            

        


        
        

            
📊 Type Hierarchy & Conversion Ranking

            

                
                    

                        Rank
                        Type
                        Size (bytes)
                        Can be promoted to
                        Demotion allowed?
                    

                
                
                    
1char1short, int, long, float, double⚠️ Explicit only

                    
2short2int, long, float, double⚠️ Explicit only

                    
3int4long, float, double⚠️ Explicit only

                    
4long8float, double⚠️ Explicit only

                    
5float4double⚠️ Explicit only

                    
6double8—⚠️ Explicit only

                
            

            

                 Lower rank → higher rank = implicit promotion (safe)

                Higher rank → lower rank = explicit cast required (may lose data)
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Practice Examples

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Basic Conversions

                int + float → float
float → int (implicit vs explicit)
                
Shows implicit promotion and explicit casting

            

            


    
Example 2: Char Promotions

    char → int → float → double
    
ASCII value conversions



            

                
Example 3: Expression Conversions

                Integer vs float division
                
Operator precedence and type promotion

            

            
            

                
Example 4: Mixed Types

                short → int → float → double → long
                
Multiple promotions in one expression

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on Type Conversions

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Consider the following C code:

            
int a = 5;
float b = 3.14;
int c = a + b;
float d = a / b;
double e = a * b;

            
Which of the following statements is TRUE about implicit type conversions?

            
A) No conversions occur in any expression

            B) a + b converts a to float, then result to int

            C) a / b performs integer division

            D) a * b converts a to double

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: B - In C, int + float promotes int to float, then result is converted to int for assignment (demotion). This loses precision.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: What is the output of the following C code?

            
int main() {
    float x = 5 / 2;
    float y = 5.0 / 2;
    float z = (float)5 / 2;
    printf("%.1f %.1f %.1f", x, y, z);
    return 0;
}

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 2.0 2.5 2.5

                    x = 5/2 → integer division (2), then converted to float (2.0)

                    y = 5.0/2 → 5.0 is float, 2 promoted to float → 2.5

                    z = (float)5/2 → 5 explicitly cast to float, 2 promoted → 2.5
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2018: In Java, which of the following requires an explicit cast?

            
A) byte → int
B) int → long
C) float → double
D) double → float

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: D) double → float - Java allows implicit widening (lower to higher rank) but requires explicit cast for narrowing (higher to lower).
                

            

        

    






    
 Type Equivalence · Structural vs Name Equivalence with Semantic Rules


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Name Equivalence
             Structural Equivalence
             Semantic Rules
             GATE Focus
        

    


    
    

        
 Type Equivalence Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Type Definitions

            

            

                
0

                
Variable Declarations

            

            

                
0

                
Assignment Checks

            

            

                
0

                
Compatible Pairs

            

            

                
0

                
Incompatible Pairs

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Language Type System Mode

        
        

            

                
                
                
            

            

                 GATE: Know the difference!
            

        

        
        

            
Name Equivalence: Types are equivalent only if they have the same name

            
Structural Equivalence: Types are equivalent if they have the same structure

        

    


    
    

        
 Type Definitions & Structures

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Type Name
                        Category
                        Structure / Members
                        Size (bytes)
                        Type ID
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             enter code and click Analyze
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Variable Declarations with Types

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Variable
                        Declared Type
                        Type Structure
                        Line
                        Type ID / Signature
                    

                
                
                    

                        No variable declarations
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Type Compatibility Analysis

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Expression/Assignment
                        Left Type
                        Right Type
                        Equivalence Type
                        Result / Semantic Rule
                    

                
                
                    

                        Analyze to see type equivalence checks
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Grammar Productions with Semantic Rules for Type Equivalence

        
        

            
            

                

                     Name Equivalence Rules
                

                
TypeDecl → ID '=' Type  {
    enterType(ID.name, Type.type)
}

VarDecl → ID ':' Type    {
    if lookupType(Type.name) exists
        addSymbol(ID.name, Type.name)
    else
        error("Type not defined")
}

Assign → ID1 ':=' ID2   {
    t1 = getType(ID1.name)
    t2 = getType(ID2.name)
    if t1.name != t2.name
        error("Type mismatch")
}

            

            
            
            

                

                     Structural Equivalence Rules
                

                
StructDecl → 'struct' ID '{' FieldList '}' {
    type = createStructType()
    type.fields = FieldList.fields
    enterType(ID.name, type)
}

FieldList → FieldList Field  {
    $$.fields = $1.fields + [$2]
}
          | Field            {
    $$.fields = [$1]
}

Assign → ID1 ':=' ID2   {
    t1 = getType(ID1.name)
    t2 = getType(ID2.name)
    if not structurallyEqual(t1, t2)
        error("Structural mismatch")
}

            

        


        
        

            
📋 Type Equivalence Algorithm (Structural)

            
function structurallyEqual(type1, type2):
    if type1.kind != type2.kind:
        return false
    
    switch type1.kind:
        case 'primitive':
            return type1.name == type2.name
        case 'array':
            return type1.size == type2.size and 
                   structurallyEqual(type1.elementType, type2.elementType)
        case 'struct':
            if type1.fields.length != type2.fields.length:
                return false
            for i from 0 to type1.fields.length-1:
                if type1.fields[i].name != type2.fields[i].name:
                    return false
                if not structurallyEqual(type1.fields[i].type, type2.fields[i].type):
                    return false
            return true
        case 'pointer':
            return structurallyEqual(type1.pointee, type2.pointee)
        case 'function':
            if not structurallyEqual(type1.returnType, type2.returnType):
                return false
            if type1.paramTypes.length != type2.paramTypes.length:
                return false
            for i from 0 to type1.paramTypes.length-1:
                if not structurallyEqual(type1.paramTypes[i], type2.paramTypes[i]):
                    return false
            return true
        default:
            return false

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Type Equivalence

        
        

            

                
📌 Name Equivalence

                
Types are equivalent only if they have the same name.

                

                    type meters = integer;

type inches = integer;

var m : meters; i : inches;

m := i; // ERROR - different names
                

                
Used in: Pascal, Ada, Modula-2

            


            

                
📌 Structural Equivalence

                
Types are equivalent if they have the same structure (same components in same order).

                

                    struct Point { int x; int y; };

struct Coord { int x; int y; };

struct Point p; struct Coord c;

p = c; // OK - same structure
                

                
Used in: C, C++ (for structs), Algol 68

            


            

                
📌 Mixed Approaches

                
Different rules for different type constructors:

                
    
                    
  • Java: Classes use name equivalence, interfaces use structural for compatibility
    • 
                    
  • C#: Classes use name equivalence, structs use structural
    • 
                    
  • TypeScript: Primarily structural (duck typing)
    • 
                

            

        


        
        

            
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions on Type Equivalence

            
            

                
GATE 2021: Consider the following Pascal-like declarations:

                
type
    T1 = integer;
    T2 = integer;
var
    a : T1;
    b : T2;
    c : integer;

                
Under name equivalence, which of the following assignments are valid?

                
a) a := b   b) a := c   c) c := a

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: Only (c) c := a is valid. Under name equivalence, T1 and T2 are different types, 
                        so a and b are incompatible. But integer is the base type, so assignments between named types and 
                        their base type depend on language rules (usually allowed in Pascal).

                        GATE Point: Name equivalence says types with different names are different, even if 
                        they have the same underlying structure.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2019: Consider the C declarations:

                
struct S1 { int a; float b; };
struct S2 { int a; float b; };
struct S1 x;
struct S2 y;

                
Under structural equivalence, is the assignment x = y valid?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: No. C uses name equivalence for struct types. Even though the structures are 
                        identical, S1 and S2 are different types. Under pure structural equivalence, it would be valid, but 
                        C doesn't allow this. This is a common GATE trick!
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2017: In which phase of compiler is type equivalence checked?

                
A) Lexical Analysis B) Syntax Analysis C) Semantic Analysis D) Code Generation

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: C) Semantic Analysis - Type checking, including type equivalence, is performed 
                        during semantic analysis using the symbol table and type information.
                    

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Name Equivalence

                Pascal style type aliases
                
Age and Years: same underlying type, different names

            

            
            

                
Example 2: Structural Equivalence

                C structs with identical fields
                
Same structure, different names - C uses name equivalence

            

            
            

                
Example 3: Pointer Types

                typedef and pointers
                
Structural equivalence: same pointee type → compatible?

            

            
            

                
Example 4: Recursive Types

                Linked list node equivalence
                
Structurally identical recursive definitions

            

        

    






    
 Function Overload Resolution · Compile-Time Polymorphism


    

        

            
            

                
                
                
                
            

        


        

            
            
        


        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Exact Match
             Promotion
             Standard Conversion
             User-Defined
             Ambiguous/No Match
        

    


    
    

        
 Overload Resolution Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Function Overloads

            

            

                
0

                
Function Calls

            

            

                
0

                
Successfully Resolved

            

            

                
0

                
Ambiguous/Errors

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Function Overloads

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Function Name
                        Return Type
                        Parameter Types
                        Param Count
                        Signature
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             enter function definitions above
