            Click on any call in the table above to see detailed resolution steps
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Overload Resolution Rules

        
        

            

                
📌 Conversion Ranking (Best to Worst)

                
    
                    
  1. Exact Match - No conversion needed
    2. 
                    
  2. Promotion - char→int, float→double
    3. 
                    
  3. Standard Conversion - int→float, double→int
    4. 
                    
  4. User-defined Conversion - constructors, conversion ops
    5. 
                    
  5. Ellipsis (...) - variable arguments
    6. 
                

            


            

                
📌 Language-Specific Rules

                
C++: All of the above + templates

                
Java: Widening only, no implicit narrowing

                
Python: Dynamic typing - last definition wins

                
C: No function overloading (different names)

            


            

                
📌 Tie-Breakers

                
    
                    
  • Non-template vs template
    • 
                    
  • More specific parameter types
    • 
                    
  • Member vs non-member
    • 
                    
  • Ambiguous if multiple equally good
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Function Overloading Support by Language

        
        

            

                
✅ C++

                
Full support: return type, parameter count, parameter types, const/volatile

            

            

                
✅ Java

                
Yes, but return type alone not sufficient. Autoboxing, varargs

            

            

                
✅ C#

                
Similar to Java + named arguments, optional parameters

            

            

                
❌ C

                
No function overloading (use different names like max_i, max_f)

            

            

                
❌ Python

                
No traditional overloading (use default args, *args, or multiple dispatch libraries)

            

        

    






    
 Context-Free Grammar (CFG) Definition


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Terminals
             Non-terminals
             Productions
             Validation
        

    


    
    

        
 Grammar Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Productions

            

            

                
0

                
Non-terminals

            

            

                
0

                
Terminals

            

            

                
E

                
Start Symbol

            

        

    


    
    

        

            
            

                
 Productions

                

                    
                

            

            
            
            

                
 Symbol Sets

                

                    
Non-terminals:

                    

                        { E, T, F }
                    

                

                

                    
Terminals:

                    

                        { +, *, (, ), id }
                    

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Grammar Validation Results

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Context-Free Grammar Properties

        
        

            

                
📌 Definition

                
A Context-Free Grammar G is a 4-tuple G = (V, Σ, R, S) where:

                
    
                    
  • V: Finite set of non-terminals
    • 
                    
  • Σ: Finite set of terminals
    • 
                    
  • R: Finite set of production rules
    • 
                    
  • S: Start symbol (S ∈ V)
    • 
                

                

                    E → E + T | T
                

            


            

                
📌 Valid Production Rules

                
    
                    
  • Left side: Single non-terminal
    • 
                    
  • Right side: String of terminals and non-terminals
    • 
                    
  • ε (epsilon): Represents empty string
    • 
                    
  • Alternatives: A → α | β | γ
    • 
                

                

                    S → ε | aS | bS
                

            


            

                
📌 Grammar Types (Chomsky Hierarchy)

                
    
                    
  • Type 0: Unrestricted
    • 
                    
  • Type 1: Context-sensitive
    • 
                    
  • Type 2: Context-Free
    • 
                    
  • Type 3: Regular
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Grammar Examples

        
        

            

                
Expression Grammar

                E → E + T | T
T → T * F | F
F → ( E ) | id
            

            
            

                
If-Else Grammar

                S → if E then S
  | if E then S else S
  | other
            

            
            

                
Palindrome Grammar

                P → ε | 0 | 1
  | 0P0 | 1P1
            

        

    






    
 Memory Allocation · Static vs Dynamic


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Static Allocation
             Stack (Automatic)
             Heap (Dynamic)
             Lifetime Management
             GATE Focus
        

    


    
    

        
 Memory Allocation Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Allocations

            

            

                
0

                
Static Allocations

            

            

                
0

                
Stack (Auto) Allocations

            

            

                
0

                
Heap Allocations

            

            

                
0

                
Total Bytes

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Memory Layout Visualization

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Memory Allocation Analysis

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Variable
                        Allocation Type
                        Storage Class
                        Size (bytes)
                        Lifetime
                        Address Range
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see memory allocation details
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Three-Address Code for Memory Operations

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        
        

            
📊 Static vs Dynamic Memory Allocation Comparison

            
            

                

                    
                        

                            Feature
                            Static Allocation
                            Stack (Automatic)
                            Heap (Dynamic)
                        

                    
                    
                        
Allocation TimeCompile-timeRuntime (function entry)Runtime (explicit call)

                        
DeallocationProgram endFunction exitExplicit (free/delete)

                        
LifetimeEntire programFunction scopeControlled by programmer

                        
Memory LocationData/BSS segmentStackHeap

                        
Size FlexibilityFixed at compile-timeFixed at compile-timeDynamic at runtime

                        
SpeedFastestVery FastSlower (overhead)

                        
FragmentationNoneNonePossible

                        
ScopeFile/function scopeBlock scopePointer scope

                        
InitializationZero-initialized (BSS)Uninitialized (garbage)Uninitialized (malloc) / initialized (calloc)

                        
C/C++ Keywordsstatic, globallocal variablesmalloc, calloc, new

                    
                

            


            
            

                
 GATE Previous Year Questions

                
                

                    
GATE 2021: Which of the following memory allocation strategies is used for static variables?

                    
A) Stack allocation
B) Heap allocation
C) Data segment allocation
D) Register allocation

                    

                        Show Answer
                        

                            Answer: C) Data segment allocation - Static variables are stored in the data segment (initialized) or BSS (uninitialized) of the program's memory layout.
                        

                    

                


                

                    
GATE 2020: What is the output of the following C program?

                    
void func() {
    static int x = 0;
    int y = 0;
    x++; y++;
    printf("%d %d ", x, y);
}
int main() {
    func(); func(); func();
    return 0;
}

                    

                        Show Answer
                        

                            Answer: 1 1 2 1 3 1 - static x retains its value across function calls (allocated in data segment), while y is reinitialized each call (allocated on stack).
                        

                    

                


                

                    
GATE 2019: Which of the following is NOT an advantage of dynamic memory allocation?

                    
A) Flexible memory usage
B) No memory fragmentation
C) Allocation at runtime
D) Can create data structures of variable size

                    

                        Show Answer
                        

                            Answer: B) No memory fragmentation - Dynamic allocation can lead to memory fragmentation, which is a disadvantage.
                        

                    

                

            

        


        
        

            
📦 Static Allocation Details

            
            

                

                    
Static Storage Classes

                    
    
                        
  • Global variables - Entire program lifetime
    • 
                        
  • Static global - File scope only
    • 
                        
  • Static local - Function scope, retains value
    • 
                        
  • Static member - Shared across class instances
    • 
                        
  • String literals - Stored in read-only data
    • 
                    

                

                
                

                    
Memory Segments

                    
Text/Code:   Executable code
Data:        Initialized static data
BSS:         Uninitialized static data
Heap:        Dynamic allocations
Stack:       Local variables

                

            


            

                
TAC for Static Variables

                
// Static global variable
global_counter: .word 0      // Allocated in data segment

// Static local variable (function-level)
func:
    // static int count = 0;
    // Compiler allocates in data/BSS, not on stack
    // Access via fixed address
    load t1, &func.count    // Load static address
    add t1, t1, 1           // Increment
    store &func.count, t1   // Store back

            

        


        
        

            
📚 Stack (Automatic) Allocation

            
            

                

                    
Stack Frame Layout

                    
[High Address]
    Arguments
    Return Address
    Old Frame Pointer
    Local Variables
    Temporaries
[Low Address] (SP points here)

                

                
                

                    
Characteristics

                    
    
                        
  • ✅ LIFO (Last In First Out)
    • 
                        
  • ✅ Automatic allocation/deallocation
    • 
                        
  • ✅ Very fast (just SP adjustment)
    • 
                        
  • ✅ No fragmentation
    • 
                        
  • ❌ Limited size
    • 
                        
  • ❌ No resizing after allocation
    • 
                    

                

            


            

                
TAC for Stack Variables

                
// Function prologue
func:
    push FP           // Save old frame pointer
    FP = SP           // Set new frame pointer
    SP = SP - 16      // Allocate 16 bytes for locals

// Access local variable at offset -8 from FP
    t1 = FP - 8       // Address of local var
    *t1 = 42          // Store value

// Function epilogue
    SP = FP           // Deallocate locals
    pop FP            // Restore old frame pointer
    return

            

        


        
        

            
🗑️ Heap (Dynamic) Allocation

            
            

                

                    
Allocation Functions

                    

                        
malloc(size)Allocates uninitialized memory

                        
calloc(n, size)Allocates zero-initialized array

                        
realloc(ptr, size)Resizes existing allocation

                        
new typeC++ single object

                        
new type[n]C++ array

                    

                

                
                

                    
Characteristics

                    
    
                        
  • ✅ Flexible size at runtime
    • 
                        
  • ✅ Can be resized (realloc)
    • 
                        
  • ✅ Lifetime controlled by programmer
    • 
                        
  • ❌ Slower allocation
    • 
                        
  • ❌ Memory leaks possible
    • 
                        
  • ❌ Fragmentation issues
    • 
                    

                

            


            

                
TAC for Heap Operations

                
// int* p = (int*)malloc(sizeof(int));
    t1 = 4                    // size = 4 bytes
    param t1                  // push size parameter
    call malloc               // call malloc
    p = return_val            // store returned address

// *p = 42;
    *p = 42                   // store value at address

// p = (int*)realloc(p, 2*sizeof(int));
    t2 = 8                    // new size = 8 bytes
    param p                   // push old pointer
    param t2                  // push new size
    call realloc              // call realloc
    p = return_val            // update pointer

// free(p);
    param p                   // push pointer
    call free                 // call free

            

        


        
        

            
🔒 Smart Pointers (C++11/14)

            
            

                

                    
std::unique_ptr

                    
Exclusive ownership, cannot be copied

                    
std::unique_ptr p1(new int(42));
// auto p2 = p1;  // Error: can't copy
auto p2 = std::move(p1);  // OK: transfer ownership

                

                
                

                    
std::shared_ptr

                    
Shared ownership, reference counted

                    
auto p1 = std::make_shared(42);
auto p2 = p1;  // Reference count = 2
// Memory freed when last shared_ptr dies

                

                
                

                    
std::weak_ptr

                    
Non-owning observer, breaks cycles

                    
std::weak_ptr wp = p1;
if (auto sp = wp.lock()) {
    // Use sp safely
}

                

            


            

                
Benefits over raw pointers

                
    
                    
  • ✅ Automatic memory management
    • 
                    
  • ✅ Exception safety
    • 
                    
  • ✅ Prevents memory leaks
    • 
                    
  • ✅ Clear ownership semantics
    • 
                    
  • ✅ No manual delete calls
    • 
                

            

        

    






    
 Medium-Level Intermediate Language · Processor-Independent IR


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             MIL
             Processor-Independent
             Machine-Independent Optimizations
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 MIL Generation Summary

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total MIL Statements

            

            

                
0

                
Arithmetic Operations

            

            

                
0

                
Control Flow

            

            

                
0

                
Temporary Variables

            

            

                
0

                
Basic Blocks

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Medium-Level IL (MIL) Output

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Basic Blocks in MIL

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 MIL Control Flow Graph

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 MIL Generation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Original Construct
                        MIL Generated
                        Optimization Applied
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Generate MIL to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 MIL vs TAC Comparison

        
        

            

                
Medium-Level IL (MIL)

                

            

            
            

                
Three-Address Code (TAC)

                

            

        

        
        

            

                
                GATE Insight: MIL is higher-level than TAC, preserving more structure for machine-independent optimizations.
                MIL uses operations like FOR_LOOP, IF_ELSE, while TAC breaks everything into simple three-address instructions.
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: MIL Properties & Characteristics

        
        

            

                
📌 Machine Independence

                
MIL is not tied to any specific processor architecture:

                
    
                    
  • No assumption about register count
    • 
                    
  • Abstract operations (e.g., ADD vs architecture-specific)
    • 
                    
  • Virtual registers/infinite temporaries
    • 
                    
  • Abstract addressing modes
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Level of Abstraction

                
    
                    
  • Preserves loop structures
    • 
                    
  • Maintains if-else constructs
    • 
                    
  • Keeps array access expressions
    • 
                    
  • Retains function calls as operations
    • 
                

                

                    FOR_LOOP(i,0,10,1) { body }
                

            


            

                
📌 Optimizations Possible

                
    
                    
  • Constant propagation
    • 
                    
  • Copy propagation
    • 
                    
  • Dead code elimination
    • 
                    
  • Loop invariant code motion
    • 
                    
  • Common subexpression elimination
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 MIL Representations

                
    
                    
  • Abstract Syntax Trees - for high-level
    • 
                    
  • Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAG) - for optimization
    • 
                    
  • Three-Address Code - for low-level
    • 
                    
  • Control Flow Graphs - for analysis
    • 
                

            

        


        

            
📊 IR Level Comparison

            

                
                    

                        Level
                        Example
                        Machine Independent?
                        Optimization Level
                    

                
                
                    

                        HIR (High-Level)
                        while (i < 10) { sum += i; }
                        ✅ Yes
                        Low (structure preserved)
                    

                    

                        MIL (Medium-Level)
                        LOOP_START L1; i < 10; L2
                        ✅ Yes
                        Medium (loop opts possible)
                    

                    

                        LIR (Low-Level)
                        t1 = i * 4; t2 = base + t1; load t3, t2
                        ⚠️ Partially
                        High (peephole, register alloc)
                    

                    

                        TAC/Quadruples
                        t1 = i + 1; i = t1;
                        ✅ Yes
                        Medium
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on MIL

        
        

            
GATE 2022: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of Medium-Level Intermediate Language (MIL)?

            
A) Machine independent
B) Preserves loop structures
C) Uses architecture-specific registers
D) Enables machine-independent optimizations

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: C) Uses architecture-specific registers - MIL is processor-independent and uses virtual registers, not architecture-specific ones.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2020: Arrange the following intermediate representations in increasing order of abstraction level (lowest to highest):

            
1. TAC  2. MIL  3. AST  4. DAG

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 1 → 4 → 2 → 3 (TAC → DAG → MIL → AST) 
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: Which optimization is typically performed at the MIL level?

            
A) Register allocation
B) Instruction scheduling
C) Loop unrolling
D) Peephole optimization

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: C) Loop unrolling - Loop optimizations are typically performed at MIL level where loop structure is preserved.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2017: In a compiler, the intermediate code that retains control flow structures like loops and conditionals is called:

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: Medium-Level Intermediate Language (MIL) or High-Level IR (HIR)
                

            

        

    






    
 Low-Level Intermediate Language · Processor-Specific Register-Based IR


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             x86-64
             ARM64
             MIPS
             RISC-V
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 LIL Generation Summary

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total LIL Instructions

            

            

                
0

                
Registers Used

            

            

                
0

                
Memory Operations

            

            

                
0

                
Control Flow

            

            

                
0

                
Register Spills

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Target Architecture

        
        

            
            
            
            
            
        

        
        

            
            
        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Low-Level IL (LIL) Output

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Register Allocation

        
        

            

                
Register Mapping

                

                    
                

            

            
            

                
Available Registers

                

                    
RAX - accumulator

                    
RBX - base

                    
RCX - counter

                    
RDX - data

                    
RSI - source index

                    
RDI - destination index

                    
R8-R15 - general purpose

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 LIL Generation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Original Construct
                        LIL Generated
                        Optimization
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Generate LIL to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Architecture Comparison

        
        

            

                
x86-64

                

            

            

                
ARM64

                

            

            

                
RISC-V

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Low-Level Intermediate Language (LIL)

        
        

            

                
📌 Register Allocation

                
Mapping virtual registers to physical registers:

                
    
                    
  • Local register allocation
    • 
                    
  • Global register allocation (graph coloring)
    • 
                    
  • Register spilling to memory
    • 
                

                

                    t1 → RAX, t2 → RBX
                

            


            

                
📌 Instruction Selection

                
Choosing target machine instructions:

                
    
                    
  • Pattern matching (tree rewriting)
    • 
                    
  • Maximal munch algorithm
    • 
                    
  • Dynamic programming
    • 
                

                

                    add t1, t2 → ADD RAX, RBX
                

            


            

                
📌 Peephole Optimization

                
Local instruction patterns:

                
    
                    
  • MOV followed by MOV (remove redundant)
    • 
                    
  • PUSH followed by POP (eliminate)
    • 
                    
  • JMP to JMP (simplify)
    • 
                

                

                    MOV RAX, RBX; MOV RBX, RAX → MOV RAX, RBX
                

            

        

    






    
 Backpatching · Filling Jump Addresses in Multi-Pass Compilation


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Temporary Labels
             Forward References
             Backpatch List
             Multi-Pass
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Backpatching Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Quadruples

            

            

                
0

                
Temporary Labels

            

            

                
0

                
Forward References

            

            

                
0

                
Successfully Patched

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Quadruples with Temporary Labels (Before Backpatching)

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Operator
                        Arg1
                        Arg2
                        Result (Temp)
                        Has Forward Ref
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Generate to see temporary labels
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Backpatch Lists (Pending Forward References)

        
        

            
            

                 Generate to see backpatch lists
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Quadruples After Backpatching (Final Code)

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Operator
                        Arg1
                        Arg2
                        Result
                        Patched
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Generate to see final code
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Backpatching Process Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Control Structure
                        Temporary Label
                        Quadruple(s) to Patch
                        Final Target
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Generate to see backpatching steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Control Flow Graph with Patch Points

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Backpatching Algorithm

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Backpatching?

                
Backpatching is a technique to handle forward jumps in one-pass compilers:

                
    
                    
  • Generate code with temporary labels for unknown targets
    • 
                    
  • Maintain backpatch lists of quadruples that need updating
    • 
                    
  • When target is known, patch all pending quadruples
    • 
                

                

                    if x > y goto ?  →  later replace ? with actual address
                

            


            

                
📌 Backpatching Lists

                
Each label maintains a list of quadruples that jump to it:

                
Label L1: [4, 7, 12]  // Quads 4,7,12 jump to L1
Label L2: [5, 9]       // Quads 5,9 jump to L2
Label L3: [3]          // Quad 3 jumps to L3

            


            

                
📌 Quadruple Format

                

                    
OperatorArg1Arg2Result

                    
if_falsex > y-L1

                    
goto--L2

                    
labelL1--

                

            

        


        

            
📊 Backpatching Algorithm Pseudocode

            
// Global data structures
quadruples: array of (op, arg1, arg2, result)
backpatch: Map[label -> list of quadruple indices]

// For conditional jump (if x > y goto L)
function emitIf(condition, trueLabel):
    quadruples.push(('if_false', condition, '-', trueLabel))
    backpatch.getOrCreate(trueLabel).push(quadruples.length - 1)

// For unconditional jump (goto L)
function emitGoto(label):
    quadruples.push(('goto', '-', '-', label))
    backpatch.getOrCreate(label).push(quadruples.length - 1)

// For label definition L:
function defineLabel(label):
    // Patch all pending jumps to this label
    for quadIndex in backpatch.get(label):
        quadruples[quadIndex].result = label
    backpatch.delete(label)
    
    // Emit label marker
    quadruples.push(('label', label, '-', '-'))

        

    


    
    

        
 Common Backpatching Patterns

        
        

            

                
if-then-else

                
(if_false, x>y, -, L1)
(assign, -, -, max=x)
(goto, -, -, L2)
(L1:)
(assign, -, -, max=y)
(L2:)

                
Backpatch: L1 and L2 patched when defined

            

            
            

                
while loop

                
(L1:)
(if_false, i<10, -, L2)
(assign, -, -, sum=sum+i)
(assign, -, -, i=i+1)
(goto, -, -, L1)
(L2:)

                
Backpatch: L2 patched after loop

            

            
            

                
nested if-else

                
(if_false, a>b, -, L1)
(assign, -, -, result=a)
(goto, -, -, L4)
(L1:)
(if_false, b>c, -, L2)
(assign, -, -, result=b)
(goto, -, -, L3)
(L2:)
(assign, -, -, result=c)
(L3:)
(L4:)

                
Multiple labels: L1, L2, L3, L4 need patching

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on Backpatching

        
        

            
GATE 2022: Consider the following code:

            
if (x > y) {
    a = b + c;
} else {
    a = b - c;
}

            
The backpatch list for label L1 (else block) contains which quadruple indices?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: The conditional jump at the start (if_false) has L1 as its target. 
                    When L1 is defined (else block start), that quadruple's result field is patched to point to the else block.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2021: In a one-pass compiler using backpatching, what is the maximum number of passes needed to resolve all forward jumps?

            
A) 1 pass B) 2 passes C) Number of labels D) Depends on nesting depth

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: B) 2 passes - First pass generates code with temporary labels and backpatch lists. Second pass patches all forward references. This is why it's called "one-pass with backpatching" - it appears one-pass but actually has a second patching phase.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2020: For the statement while (E) S, the backpatch list for the exit label contains:

            
A) Only the conditional jump
B) The conditional jump and the loop body
C) All jumps to exit
D) No jumps until S is processed

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: A) Only the conditional jump - The conditional jump (if_false) is the only jump that targets the exit label. The backpatch list for the exit label contains just that one quadruple.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: In backpatching, what does the `makelist(i)` function do?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: `makelist(i)` creates a new list containing only the quadruple index `i`. This is used when first encountering a jump to an undefined label. The list is later merged with other lists that jump to the same label using `merge(p1, p2)`.
                

            

        

    






    
 Control Flow Graph · Beautiful Visualization of Program Flow


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
            
        

    


    
    

        

             Control Flow Graph Visualization
            
                Basic Blocks
                Edges
                ← Scroll →
            
        

        
        
        

            
            

            
            
            

                
                
            

            
            
            

                 Drag to scroll
                |
                 Click & drag
            

            
            
            

                

                    

                    Entry Block
                

                

                    

                    Exit Block
                

                

                    

                    Basic Block

            Navigation:
            Use scroll bars or drag the canvas to explore the entire graph. The canvas size is 2000×1200 pixels for large graphs.
        

    


    
    

        
 Basic Blocks

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Block ID
                        Instructions
                        Successors
                        Predecessors
                    

                
                
                    

                        Generate CFG to see blocks
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        

             GATE Insight: Control Flow Graph
        

        
A Control Flow Graph (CFG) is a graphical representation of all paths that might be traversed through a program during its execution. Each node represents a basic block (straight-line sequence of statements with no jumps in/out). Edges represent control flow between blocks.

        
        

            

                📌 Dominators
                
Node d dominates node n if every path from entry to n goes through d.

            

            

                📌 Natural Loops
                
A cycle with a single entry point (header) and a back edge.

            

            

                📌 Critical Edges
                
Edges from a node with multiple successors to a node with multiple predecessors.

            

        

    






    
 Constant Folding · Compute Constant Expressions at Compile Time


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Constant Folding
             Compile-Time Evaluation
             Optimization
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Constant Folding (Optimized)

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Constant Folding Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Original Expression
                        Folded Result
                        Optimization Applied
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Fold constants to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Parse Tree Visualization

        
        

            
            

                

                     Before Optimization
                

                

                    Select expression to see tree
                

            

            
            
            

                

                     After Constant Folding
                

                

                    Folded expression will appear here
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Constant Folding

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Constant Folding?

                
Constant folding is a compiler optimization where expressions consisting of constants are evaluated at compile time rather than at runtime.

                

                    a = 3 * 4 + 5 → a = 12 + 5 → a = 17
                

            


            

                
📌 Benefits

                
    
                    
  • Faster execution: No runtime computation
    • 
                    
  • Smaller code: Removes redundant operations
    • 
                    
  • Enables further optimizations: Constant propagation
    • 
                    
  • Better instruction scheduling
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Folding Rules

                
    
                    
  • c1 + c2 = c3 (constant addition)
    • 
                    
  • c1 * c2 = c3 (constant multiplication)
    • 
                    
  • c1 - c2 = c3 (constant subtraction)
    • 
                    
  • c1 / c2 = c3 (constant division)
    • 
                

            

        

    






    
 Constant Propagation · Replacing Variables with Constant Values


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Constant Propagation
             Copy Propagation
             Compile-Time Evaluation
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Constant Propagation

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Before vs After Comparison

        
        

            

                

                     Before Optimization
                

                

                    
                

            

            
            

                

                     After Constant Propagation
                

                

                    
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Constant Propagation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Original Statement
                        Optimized Statement
                        Propagation Rule
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Propagate constants to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Constant Value Table

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Variable
                        Constant Value
                        Defined At
                        Used In
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Propagate to see constant table
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Control Flow Graph with Constants

        
        

            

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Constant Propagation

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Constant Propagation?

                
Constant propagation is a compiler optimization where the actual values of constants are propagated forward through the program, replacing variable references with their constant values.

                

                    int a = 5; int b = a + 10; → int a = 5; int b = 5 + 10;
                

            


            

                
📌 Benefits

                
    
                    
  • Faster execution: Values known at compile time
    • 
                    
  • Enables constant folding: 5 + 10 → 15
    • 
                    
  • Dead code elimination: Unused branches removed
    • 
                    
  • Reduced register pressure
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 vs Copy Propagation

                
Constant Propagation: x = 5; y = x + 2 → y = 5 + 2

                
Copy Propagation: x = y; z = x + 2 → z = y + 2

            

        


        
        

            
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions on Constant Propagation

            
            

                
GATE 2020: Consider the following code:

                
int a = 5;
int b = a * 2;
int c = b + a;
int d = c - 3;

                
After constant propagation and folding, what is the value of d?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: 12

                        Step 1: b = 5 * 2 = 10

                        Step 2: c = 10 + 5 = 15

                        Step 3: d = 15 - 3 = 12
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2018: Which optimization is performed in the following transformation?

                
x = 5; y = x + 3; z = y * 2; → x = 5; y = 8; z = 16;

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: Constant propagation followed by constant folding
                    

                

            

        

    






    
 Copy Propagation · Replacing Variables with Their Assigned Copies


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Copy Propagation
             Variable Substitution
             Compile-Time Optimization
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Copy Propagation Analysis

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Variables

            

            

                
0

                
Copy Assignments

            

            

                
0

                
Replacements Made

            

            

                
0

                
Optimizations

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Copy Propagation

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Copy Assignments Analysis

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Copy Assignment
                        Source Variable
                        Target Variable
                        Used In Lines
                        Status / Propagation
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see copy assignments
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Propagation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Original Statement
                        Propagation Applied
                        Optimized Statement
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see propagation steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Variable Flow Graph

        
        

            

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Copy Propagation

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Copy Propagation?

                
Copy propagation replaces occurrences of a variable that has been assigned a copy of another variable (directly or indirectly) with the original variable.

                

                    int a = 5;
int b = a;   // copy assignment
int c = b + 3; → int c = a + 3;
                

            


            

                
📌 Benefits

                
    
                    
  • Enables constant folding: After propagation, more constants appear
    • 
                    
  • Reduces register pressure: Fewer variables need registers
    • 
                    
  • Dead code elimination: Unused copies can be removed
    • 
                    
  • Simplifies data flow analysis
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Limitations

                
    
                    
  • Cannot propagate across variable redefinitions
    • 
                    
  • Complex control flow may block propagation
    • 
                    
  • Phi functions in SSA form needed for complex cases
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 vs Constant Propagation

                
Constant Propagation: x = 5; y = x + 2 → y = 5 + 2

                
Copy Propagation: x = y; z = x + 2 → z = y + 2

            

        


        
        

            
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions on Copy Propagation

            
            

                
GATE 2020: Consider the following code:

                
a = b;
c = a + d;
e = a - f;
g = c + e;

                
After copy propagation, which variable is eliminated?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: Variable 'a' is eliminated. The code becomes:

                        c = b + d;

                        e = b - f;

                        g = c + e;
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2018: What is the output after copy propagation?

                
x = 5;
y = x;
z = y + 2;
y = 10;
w = y + z;

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: z = 5 + 2 (z = 7), w = 10 + 7 (w = 17). Note that y is redefined, so only the first occurrence of y (in z) is propagated.
                    

                

            

        

    






    
 Dead Code Elimination · Removing Unused Computations


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Dead Code
             Live Code
             Live Variable Analysis
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Dead Code Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Statements

            

            

                
0

                
Live Statements

            

            

                
0

                
Dead Statements

            

            

                
0

                
Statements Removed

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Live Variable Analysis

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Line
                        Statement
                        Defined Variables
                        Used Variables
                        Live Variables (after)
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see live variable analysis
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Dead Code Detection Results

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Line
                        Statement
                        Dead Code Type
                        Reason
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see dead code detection
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Optimized Code (After Dead Code Elimination)

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Control Flow Graph (Dead Code Highlighted)

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Dead Code Elimination

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Dead Code?

                
Dead code refers to statements that compute values which are never used anywhere in the program. Types of dead code:

                
    
                    
  • Unused variables: Variables defined but never referenced
    • 
                    
  • Unreachable code: Code that can never be executed (after return, unreachable branches)
    • 
                    
  • Dead assignments: Values assigned but overwritten before use
    • 
                    
  • Uncalled functions: Functions that are never invoked
    • 
                

                

                    int x = 5;    // DEAD if x never used
int y = x + 3; // LIVE if y is used later
                

            


            

                
📌 Live Variable Analysis

                
Live Variable Analysis is a data-flow analysis that determines for each program point which variables are "live" (will be used in the future).

                

                    Algorithm: Liveness = use[n] ∪ (out[n] - def[n])
                

                
• in[n]: variables live at entry of node n

                • out[n]: variables live at exit of node n

                • def[n]: variables defined at node n

                • use[n]: variables used at node n

            


            

                
📌 Types of Dead Code

                
    
                    
  • Type 1: Assignments to unused variables
    • 
                    
  • Type 2: Code after unconditional jumps (return, break, goto)
    • 
                    
  • Type 3: If-else branches that never execute
    • 
                    
  • Type 4: Functions that are never called
    • 
                    
  • Type 5: Redundant assignments (x = x)
    • 
                

            

        


        
        

            
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions on Dead Code Elimination

            
            

                
GATE 2022: Consider the following code snippet:

                
int a = 5;
int b = a + 3;
int c = b * 2;
int d = c - 10;
int e = d + 5;
int f = a + e;
printf("%d", f);

                
After dead code elimination, how many statements remain?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: All 6 statements are live because each value is used in subsequent computations, and f is printed.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2021: In compiler design, which analysis is used to detect dead code?

                
A) Constant propagation analysis
B) Live variable analysis
C) Reaching definitions analysis
D) Available expressions analysis

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: B) Live variable analysis - Dead code elimination uses liveness information to determine which assignments produce values that are never used.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2019: Consider the code:

                
x = 10;
y = 20;
if (x > y) {
    z = x + y;
} else {
    w = x - y;
}
printf("%d", w);

                
What statements are dead code?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: The then-block (z = x + y) is dead code because it's never executed (x > y is false for x=10, y=20). Also, x and y assignments are live as they're used in condition.
                    

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Practice Examples

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Simple Dead Code

                int a=5, b=10, c=a+b, d=c*2, e=100; return c;
                
d and e are dead because they're never used

            

            
            

                
Example 2: Unreachable Code

                Code after return and unused assignments
                
Statements after return are unreachable

            

            
            

                
Example 3: Conditional Dead Code

                Code in never-executed branches
                
Then-branch is dead when condition always false

            

            
            

                
Example 4: Overwritten Variables

                Values overwritten before use
                
First assignment to t2 is dead (overwritten)

            

        

    






    
 Common Subexpression Elimination · Detecting & Removing Redundant Computations


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Common Subexpressions
             Temporary Variables
             Code Optimization
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 CSE Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Statements

            

            

                
0

                
Common Expressions

            

            

                
0

                
Redundant Computations

            

            

                
0

                
Optimization Savings

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Common Subexpressions Detected

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Expression
                        Occurrences
                        Lines Found
                        Temp Variable
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Perform CSE to detect common expressions
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Optimized Code (After CSE)

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 CSE Transformation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Original Expression
                        Replaced With
                        Temp Variable
                        Savings
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Perform CSE to see transformation steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Expression DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)

        
        

            

                
📌 What is CSE?

                
CSE is an optimization technique that identifies expressions that are computed multiple times and computes them only once, storing the result in a temporary variable for reuse.

                

                    Before: a = b * c + d; e = b * c + f;

                    After:  t = b * c; a = t + d; e = t + f;
                

            


            

                
📌 Benefits

                
    
                    
  • Faster execution: Reduces number of computations
    • 
                    
  • Smaller code: Less redundant operations
    • 
                    
  • Register allocation: Better utilization
    • 
                    
  • Power reduction: Fewer CPU cycles
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Implementation Techniques

                
    
                    
  • Value Numbering: Assign IDs to expressions
    • 
                    
  • DAG Construction: Build expression graph
    • 
                    
  • Available Expressions: Data flow analysis
    • 
                    
  • Local vs Global: Basic block vs entire function
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 GATE Key Points

                
    
                    
  • CSE is a machine-independent optimization
    • 
                    
  • Works best with three-address code
    • 
                    
  • Can be applied locally or globally
    • 
                    
  • Often combined with copy propagation
    • 
                

            

        


        
        

            
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions on CSE

            
            

                
GATE 2022: Consider the code: a = b * c + d; e = b * c + f; After CSE, how many temporary variables are introduced?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: 1 (t = b * c)
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2021: Which data structure is most commonly used for implementing CSE?

                
A) Hash Table B) Binary Tree C) Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) D) Stack

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: C) DAG - DAG helps identify common subexpressions by sharing nodes.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2019: Is CSE a machine-dependent or machine-independent optimization?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: Machine-independent - It works on intermediate code without knowledge of target machine.
                    

                

            

        

    






    
 Loop Invariant Code Motion · Moving Invariant Computations Outside Loops


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Loop Invariant
             Code Motion
             Optimization
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Invariant Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Loops Detected

            

            

                
0

                
Invariant Expressions

            

            

                
0

                
Moved Outside Loop

            

            

                
0

                
Computations Saved

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Loop Invariant Code Motion

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Detection & Invariant Analysis

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Loop Type
                        Loop Header
                        Invariant Expressions
                        Optimization Applied
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see loop invariants
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Invariant Expressions Identified

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Expression
                        Loop Depth
                        Moved To
                        Reason / Dependency
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see invariant expressions
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Invariant Code Motion Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Loop
                        Original Statement
                        Optimization Applied
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see optimization steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Structure Visualization

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Loop Invariant Code Motion

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Loop Invariant?

                
An expression is loop invariant if its value does not change during loop execution. Conditions for invariance:

                
    
                    
  • All operands are constants
    • 
                    
  • All operands are defined outside the loop
    • 
                    
  • Operands are not modified inside the loop
    • 
                

                

                    for(i=0; i<100; i++) { x = a * b + i; }

                    → a*b is invariant, moved before loop
                

            


            

                
📌 Code Motion Safety Conditions

                
To safely move code out of loop, check:

                
    
                    
  • Safety: Moving doesn't change program behavior
    • 
                    
  • Profitability: Expression computed at least once
    • 
                    
  • Dominance: Pre-header dominates all loop exits
    • 
                

                

                    t = a * b;  // moved outside
for(...) { x = t + i; }
                

            


            

                
📌 Benefits & Trade-offs

                
Benefits:

                
    
                    
  • ✓ Reduces computation count (n to 1)
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Improves execution speed
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Enables further optimizations
    • 
                

                
Trade-offs:

                
    
                    
  • ⚠️ Increases register pressure
    • 
                    
  • ⚠️ May increase code size
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 GATE Key Points

                
    
                    
  • LICM is a loop optimization technique
    • 
                    
  • Works on both for and while loops
    • 
                    
  • Requires data flow analysis
    • 
                    
  • Often combined with induction variables
    • 
                

            

        


        
        

            
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions on Loop Invariant Code Motion

            
            

                
GATE 2022: Consider the following code:

                
for (i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
    x = a * b;
    y = x + i;
    z = y + c;
}

                
How many loop invariant expressions can be moved outside the loop?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: 1 - Only "a * b" is loop invariant. "x + i" uses i (changes), 
                        "y + c" uses y which depends on x which is invariant? Actually y depends on x, so after moving x outside, 
                        y becomes invariant too! So ultimately both x and y computations can be moved.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2021: Which of the following is NOT a condition for loop invariant code motion?

                
A) Expression must be computed in every iteration

                B) Operands must not change inside loop

                C) Moving code must not affect program semantics

                D) Expression must use loop induction variable

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: D - Using induction variable actually prevents invariance. 
                        Invariant expressions do NOT use loop variables.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2019: Consider the loop:

                
while (i < 100) {
    t1 = 5 * 6;
    t2 = t1 + i;
    t3 = t2 * 2;
    i = i + 1;
}

                
After applying loop invariant code motion, how many statements are moved?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: 1 - Only t1 = 5 * 6 (constant 30) is loop invariant. 
                        t2 uses i, t3 uses t2 which depends on i.
                    

                

            

        

    






    
 Strength Reduction · Replace Expensive Operations with Cheaper Equivalents


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             ×2 → +
             ×4 → << 2
             /2 → >> 1
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Strength Reduction Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

0

                
Reduced Operations

            

            

                
0

                
CPU Cycles Saved

            

            

                
0

                
Multiplications Eliminated

 After Strength Reduction

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Strength Reduction Rules Applied

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Original Expression
                        Strength Reduced Form
                        Rule Applied
                        Cycle Savings
                        Description
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Apply strength reduction to see rules
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Strength Reduction Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Original Statement
                        Transformed Statement
                        Cycle Reduction
                        Rule
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Apply strength reduction to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Induction Variable Analysis (Loop Optimization)

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Performance Comparison: Before vs After

        
        

            

                

                    

                

                
Before

                
0 cycles

            

            

                

                    

                

                
After

                
0 cycles

            

            

                

                    

                

                
Savings

                
0 cycles

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Strength Reduction Rules

        
        

            

                
📌 Multiplication by Constant

                

                    
x * 2→x + x (1 cycle)

                    
x * 4→x << 2 (1 cycle)

                    
x * 8→x << 3 (1 cycle)

                    
x * 16→x << 4 (1 cycle)

                

            


            

                
📌 Division by Constant

                

                    
x / 2→x >> 1 (1 cycle)

                    
x / 4→x >> 2 (1 cycle)

                    
x / 8→x >> 3 (1 cycle)

                    
x / 16→x >> 4 (1 cycle)

                

            


            

                
📌 Induction Variables

                
Original: for i = 0 to N: y = i * 2

                
Reduced: t = 0; for i = 0 to N: y = t; t = t + 2

                
Eliminates multiplication per iteration

            


            

                
📌 CPU Cycle Comparison

                
Multiplication: 3-5 cycles

                
Addition: 1 cycle

                
Shift: 1 cycle

                
Savings: 2-4 cycles per operation

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on Strength Reduction

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Consider the loop: for (i=0; i<100; i++) { x = i * 4 + 5; } After strength reduction and induction variable optimization, what is the transformed code?

            

                Show Answer
                
t = 5;
for (i=0; i<100; i++) {
    x = t;
    t = t + 4;
}

                
Savings: 100 multiplications eliminated

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: Which of the following is an example of strength reduction?

            
A) x = y * 2 → x = y + y

            B) x = y + 2 → x = y + 2

            C) x = y * z → x = y * z

            D) x = y / 2 → x = y * 0.5

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: A and D - Both replace expensive operations with cheaper ones. A uses addition instead of multiplication, D uses multiplication instead of division.
                

            

        

    






    
 Induction Variable Elimination · Optimizing Loop Counters


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Induction Variable
             Basic Induction
             Derived Induction
             Strength Reduction
             GATE 2024

0

 After Induction Variable Elimination
