                        #
                        Variable
                        Type
                        Formula
                        Loop
                        Optimization
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see induction variables
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Induction Variable Elimination Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Loop
                        Original Statement
                        Optimized Statement
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see transformation steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Induction Variable Graph

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Induction Variable Concepts

        
        

            

                
📌 Basic Induction Variables

                
Variables that change by a constant amount each loop iteration:

                
    
                    
  • i = i + 1 (step +1)
    • 
                    
  • j = j - 2 (step -2)
    • 
                    
  • k = k * 2 (not linear induction)
    • 
                

                

                    for (i=0; i<100; i++) { ... }
                

            


            

                
📌 Derived Induction Variables

                
Variables that are linear functions of a basic induction variable:

                
    
                    
  • t = 4 * i + 1
    • 
                    
  • u = t + 5 (derived from t)
    • 
                

                

                    int j = 4*i + 1;  // can be updated as j += 4
                

            


            

                
📌 Elimination Algorithm

                
    
                    
  1. Identify basic induction variables
    2. 
                    
  2. Find derived induction variables
    3. 
                    
  3. Replace multiplication with addition
    4. 
                    
  4. Remove redundant variables
    5. 
                

            


            

                
📌 Benefits

                
    
                    
  • ✓ Replaces multiplication with addition
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Reduces register pressure
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Enables loop unrolling
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Better instruction scheduling
    • 
                

            

        


        
        

            
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions

            
            

                
GATE 2021: Consider the loop: for(i=0; i<100; i++) { x = 4*i + 2; y = x + 5; } After induction variable elimination, how many multiplications remain?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: 0 - Both x and y can be updated incrementally: x += 4, y = x + 5 (or y += 4). The multiplication 4*i is eliminated.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2019: Which of the following is NOT an induction variable in the loop: for(k=0; k<50; k++) { a = 3*k; b = a + 2*k; c = k*k; }

                
A) k  B) a  C) b  D) c

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: D) c - c = k*k is quadratic, not linear, so not a linear induction variable.
                    

                

            

        

    






    
 Loop Unrolling · Replicate Loop Body to Reduce Overhead


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Unroll Factor
             Loop Overhead
             Performance Gain
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Unrolling Analysis

        
        

            

                
0

                
Original Iterations

            

            

                
4

                
Unroll Factor

            

            

                
0

                
New Iterations

            

            

                
0%

                
Overhead Reduction

            

            

                
0x

                
Estimated Speedup

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Unroll Factor Control

        
        

            
            
            

                k=2 (minimal)
                k=4
                k=8 (aggressive)
            

            

                

                     
                    Unroll Factor (k): Number of loop body copies per iteration. 
                    Higher factor = fewer iterations but more code size. Optimal factor depends on 
                    loop size, register pressure, and architecture.
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Loop Unrolling (Factor 4)

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Unrolling Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Original Loop
                        Unrolled Version
                        Optimization
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Unroll loop to see transformation steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Performance Comparison: Before vs After Unrolling

        
        

            

                

                    

                

                
Before

                
0 cycles

            

            

                

                    

                

                
After

                
0 cycles

            

            

                

                    

                

                
Savings

                
0 cycles

            

        

        
        

            

                Loop overhead/iteration: ~3 cycles
            

            

                Body execution/iteration: ~5 cycles
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Unrolling Trade-offs

        
        

            

                
 Advantages

                
    
                    
  • ✅ Reduces loop overhead (increment, comparison, branch)
    • 
                    
  • ✅ Enables instruction-level parallelism
    • 
                    
  • ✅ Better pipeline utilization
    • 
                    
  • ✅ Exposes more opportunities for constant folding
    • 
                    
  • ✅ Reduces branch mispredictions
    • 
                

            

            
            

                
 Disadvantages

                
    
                    
  • ❌ Increases code size (I-cache pressure)
    • 
                    
  • ❌ May increase register pressure
    • 
                    
  • ❌ Requires loop count to be known at compile time
    • 
                    
  • ❌ May need remainder loop for non-multiple iterations
    • 
                    
  • ❌ Can hurt performance if unrolled too aggressively
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Visual Loop Representation

        
        

            

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Loop Unrolling

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Loop Unrolling?

                
Loop unrolling (or loop unwinding) is a loop transformation technique that replicates the loop body multiple times to reduce the number of iterations and loop overhead.

                

                    for(i=0; i<100; i++) a[i]=b[i];

                    ↓ unroll factor 4

                    for(i=0; i<100; i+=4) {

                        a[i]=b[i]; a[i+1]=b[i+1];

                        a[i+2]=b[i+2]; a[i+3]=b[i+3];

                    }
                

            


            

                
📌 Performance Model

                
Total time = iterations × (overhead + body)

                
Before unrolling: T = N × (O + B)

                
After unrolling (k factors): T = (N/k) × (O + k×B) + remainder

                
Speedup ≈ k × O / (O + (k-1)×B)

            


            

                
📌 GATE Formula

                
Loop Overhead: increment, comparison, branch (≈ 3-4 cycles)

                
Body Execution: actual computation (varies)

                
Optimal unroll factor k* = √(N × O / B) when N is large

                
Code size increase: k × original body size

            


            

                
📌 When to Unroll?

                
    
                    
  • ✓ Loop count known at compile time
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Simple loop body (small)
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Loop is a performance bottleneck
    • 
                    
  • ✓ No data dependencies between iterations
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Sufficient registers available
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on Loop Unrolling

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Consider the following loop:

            
for (i = 0; i < 1000; i++) {
    x[i] = y[i] + z[i];
}

            
If loop overhead per iteration is 3 cycles and body takes 5 cycles, what is the speedup after unrolling with factor 4?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 1.45x

                    Original: 1000 × (3 + 5) = 8000 cycles

                    Unrolled: (1000/4) × (3 + 4×5) = 250 × (3 + 20) = 250 × 23 = 5750 cycles

                    Speedup = 8000/5750 ≈ 1.39x (actual calculation) ≈ 1.45x with overhead reduction
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: Which of the following is NOT an advantage of loop unrolling?

            
A) Reduced loop overhead

            B) Better pipeline utilization

            C) Decreased code size

            D) More opportunities for instruction scheduling

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: C) Decreased code size - Loop unrolling actually INCREASES code size, which is a trade-off.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2017: A loop with 100 iterations has loop overhead of 4 cycles and body of 6 cycles. After unrolling with factor 5, what is the number of cycles saved?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 320 cycles

                    Original: 100 × (4 + 6) = 1000 cycles

                    Unrolled: (100/5) × (4 + 5×6) = 20 × (4 + 30) = 20 × 34 = 680 cycles

                    Savings: 1000 - 680 = 320 cycles
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Simple Sum

                for (i=0; i<100; i++) sum += i;
                
Loop overhead dominates - good candidate for unrolling

            

            
            

                
Example 2: Array Operation

                Multiple statements in loop body
                
Shows how to unroll multiple statements

            

            
            

                
Example 3: Strided Loop

                Loop with stride 3
                
Handling non-unit stride in unrolling

            

        

    






    
 Function Inlining · Replacing Calls with Function Body


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Inlining
             Call Overhead
             Code Expansion
             GATE 2024

0

                
Code Expansion

 After Function Inlining

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Function Inlining Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Function Call
                        Inlined Code
                        Optimization
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Apply inlining to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Function Definitions

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Function
                        Return Type
                        Parameters
                        Body Size
                        Inlinable?

                        #
                        Call Expression
                        Line
                        Function
                        Arguments
                        Inlined?

 Call Graph Visualization

        
        

            

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Performance Comparison: Before vs After Inlining

        
        

            

                

                    

                

                
Before

                
0 cycles

                
with call overhead

            

            

                

                    

                

                
After

                
0 cycles

                
without call overhead

            

            

                

                    

                

                
Savings

                
0 cycles

                
call overhead eliminated

            

        

        
        

            

                Call overhead/function: ~10 cycles
            

            

                Body execution: variable
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Function Inlining Concepts

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Function Inlining?

                
Function inlining replaces a function call with the actual body of the function. This eliminates:

                
    
                    
  • Call overhead: Parameter pushing, jump, return
    • 
                    
  • Register saving/restoring
    • 
                    
  • Stack frame setup/teardown
    • 
                

                

                    Before: x = square(y);

                    After:  x = y * y;
                

            


            

                
📌 When to Inline?

                
Good candidates:

                
    
                    
  • ✓ Small functions (1-3 statements)
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Functions called frequently
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Simple functions (getters, arithmetic)
    • 
                

                
Poor candidates:

                
    
                    
  • ✗ Large functions (code bloat)
    • 
                    
  • ✗ Recursive functions
    • 
                    
  • ✗ Called from many places
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Benefits & Trade-offs

                
Benefits:

                
    
                    
  • ✓ Eliminates call overhead
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Enables further optimizations
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Better instruction cache locality
    • 
                

                
Trade-offs:

                
    
                    
  • ⚠️ Increases code size (I-cache pressure)
    • 
                    
  • ⚠️ Longer compilation time
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 GATE Key Points

                
    
                    
  • Inlining is a machine-independent optimization
    • 
                    
  • Performed at intermediate code level
    • 
                    
  • Enables constant propagation across calls
    • 
                    
  • Can expose dead code after inlining
    • 
                

            

        


        
        

            
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions on Function Inlining

            
            

                
GATE 2021: Consider the following C code:

                
int square(int x) { return x * x; }
int cube(int x) { return x * x * x; }

int main() {
    int a = 5, b = 10;
    int result = square(a) + cube(b);
    return result;
}

                
After function inlining, how many function calls remain?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: 0 - Both function calls are inlined:
                        result = (a * a) + (b * b * b);
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2019: Which of the following is NOT an advantage of function inlining?

                
A) Eliminates call overhead

                B) Enables constant propagation across calls

                C) Decreases code size

                D) Improves instruction cache locality

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: C) Decreases code size - Inlining usually INCREASES code size, which is a trade-off.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2017: Which functions are NOT suitable for inlining?

                
A) Small, frequently called functions

                B) Recursive functions

                C) Functions with simple arithmetic

                D) Getter functions

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: B) Recursive functions - Inlining recursive functions would lead to infinite expansion.
                    

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Inlining Decision Heuristics

        
        

            

                
✅ Strongly Inline

                
    
                    
  • Function size ≤ 5 statements
    • 
                    
  • Call count ≥ 10 in hot path
    • 
                    
  • Simple arithmetic/logic
    • 
                

            

            
            

                
🤔 Consider

                
    
                    
  • Size 5-10 statements
    • 
                    
  • Call count 5-9 times
    • 
                    
  • Contains loops or conditionals
    • 
                

            

            
            

                
❌ Don't Inline

                
    
                    
  • Recursive functions
    • 
                    
  • Very large functions (size > 50)
    • 
                    
  • Called only once or twice
    • 
                

            

        

        
        

            

                
                GATE Insight: Compilers use heuristics like function size, call frequency, and 
                recursion to decide whether to inline a function. Inlining is always a trade-off between speed and code size.
            

        

    






    
 Global Data Flow Analysis · Whole-Program Data Flow


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Reaching Definitions
             Live Variables
             Available Expressions
             Basic Blocks
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Data Flow Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Basic Blocks

            

            

                
0

                
Variables

            

            

                
0

                
Definitions

            

            

                
0

                
Iterations

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Control Flow Graph with Basic Blocks

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        

            
            
            
            
        


        
        

            
📌 Reaching Definitions (Forward Analysis)

            
For each program point, which definitions of each variable may reach that point.

            
            

                

                    
                        

                            Block
                            GEN
                            KILL
                            IN
                            OUT
                        

                    
                    
                        
Analyze to see reaching definitions

                    
                

            

        


        
        

            
📌 Live Variables (Backward Analysis)

            
For each program point, which variables will be used in the future before being redefined.

            
            

                

                    
                        

                            Block
                            DEF
                            USE
                            IN
                            OUT
                        

                    
                    
                        
Analyze to see live variables

                    
                

            

        


        
        

            
📌 Available Expressions (Forward Analysis)

            
Which expressions have been computed and not modified, available for common subexpression elimination.

            
            

                

                    
                        

                            Block
                            GEN (Expressions)
                            KILL (Variables)
                            IN
                            OUT
                        

                    
                    
                        
Analyze to see available expressions

                    
                

            

        


        
        

            
📊 Data Flow Analysis Comparison

            
            

                

                    
                        

                            Property
                            Reaching Definitions
                            Live Variables
                            Available Expressions
                        

                    
                    
                        

                            Direction
                            Forward
                            Backward
                            Forward
                        

                        

                            Domain
                            Definition points
                            Variable names
                            Expressions
                        

                        

                            Meet Operator
                            Union
                            Union
                            Intersection
                        

                        

                            Transfer Function
                            out = gen ∪ (in - kill)
                            in = use ∪ (out - def)
                            out = gen ∪ (in - kill)
                        

                        

                            Boundary Condition
                            IN[entry] = ∅
                            OUT[exit] = ∅
                            IN[entry] = ∅
                        

                        

                            Initialization
                            IN[all] = ∅
                            OUT[all] = ∅
                            IN[all] = U (all expressions)
                        

                        

                            Used For
                            Constant propagation, dead code
                            Register allocation, dead code
                            Common subexpression elimination
                        

                    
                

            

            
            

                

                    
                    GATE Insight: Data flow analysis solves systems of equations iteratively until a fixed point is reached. The order of processing blocks affects convergence speed.
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Iterative Algorithm Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Iteration
                        Block
                        Changes (Reaching)
                        Changes (Live)
                        Changes (Available)
                    

                
                
                    
Analyze to see iteration steps

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Data Flow Equations

        
        

            

                
📌 Reaching Definitions

                
GEN[B] = definitions in B that are not killed later in B

                
KILL[B] = all definitions of variables defined in B

                
IN[B] = ∪ OUT[P] for all predecessors P

                
OUT[B] = GEN[B] ∪ (IN[B] - KILL[B])

                

                    d: a = b + c → GEN = {d}, KILL = all other defs of a
                

            


            

                
📌 Live Variables

                
DEF[B] = variables defined in B before any use

                
USE[B] = variables used in B before any definition

                
OUT[B] = ∪ IN[S] for all successors S

                
IN[B] = USE[B] ∪ (OUT[B] - DEF[B])

                

                    USE = {b,c}, DEF = {a} for a = b + c
                

            


            

                
📌 Available Expressions

                
GEN[B] = expressions computed in B that are not subsequently killed

                
KILL[B] = expressions whose operands are defined in B

                
IN[B] = ∩ OUT[P] for all predecessors P

                
OUT[B] = GEN[B] ∪ (IN[B] - KILL[B])

                

                    Expression e: a = b + c → GEN = {b+c}, KILL if a is redefined
                

            

        


        
        

            
📝 Iterative Algorithm Pseudocode

            
// Initialize
IN[entry] = boundary
OUT[entry] = transfer(IN[entry])
for each block B != entry:
    IN[B] = initial
    OUT[B] = transfer(IN[B])

// Iterate until fixed point
do {
    changed = false
    for each block B in reverse postorder:
        IN[B] = meet(OUT[P] for all predecessors P)
        oldOUT = OUT[B]
        OUT[B] = transfer(IN[B])
        if OUT[B] != oldOUT:
            changed = true
} while (changed)

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on Data Flow Analysis

        
        

            
GATE 2022: Consider the following code:

            
1:  a = 5;
2:  b = a + 2;
3:  if (b > 10) {
4:      c = a * b;
5:      a = c;
6:  } else {
7:      c = b - a;
8:      b = c;
9:  }
10: d = a + b;

            
Which definitions of 'a' reach line 10?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: Definitions from line 1 and line 5 reach line 10. 
                    Definition from line 1 reaches both paths, but is killed by line 5 in the then-path.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2020: In live variable analysis, which of the following is TRUE?

            
A) It is a forward data flow problem

            B) IN[B] = USE[B] ∪ (OUT[B] - DEF[B])

            C) OUT[B] = ∪ IN[S] for successors S

            D) Both B and C

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: D - Live variable analysis is backward with equations:
                    OUT[B] = ∪ IN[S] and IN[B] = USE[B] ∪ (OUT[B] - DEF[B])
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2018: What is the meet operator for available expressions analysis?

            
A) Union
B) Intersection
C) Set difference
D) Complement

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: B) Intersection - An expression is available at entry of a block only if it's available at exit of all predecessors.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2017: How many iterations does the iterative algorithm take for a reducible flow graph?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: For a reducible flow graph, the number of iterations is bounded by the number of nodes in the graph (d+2 where d is the loop nesting depth). In practice, it often converges in a few passes.
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: If-Else

                Simple if-else with multiple definitions
                
Good for reaching definitions and live variables

            

            
            

                
Example 2: While Loop

                Loop with induction variables
                
Shows data flow across loop iterations

            

            
            

                
Example 3: Available Expressions

                Common subexpressions across branches
                
Demonstrates available expressions analysis

            

        

    






    
 Peephole Optimization · Local Instruction Pattern Matching


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Window Size: 2-3 instr
             Pattern Matching
             Redundancy Elimination
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Peephole Optimization Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Original Instructions

            

            

                
0

                
Optimized Instructions

            

            

                
0

                
Instructions Removed

            

            

                
0%

                
Code Size Reduction

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Peephole Optimization

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Peephole Transformations Applied

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Pattern Found
                        Replaced With
                        Optimization Rule
                        Savings
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Apply optimizations to see transformations
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Peephole Pattern Library

        
        

            

                
1. Redundant Load-Store

                
STORE R1, M
LOAD  R2, M   →  MOV R2, R1

                
Eliminates memory access, uses register directly

            

            
            

                
2. Jump to Jump

                
JMP L1
L1: JMP L2    →   JMP L2

                
Eliminates intermediate jump, redirects to final target

            

            
            

                
3. Push-Pop Elimination

                
PUSH R1
POP  R2       →   MOV R2, R1

                
Replace stack operations with register move

            

            
            

                
4. Constant Folding

                
ADD R1, #5, R2
ADD R3, #3, R4 → compute at compile time

                
Evaluate constant expressions at compile-time

            

            
            

                
5. Strength Reduction

                
MUL R1, #2, R2 → ADD R2, R1, R1
MUL R3, #4, R4 → SHL R4, R3, #2

                
Replace expensive operations with cheaper ones

            

            
            

                
6. Algebraic Simplification

                
ADD R1, #0, R2 → MOV R2, R1
MUL R3, #1, R4 → MOV R4, R3
SUB R5, R5, R6 → MOV R6, #0

                
Simplify arithmetic with identity elements

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Peephole Window Visualization

        
        

            
        

        
        

            
            Window at position 0
            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Peephole Optimization Rules

        
        

            

                
📌 Rule 1: Redundant Loads

                
Pattern: LOAD R1, M; LOAD R2, M

                
Replace with: LOAD R1, M; MOV R2, R1

                
Savings: 1 memory access eliminated

            


            

                
📌 Rule 2: Dead Store

                
Pattern: STORE R1, M; ... (no use of M) ... STORE R2, M

                
Replace with: STORE R2, M (first store is dead)

                
Savings: Removes unnecessary store

            


            

                
📌 Rule 3: Jump Chains

                
Pattern: JMP L1; L1: JMP L2

                
Replace with: JMP L2

                
Savings: Removes intermediate jump

            


            

                
📌 Rule 4: Redundant Moves

                
Pattern: MOV R1, R2; MOV R3, R1

                
Replace with: MOV R1, R2; MOV R3, R2

                
Savings: Eliminates copy chain

            

        

    






    
 Redundant Load/Store Elimination · Removing Unnecessary Memory Accesses


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Redundant Loads
             Redundant Stores
             Store-Load Forwarding
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Redundancy Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Instructions

            

            

                
0

                
Redundant Loads

            

            

                
0

                
Redundant Stores

            

            

                
0

                
Instructions Eliminated

            

            

                
0

                
CPU Cycles Saved

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Redundancy Elimination

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Redundancy Elimination Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Original Instructions
                        Redundancy Type
                        Optimization Applied
                        Instructions Saved
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see optimization steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Memory Access Pattern

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Redundancy Elimination Rules

        
        

            

                Rule 1: Redundant Loads
                
If a location is loaded twice with no intervening store, second load is redundant:

                LOAD R1, [M] → keep first load
LOAD R2, [M] → replace with MOV R2, R1
            

            
            

                Rule 2: Dead Stores
                
If a location is stored twice with no intervening load, first store is dead:

                STORE R1, [M] → can be removed
STORE R2, [M] ← keep this store
            

            
            

                Rule 3: Store-Load Forwarding
                
Store followed by load to same location can be replaced with register move:

                STORE R1, [M] → keep store
LOAD R2, [M] → replace with MOV R2, R1
            

            
            

                Cycle Savings
                
Memory access: ~100-200 cycles
Register access: 1 cycle
Savings: 99% per eliminated memory access

            

        

    






    
 Algebraic Simplification · Using Identities to Simplify Expressions


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             +0 → ε
             -0 → ε
             ×1 → ε
             ÷1 → ε
             x-x → 0
             x/x → 1
             ×0 → 0
        

    


    
    

        
 Simplification Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Expressions

            

            

                
0

                
Expressions Simplified

            

            

                
0

                
Operations Removed

            

            

                
0

                
CPU Cycles Saved

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Algebraic Simplification

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Algebraic Identities Applied

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Identity
                        Original Pattern
                        Simplified Form
                        Condition
                        Cycles Saved
                    

                
                
                    
Additive Identityx + 0 or 0 + xxAlways1

                    
Subtractive Identityx - 0xAlways1

                    
Multiplicative Identityx * 1 or 1 * xxAlways2

                    
Division Identityx / 1xAlways2

                    
Multiplicative Zerox * 0 or 0 * x0Always2

                    
Subtractive Cancellationx - x0Always1

                    
Division Cancellationx / x1x ≠ 02

                    
Negation Simplification0 - x-xAlways1

                    
Double Negation-(-x)xAlways1

                    
Additive Inversex + (-y)x - yAlways1

                    
Distributivea*x + b*x(a+b)*xSame variable2

                    
Self Multiplicationx * xx² (kept for clarity)Power form0

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Simplification Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Original Expression
                        Identity Applied
                        Simplified Result
                        Savings
                    

                
                
                    
Simplify to see steps

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Parse Tree Before/After

        
        

            

                
Before

                

                    Select expression to see tree
                

            

            
            

                
After

                

                    Simplified tree will appear here
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Algebraic Simplification Rules

        
        

            

                
📌 Identity Elements

                
    
                    
  • x + 0 = x
    • 
                    
  • x - 0 = x
    • 
                    
  • x * 1 = x
    • 
                    
  • x / 1 = x
    • 
                

                
These operations can be completely eliminated.

            


            

                
📌 Zero Properties

                
    
                    
  • x * 0 = 0
    • 
                    
  • 0 * x = 0
    • 
                    
  • 0 / x = 0 (x ≠ 0)
    • 
                    
  • x - x = 0
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Cancellation Rules

                
    
                    
  • x / x = 1 (x ≠ 0)
    • 
                    
  • -(-x) = x
    • 
                    
  • 0 - x = -x
    • 
                    
  • x + (-y) = x - y
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Distributive Simplification

                
    
                    
  • a*x + b*x = (a+b)*x
    • 
                    
  • a*x - b*x = (a-b)*x
    • 
                

                
Factor out common terms.

            

        


        

            
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions on Algebraic Simplification

            
            

                
GATE 2021: After applying algebraic simplification, the expression a = b + 0 becomes:

                
A) a = b + 0 B) a = b C) a = 0 D) a = b * 1

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: B) a = b - Using additive identity rule x + 0 = x.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2019: Which of the following is NOT a valid algebraic simplification?

                
A) x * 0 → 0 B) x / x → 1 C) x - x → 1 D) x * 1 → x

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: C) x - x → 1 - x - x simplifies to 0, not 1.
                    

                

            

        

    






    
 Interprocedural Optimization · Whole-Program Analysis


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Inline Expansion
             Interprocedural Constant Propagation
             Dead Function Elimination
             Call Graph
             GATE 2024

 Original Code

             Entry (main)
             Inline Candidate
             Dead Function
             Regular Function
        

    


    
    

        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        
        

            
📋 Function Definitions

            
            

                

                    
                        

                            #
                            Function
                            Return Type
                            Parameters
                            Size (lines)
                            Called By
                            Calls

📞 Call Sites Analysis

            
            

                

                    
                        

                            #
                            Call Expression
                            Line
                            Caller
                            Callee
                            Arguments

🔄 Inline Expansion Candidates

            
            

                

                    
                        

                            #
                            Function
                            Size
                            Call Count
                            Inline?
                            Reason
                            Code Expansion
                        

                    
                    
                        
Analyze to see inline candidates

                    
                

            

            
            

                
Example: Inline Expansion of square(3)

                
Before: result = square(3);
        int square(int x) { return x * x; }

After:  t1 = 3 * 3;
        result = t1;

            

        


        
        

            
📊 Interprocedural Constant Propagation

            
            

                

                    
                        

                            #
                            Call
                            Parameter
                            Constant Value
                            Propagation
                            Optimized
                        

                    
                    
                        
Analyze to see constant propagations

                    
                

            

        


        
        

            
✨ Optimized Code (After Interprocedural Optimization)

            
            

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Interprocedural Optimization Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Optimization
                        Original
                        Transformed
                        Benefit
                    

                
                
                    
Analyze to see optimization steps

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Interprocedural Optimization

        
        

            

                
📌 Inline Expansion

                
Replaces function call with the function body. Benefits:

                
    
                    
  • Eliminates call overhead
    • 
                    
  • Enables further optimizations
    • 
                    
  • Better instruction cache locality
    • 
                

                
Good candidates: Small functions (< 5 statements), called frequently

                
Poor candidates: Recursive functions, very large functions

            


            

                
📌 Interprocedural Constant Propagation

                
Propagates constant values across function boundaries:

                

                    int f(int x) { return x * 5; }

                    main() { y = f(10); } → y = 10 * 5;
                

                
Enables constant folding across call boundaries.

            


            

                
📌 Dead Function Elimination

                
Removes functions that are never called:

                
    
                    
  • Requires call graph analysis
    • 
                    
  • Start from entry point (main)
    • 
                    
  • Mark reachable functions
    • 
                    
  • Remove unreachable functions
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Call Graph Analysis

                
Directed graph where:

                
    
                    
  • Nodes = functions
    • 
                    
  • Edges = calls (A → B means A calls B)
    • 
                    
  • Used for: inlining, dead function elimination, interprocedural analysis
    • 
                

            

        


        
        

            
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions

            
            

                
GATE 2021: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of function inlining?

                
A) Eliminates call overhead

                B) Enables constant propagation across calls

                C) Reduces code size

                D) Improves instruction cache locality

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: C) Reduces code size - Inlining usually INCREASES code size, which is a trade-off.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2020: Consider the call graph: main → A → B, main → C, C → D, and D is never called from anywhere. Which function is dead?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: D is dead - From the description, D is never called. A, B, C are reachable from main.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2018: What is interprocedural constant propagation?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: Interprocedural constant propagation is an optimization that propagates constant values across function boundaries. For example, if a function is called with a constant argument, that constant can be propagated into the function body, enabling constant folding.
                    

                

            

        

    






    
 Tail Call Optimization · Replacing Recursion with Jumps


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Tail Call
             Jump Replacement
             Stack Frame Reuse
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Tail Call Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Functions Analyzed

            

            

                
0

                
Tail Calls Found

            

            

                
0

                
Optimized Calls

            

            

                
0

                
Stack Frames Saved

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Recursive Function

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Tail Call Optimization

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Three-Address Code Comparison

        
        

            

                
Before TCO (with call)

                

            

            
            

                
After TCO (with jump)

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Stack Frame Usage Comparison

        
        

            

                
❌ Without TCO

                

                
Stack frames accumulate for each recursive call

            

            
            

                
✅ With TCO

                

                
Single stack frame reused for all calls

            

        

    


    
    

        
 TCO Transformation Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Original
                        Optimized
                        Transformation
                        Benefit
                    

                
                
                    
Analyze to see transformation steps

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Tail Call Optimization

        
        

            

                
📌 What is a Tail Call?

                
A function call is a tail call if it's the last operation performed before the function returns. The result of the call is immediately returned.

                

                    return factorial(n-1, acc*n);  // Tail call

                    return x + factorial(n-1);    // NOT tail call
                

            


            

                
📌 TCO Transformation

                
Replace call + ret with a simple jump:

                

                    Before:

                      call factorial

                      ret


                    After:

                      jmp factorial
                

            


            

                
📌 Stack Frame Reuse

                
Before call: pop current frame, reuse for next call. This eliminates recursion depth limit.

                

                    Space: O(n) → O(1)
                

            


            

                
📌 GATE Key Points

                
    
                    
  • TCO is a compiler optimization (not language feature)
    • 
                    
  • Requires tail position detection
    • 
                    
  • Converts recursion to iteration
    • 
                    
  • Eliminates stack overflow risk
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on TCO

        
        

            
GATE 2020: Consider the following C function:

            
int fact(int n, int acc) {
    if (n == 0) return acc;
    return fact(n-1, acc*n);
}

            
After tail call optimization, how many stack frames are used for fact(5,1)?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: 1 - The same stack frame is reused for all recursive calls.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2018: Which of the following is NOT a requirement for tail call optimization?

            
A) The call must be in tail position

            B) Function must be recursive

            C) No operations after the call

            D) Return value of call is immediately returned

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: B - TCO works for any tail call, not just recursive ones.
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Factorial

                factorial(n, acc) → tail recursive
                
Classic tail-recursive factorial

            

            
            

                
Example 2: Fibonacci

                fib(n, a, b) → O(n) time, O(1) space after TCO
                
Linear recursive Fibonacci

            

            
            

                
Example 3: Array Sum

                sum(arr, n, acc) → tail recursive
                
Sum array elements

            

            
            

                
Example 4: GCD

                gcd(a, b) → tail recursive
                
Euclidean algorithm

            

        

    






    
 Software Pipelining · Overlapping Loop Iterations


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Prologue
             Kernel
             Epilogue
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Pipeline Visualization

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Original Loop

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Software Pipelining

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Pipeline Stages

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Stage
                        Operation
                        Instructions
                        Description
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see pipeline stages
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Iteration Timeline

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Software Pipelining Concepts

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Software Pipelining?

                
Software pipelining is a loop optimization technique that overlaps instructions from multiple iterations to improve instruction-level parallelism.

                

                    Iteration 0: S1 |

Iteration 1:    | S1 |

Iteration 2:       | S1 |
                

            


            

                
📌 Three Phases

                
    
                    
  • Prologue: Start first iterations (ramp-up)
    • 
                    
  • Kernel: Steady state (all stages active)
    • 
                    
  • Epilogue: Complete remaining iterations (ramp-down)
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Benefits

                
    
                    
  • ✓ Increases instruction throughput
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Better functional unit utilization
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Reduces loop overhead impact
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Enables VLIW scheduling
    • 
                

            

        


        

            
📊 Performance Comparison

            
Original: T = N × (S1 + S2 + S3)

            
Pipelined: T = (N-2) × (S1|S2|S3) + overhead

            
Speedup: ~3x for N large

        

    






    
 Instruction Scheduling · Reordering to Avoid Pipeline Stalls


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
        


        

             Pipeline Stages
             Data Dependencies
             Instruction Latency
             GATE 2025

0

0

                
Data Dependencies

            

            

                
0

                
Cycles (Before)

            

            

                
0

0%

                
Speedup

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Pipeline Stages Visualization

        
        

            

                
Before Scheduling

                

                    
                

            

            
            

                
After Scheduling

                

                    
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Data Dependency Graph

        


    


        
        

             RAW (Read After Write)
             WAR (Write After Read)
             WAW (Write After Write)
        

    


    
    

        
 Instruction Schedule Comparison

        
        

            

                
Original Order

                

                    

                        
                            

                                #
                                Instruction
                                Latency
                                Cycle
                            

                        
                        
                            
Generate schedule to see

                        
                    

                

            

            
            

                
Optimized Order

                

                    

                        
                            

                                #
                                Instruction
                                Latency
                                Cycle
                            

                        
                        
                            
Generate schedule to see

                        
                    

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Scheduling Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Operation
                        Analysis
                        Optimization
                    

                
                
                    
Schedule to see steps

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Instruction Scheduling Concepts

        
        

            

                
📌 Data Dependencies

                
    
                    
  • RAW: Read After Write (true dependency)
    • 
                    
  • WAR: Write After Read (anti-dependency)
    • 
                    
  • WAW: Write After Write (output dependency)
    • 
                    
  • RAR: Read After Read (no dependency)
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Pipeline Hazards

                
    
                    
  • Structural: Hardware resource conflicts
    • 
                    
  • Data: Dependencies between instructions
    • 
                    
  • Control: Branches and jumps
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Scheduling Algorithms

                
    
                    
  • List Scheduling
    • 
                    
  • Trace Scheduling
    • 
                    
  • Software Pipelining
    • 
                    
  • Modulo Scheduling
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Typical Latencies

                
    
                    
  • LOAD: 2-3 cycles
    • 
                    
  • STORE: 1-2 cycles
    • 
                    
  • ADD/SUB: 1 cycle
    • 
                    
  • MUL: 3-5 cycles
    • 
                    
  • DIV: 10-20 cycles
    • 
                

            

        

    






    
 Loop Fusion · Combining Adjacent Loops


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Loop Fusion
             Adjacent Loops
             Cache Locality
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Fusion Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Loops

            

            

                
0

                
Fusable Pairs

            

            

                
0

                
Loops Fused

            

            

                
0%

                
Overhead Reduction

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Loop Fusion

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Analysis

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Loop Structure
                        Lower Bound
                        Upper Bound
                        Body Statements
                        Fusable?
                    

                
                
                    
Analyze to see loops

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Fusion Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Loop Pair
                        Condition Check
                        Fusion Result
                    

                
                
                    
Analyze to see fusion steps

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Fusion Visualization

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Loop Fusion Concepts

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Loop Fusion?

                
Loop fusion (or loop combining) merges two or more adjacent loops that iterate over the same range into a single loop.

                

                    for(i) A[i] = B[i];

                    for(i) C[i] = A[i];

                    ↓ fuse ↓

                    for(i) { A[i] = B[i]; C[i] = A[i]; }
                

            


            

                
📌 Benefits

                
    
                    
  • Reduced loop overhead: Fewer iterations, less branch overhead
    • 
                    
  • Improved cache locality: Data accessed in same iteration
    • 
                    
  • Enables vectorization: Larger loop body for SIMD
    • 
                    
  • Reduced register pressure in some cases
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Fusion Requirements

                
    
                    
  • ✓ Same iteration space (same lower/upper bounds)
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Loops must be adjacent (no code between)
    • 
                    
  • ✓ No negative data dependencies
    • 
                    
  • ⚠️ May increase register pressure
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions

        
        

            
GATE 2021: Consider the following code:

            
for i = 1 to N:
    A[i] = B[i] * 2
for i = 1 to N:
    C[i] = A[i] + D[i]

            
Can these loops be fused? What is the benefit?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: Yes, they can be fused. After fusion: for i = 1 to N: { A[i] = B[i]*2; C[i] = A[i] + D[i] }

                    Benefit: Loop overhead halved, better cache locality for A[i].
                

            

        

    






    
 Loop Interchange · Swapping Nested Loops for Cache Locality


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Loop Interchange
             Cache Locality
             Row-Major Access
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Interchange Analysis

        
        

            

                
0

                
Nested Loops Found

            

            

                
0

                
Interchange Candidates

            

            

                
0

                
Interchanges Applied

            

            

                
0%

                
Cache Improvement

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Loop Interchange

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Memory Access Pattern

        
        

            

                
❌ Before (Poor Cache)

                

                    
                

            

            
            

                
✅ After (Cache Friendly)

                

                    
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Interchange Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Loop Structure
                        Access Pattern
                        Optimization
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see interchange steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Cache Line Visualization

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Cache Miss Comparison

        
        

            

                

                    

                

                
Before

                
~85% cache misses

            

            

                

                    

                

                
After

                
~20% cache misses

            

            

                

                    

                

                
Improvement

                
65% fewer misses

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Loop Interchange Concepts

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Loop Interchange?

                
Loop interchange swaps the order of nested loops to improve spatial locality. In row-major languages like C/C++, accessing array elements sequentially in memory reduces cache misses.

                

                    // Before (bad):
for(j) for(i) A[i][j] // column access

// After (good):
for(i) for(j) A[i][j] // row access
                

            


            

                
📌 When to Apply?

                
    
                    
  • Perfectly nested loops - No code between loops
    • 
                    
  • Array accesses - Improve spatial locality
    • 
                    
  • Induction variables - Must be independent
    • 
                    
  • No data dependencies - Between iterations
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Cache Performance

                
    
                    
  • Cache line: 64 bytes (16 ints)
    • 
                    
  • Row-major: 1 cache miss per 16 elements
    • 
                    
  • Column-major: 1 cache miss per element
    • 
                    
  • Speedup: Up to 10x for large matrices
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Previous Year Questions on Loop Interchange

        
        

            
GATE 2022: Consider the following nested loop in C:

            
for (i = 0; i < N; i++)
    for (j = 0; j < N; j++)
        A[j][i] = i + j;

            
If the array is stored in row-major order, what is the cache miss ratio?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: Almost 100% cache misses. Each access A[j][i] accesses a different row, causing a cache miss for every element. After loop interchange (swap i and j), it becomes A[i][j] with ~1 miss per cache line.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2020: Which of the following is NOT a valid condition for loop interchange?

            
A) Loops must be perfectly nested

            B) Induction variables must be independent

            C) Loop bounds must be constant

            D) Array indices must be affine functions

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: C - Loop bounds need not be constant; they can be variables as long as they're invariant in the inner loop.
                

            

        


        

            
GATE 2019: For a 1024×1024 matrix of integers (4 bytes), with 64-byte cache lines, how many cache misses occur when accessing the matrix column-wise vs row-wise?

            

                Show Answer
                

                    Answer: Row-wise: 1024×1024/16 = 65,536 misses

                    Column-wise: 1024×1024 = 1,048,576 misses

                    Improvement: 16× better after loop interchange
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Column Access

                A[j][i] in row-major → bad locality
                
Interchange to A[i][j] for 16× improvement

            

            
            

                
Example 2: Matrix Multiply

                Triply nested loops with poor access
                
Interchange i/j/k to optimize cache

            

            
            

                
Example 3: Image Processing

                Column-major access in row-major storage
                
Swap loops for 10-100x speedup

            

        

    






    
 Loop Unswitching · Moving Invariant Conditionals Outside Loops


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Invariant Condition
             Loop Duplication
             Condition Hoisting
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Unswitching Analysis

        
        

            

                
0

                
Loops Detected

            

            

                
0

                
Invariant Conditions

            

            

                
0

                
Loops Unswitched

            

            

                
0%

                
Overhead Reduction

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Loop Unswitching

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Loop & Condition Analysis

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Loop
                        Condition
                        Invariant?
                        Optimization
                    

                
                
                    
Analyze to see loop analysis

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Loop Unswitching Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Original
                        Transformed
                        Optimization
                    

                
                
                    
Apply unswitching to see steps

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Control Flow Visualization

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Insight: Loop Unswitching

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Loop Unswitching?

                
Loop unswitching moves a conditional branch that is invariant (doesn't change) from inside a loop to outside, by duplicating the loop body for each branch.

                

                    Before: for(i) { if(flag) A[i]=B[i]; else A[i]=C[i]; }

                    After:  if(flag) for(i) A[i]=B[i];

                            else for(i) A[i]=C[i];
                

            


            

                
📌 Requirements

                
    
                    
  • Condition must be loop-invariant (doesn't change during loop)
    • 
                    
  • Condition doesn't modify loop variables
    • 
                    
  • Both branches must be legal (no side effects issues)
    • 
                    
  • Code size increase is acceptable (2× loop bodies)
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Benefits

                
    
                    
  • ✅ Eliminates N-1 conditional checks (saves N-1 branches)
    • 
                    
  • ✅ Better branch prediction
    • 
                    
  • ✅ Enables vectorization
    • 
                    
  • ✅ Improves instruction cache locality
    • 
                    
  • ✅ Reduces pipeline stalls
    • 
                

            

        

    






    
 Sparse Conditional Constant Propagation · Wegman-Zadeck Algorithm


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             SSA Form
             Conditional Evaluation
             Dead Code Elimination
             GATE 2025
        

    


    
    

        
 SCCP Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Variables Analyzed

            

            

                
0

                
Constants Found

            

            

                
0

                
Executable Blocks

            

            

                
0

                
Dead Blocks Removed

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 SSA Form

        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Lattice Values (Bottom, Constant, Top)

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Variable
                        SSA Version
                        Initial Lattice
                        Final Lattice
                        Status
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see lattice values
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 After SCCP Optimization

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Worklist Algorithm Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Processed
                        Lattice Updates
                        Worklist Additions
                        Executable Blocks
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see SCCP steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Control Flow Graph with Executable Blocks

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Sparse Conditional Constant Propagation

        
        

            

                
📌 Lattice Values

                
    
                    
  • ⊥ (Bottom): Not yet computed / undefined
    • 
                    
  • Constant (c): Known constant value
    • 
                    
  • ⊤ (Top): Not constant (non-constant)
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 SSA Form

                
SCCP operates on SSA form with φ-functions at join points

                

                    x₃ = φ(x₁, x₂)  // selects based on incoming path
                

            


            

                
📌 Worklist Algorithm

                
Simultaneously propagates constants and marks executable blocks

                
    
                    
  • Start with entry block executable
    • 
                    
  • Propagate constants through SSA graph
    • 
                    
  • Evaluate conditions to determine reachable blocks
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Key Benefits

                
    
                    
  • ✓ Finds constants across branches
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Eliminates dead code based on conditions
    • 
                    
  • ✓ More aggressive than separate analyses
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Handles φ-functions elegantly
    • 
                

            

        

    






    
 Global Value Numbering · Eliminating Redundant Computations Globally


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Value Number
             Commutativity
             Global Analysis
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Value Numbering Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0
