0

                
Unique Values

            

            

                
0

                
Redundancies Found

            

            

                
0

                
Computations Saved

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Value Numbering Results

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Expression
                        Value Number
                        Hash Key
                        First Occurrence
                        Congruent Expressions
                        Status
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see value numbers
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Value Number Table (VN Table)

        
        

            

                
                    

                        VN
                        Expression / Value
                        Operator
                        Left VN
                        Right VN
                        Variables
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see value table
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 After Value Numbering (Optimized)

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Congruence Classes

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Value Numbering Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Operation
                        Expression
                        Value Number
                        Action
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Global Value Numbering (GVN)

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Value Numbering?

                
Value numbering assigns unique identifiers to values based on their computation. Two expressions have the same value number if they compute the same value.

                

                    a = x + y   → VN1
b = x + y   → VN1 (same as a)
                

            


            

                
📌 Hash-Based Algorithm

                
Each expression is hashed based on its operator and the value numbers of its operands:

                
    
                    
  • Constants: hash = const value
    • 
                    
  • Variables: hash = variable name
    • 
                    
  • Binary ops: hash(op, VN(left), VN(right))
    • 
                    
  • Commutativity: op sorted operands
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Congruence Classes

                
Values that are proven equal belong to the same congruence class. GVN identifies:

                
    
                    
  • Local redundancies (same block)
    • 
                    
  • Global redundancies (across blocks)
    • 
                    
  • Constant propagation opportunities
    • 
                    
  • Commutative expression matching
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Key Benefits

                
    
                    
  • ✓ Finds more redundancies than CSE
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Works across basic blocks
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Handles commutativity (a+b = b+a)
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Detects constant folding opportunities
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Prepares for copy propagation
    • 
                

            

        


        
        

            
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions on GVN

            
            

                
GATE 2022: Consider the following code:

                
a = x + y;
b = x + y;
c = a + 5;
d = (x + y) + 5;

                
After global value numbering, how many unique value numbers exist?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: 3

                        VN1: x + y (a and b)

                        VN2: 5 (constant)

                        VN3: VN1 + 5 (c and d)
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2020: Which of the following is TRUE about value numbering?

                
A) It only works within basic blocks

                B) It cannot handle commutative operators

                C) It assigns numbers based on operator and operand values

                D) It is a runtime optimization

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: C - Value numbering uses the operator and the value numbers of operands to create a hash key.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2019: In global value numbering, what is a congruence class?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: A set of expressions or values that are proven to be equal. All members of a congruence class can be replaced by the same representative expression.
                    

                

            

        

    






    
 Global Value Numbering · Eliminating Redundant Computations Globally

                        Step
                        Operation
                        Expression

                        Action
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Global Value Numbering (GVN)

        
        

            

                
📌 What is Value Numbering?

                
Value numbering assigns unique identifiers to values based on their computation. Two expressions have the same value number if they compute the same value.

                

                    a = x + y   → VN1
b = x + y   → VN1 (same as a)
                

            


            

                
📌 Hash-Based Algorithm

                
Each expression is hashed based on its operator and the value numbers of its operands:

                
    
                    
  • Constants: hash = const value
    • 
                    
  • Variables: hash = variable name
    • 
                    
  • Binary ops: hash(op, VN(left), VN(right))
    • 
                    
  • Commutativity: op sorted operands
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Congruence Classes

                
Values that are proven equal belong to the same congruence class. GVN identifies:

                
    
                    
  • Local redundancies (same block)
    • 
                    
  • Global redundancies (across blocks)
    • 
                    
  • Constant propagation opportunities
    • 
                    
  • Commutative expression matching
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Key Benefits

                
    
                    
  • ✓ Finds more redundancies than CSE
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Works across basic blocks
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Handles commutativity (a+b = b+a)
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Detects constant folding opportunities
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Prepares for copy propagation
    • 
                

            

        


        
        

            
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions on GVN

            
            

                
GATE 2022: Consider the following code:

                
a = x + y;
b = x + y;
c = a + 5;
d = (x + y) + 5;

                
After global value numbering, how many unique value numbers exist?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: 3

                        VN1: x + y (a and b)

                        VN2: 5 (constant)

                        VN3: VN1 + 5 (c and d)
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2020: Which of the following is TRUE about value numbering?

                
A) It only works within basic blocks

                B) It cannot handle commutative operators

                C) It assigns numbers based on operator and operand values

                D) It is a runtime optimization

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: C - Value numbering uses the operator and the value numbers of operands to create a hash key.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2019: In global value numbering, what is a congruence class?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: A set of expressions or values that are proven to be equal. All members of a congruence class can be replaced by the same representative expression.
                    

                

            

        

    






    
 Partial Redundancy Elimination (PRE) · Moving Computations to Optimal Points


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Available on Some Paths
             Expression Anticipation
             Earliest/Earliest Placement
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 PRE Analysis Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Statements

            

            

                
0

                
Expressions Analyzed

            

            

                
0

                
Partially Redundant

            

            

                
0

                
Totally Redundant

            

            

                
0

                
Redundancies Eliminated

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Control Flow Graph with Expression Availability

        
        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 Data Flow Analysis for PRE

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Block
                        ANTLOC (Anticipated)
                        AVAIL (Available)
                        EARLIEST
                        LATEST
                        INSERT
                        DELETE
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see data flow equations
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Expression Redundancy Analysis

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Expression
                        Occurrences
                        Redundancy Type
                        Optimization
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see expression analysis
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 After Partial Redundancy Elimination

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 PRE Algorithm Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Expression
                        Analysis
                        Optimization
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Analyze to see PRE steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Partial Redundancy Elimination

        
        

            

                
📌 What is PRE?

                
Partial Redundancy Elimination is an optimization that removes expressions that are redundant on some (but not all) execution paths. It moves computations to optimal points in the control flow graph.

                

                    Before: if (c) x = a+b; else y = a+b; z = a+b;

                    After:  t = a+b; if (c) x = t; else y = t; z = t;
                

            


            

                
📌 Data Flow Equations

                
ANTLOC (Anticipated): Expressions that will be evaluated on all paths from block exit

                
AVAIL (Available): Expressions that are available at block entry

                
EARLIEST: Where expression can first be placed

                
LATEST: Optimal placement point

            


            

                
📌 Four-Step Algorithm

                
    
                    
  1. Compute ANTLOC (anticipated expressions)
    2. 
                    
  2. Compute AVAIL (available expressions)
    3. 
                    
  3. Find EARLIEST placement points
    4. 
                    
  4. Find LATEST placement points
    5. 
                    
  5. Insert at LATEST, delete redundant
    6. 
                

            


            

                
📌 Benefits & Trade-offs

                
    
                    
  • ✓ Eliminates partially redundant expressions
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Moves computations out of loops
    • 
                    
  • ✓ Reduces code size in some cases
    • 
                    
  • ⚠️ May increase register pressure
    • 
                    
  • ⚠️ Requires data flow analysis
    • 
                

            

        


        
        

            
📝 GATE Previous Year Questions on PRE

            
            

                
GATE 2022: Consider the following code:

                
if (cond)
    x = a + b;
else
    y = a + b;
z = a + b;

                
After applying PRE, how many computations of a+b remain?

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: 1 - The expression a+b is computed once before the conditional, and the result is used in all three places.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2020: In the context of PRE, what does "ANTLOC" stand for?

                
A) Anticipated expressions

                B) Available expressions

                C) Earliest placement

                D) Latest placement

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: A) Anticipated expressions - ANTLOC indicates expressions that will be evaluated on all paths from block exit.
                    

                

            


            

                
GATE 2019: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of Partial Redundancy Elimination?

                
A) Removes redundant computations across paths

                B) Can move loop invariant code out of loops

                C) Always reduces code size

                D) Reduces execution time

                

                    Show Answer
                    

                        Answer: C) Always reduces code size - PRE may sometimes increase code size by inserting temporary computations at join points.
                    

                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Practice Examples for GATE

        
        

            

                
Example 1: Simple PRE

                Expression available on some paths
                
a+b computed 3 times → reduce to 1 computation

            

            
            

                
Example 2: Loop PRE

                Expression redundant after first iteration
                
Move computation to loop header

            

            
            

                
Example 3: Nested PRE

                Complex control flow with partial redundancy
                
Find optimal placement point

            

        

    






    
 Bitwise Operations Optimization · Simplifying Shifts & Masks


    

        

            
            
        


        

            
            
            
            
            
        


        

             Shift Left
             Shift Right
             AND
             OR
             XOR
             GATE 2024
        

    


    
    

        
 Bitwise Optimization Dashboard

        
        

            

                
0

                
Total Operations

            

            

                
0

                
Operations Simplified

            

            

                
0

                
Operations Eliminated

            

            

                
0

                
CPU Cycles Saved

            

            

                
0

                
Masks Simplified

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Original Code

        

            
        

    


    
    

        
 After Bitwise Optimization

        
        

            

        

    


    
    

        
 Bitwise Optimization Rules Applied

        
        

            

                
                    

                        #
                        Rule Category
                        Original Pattern
                        Optimized Form
                        Description
                        Cycles Saved
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Apply optimization to see rules
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Bitwise Optimization Steps

        
        

            

                
                    

                        Step
                        Original Statement
                        Optimized Statement
                        Rule Applied
                        Savings
                    

                
                
                    

                        
                             Apply optimization to see steps
                        
                    

                
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Performance Comparison: Before vs After

        
        

            

                

                    

                

                
Before

                
0 cycles

            

            

                

                    

                

                
After

                
0 cycles

            

            

                

                    

                

                
Savings

                
0 cycles

            

        

        
        

            

                Shift/bitwise operation: ~2 cycles
            

        

    


    
    

        
 Bitwise Operations Cheat Sheet

        
        

            

                
Shift Optimizations

                
    
                    
  • x << 0 = x
    • 
                    
  • x >> 0 = x
    • 
                    
  • (x << a) << b = x << (a + b)
    • 
                    
  • (x << a) >> a = x (if no overflow)
    • 
                    
  • (x >> a) << a = x & ~((1 << a) - 1)
    • 
                

            

            
            

                
AND Optimizations

                
    
                    
  • x & 0 = 0
    • 
                    
  • x & -1 = x
    • 
                    
  • x & x = x
    • 
                    
  • x & ~x = 0
    • 
                    
  • (x & mask1) & mask2 = x & (mask1 & mask2)
    • 
                

            

            
            

                
OR Optimizations

                
    
                    
  • x | 0 = x
    • 
                    
  • x | -1 = -1
    • 
                    
  • x | x = x
    • 
                    
  • (x | mask1) | mask2 = x | (mask1 | mask2)
    • 
                

            

            
            

                
XOR Optimizations

                
    
                    
  • x ^ 0 = x
    • 
                    
  • x ^ -1 = ~x
    • 
                    
  • x ^ x = 0
    • 
                    
  • (x ^ y) ^ y = x
    • 
                

            

            
            

                
Mask Generation

                
    
                    
  • (1 << n) - 1 = n-bit mask
    • 
                    
  • ~((1 << n) - 1) = clear n low bits
    • 
                    
  • (x >> n) & 1 = nth bit
    • 
                    
  • ((x >> n) & mask) << n = bit field
    • 
                

            

        

    


    
    

        
 GATE Level: Bitwise Operation Optimization Concepts

        
        

            

                
📌 Shift Elimination

                
    
                    
  • Zero shift: x << 0 → x, x >> 0 → x
    • 
                    
  • Combine shifts: (x << a) << b = x << (a + b)
    • 
                    
  • Cancel shifts: (x << a) >> a = x (if a ≤ bits)
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Identity Operations

                
    
                    
  • AND: x & 0 = 0, x & -1 = x
    • 
                    
  • OR: x | 0 = x, x | -1 = -1
    • 
                    
  • XOR: x ^ 0 = x, x ^ -1 = ~x
    • 
                    
  • Self: x & x = x, x | x = x, x ^ x = 0
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Mask Simplification

                
    
                    
  • Create mask: (1 << n) - 1
    • 
                    
  • Clear bits: ~((1 << n) - 1)
    • 
                    
  • Bit extraction: (x >> n) & 1
    • 
                    
  • Bit field: ((x >> n) & m) << n
    • 
                

            


            

                
📌 Cycle Savings

                
    
                    
  • Shift: 1 cycle
    • 
                    
  • AND/OR/XOR: 1 cycle
    • 
                    
  • Redundant ops: 1-2 cycles saved
    • 
                    
  • Combining: N cycles saved for N operations
    • 
                

            

        

    



        

    


    

        
Compiler design interactive · Lexical Analysis module · for B.Tech + GATE · more tabs coming.
